gmw_00009802.name: Mrk 750
gmw_00009802.overview: Mrk 750 is an extremely faint blue compact dwarf galaxy classified as a Wolf-Rayet galaxy [arXiv:1004.2858]. It features a compact central star-forming region, asymmetric continuum structures, and neutral gas extending to twice its optical diameter [arXiv:1004.2858].

sources.summary: Mrk 750 is treated as an extremely faint BCD/Wolf-Rayet galaxy with compact central star formation, asymmetric continuum structure, dust, strong emission lines, and detected WR features.

quotes.quote: The galaxy Mrk 750 is an extremely faint ( M_ B =-12.82 ) BCD, classified as BCD/Im in . It belongs to the sample of low metallicity galaxies Izotov1999,Izotov2007 , and it is also a well-known Wolf-Rayet galaxy: first pointed out the broad He ii 4686 \ emission, and reported the detection of N iii 4640 ; these results are consistent with those found in ; also noticed C iv 5808 . Broad-band optical and \ observations were published by . From high spatial resolution H i synthesis observations, found that the neutral gas extends to approximately twice the optical diameter of the galaxy, peaking in the central region of star formation.

The galaxy has a similar morphology both in the emission line and in the continuum maps, with roughly circular isophotes and a single central peak, whose position is the same in all maps (emission lines and continuum). This peculiar morphology was interpreted as an interaction sign in . The integrated and nuclear spectra are both blue, OB young stars dominated spectra with very prominent emission lines; neither Balmer absorption wings nor absorption lines are visible.

gmw_00009803.name: Mrk 206
gmw_00009803.overview: Mrk 206 is a blue compact dwarf galaxy that exhibits a regular morphology in both its emission line and continuum maps [arXiv:1004.2858]. It features a central star-forming region, inhomogeneous dust distribution, and ionized gas rotating along a southeast-northwest axis [arXiv:1004.2858].

sources.summary: Mrk 206 is analyzed as a BCD with regular emission and continuum morphology, central star formation, inhomogeneous dust, high metallicity for the sample, and rotating ionized gas.

quotes.quote: Mrk 206 (=UGCA 280, SHOC 371) The galaxy Mrk 206 is classified as a BCD in . Intensities and equivalent widths of hydrogen and oxygen emission lines as well as oxygen abundances are published in . Our IFU data cover a region of 1.9 1.9 kpc with a spatial sampling of 120 pc per spaxel. Fig. Figure:mrk206 displays the two-dimensional maps of Mrk 206.

The galaxy shows a regular morphology both in emission line and continuum maps with a central SF region and roughly circular isophotes. All the excitation maps trace this central SF region, and in all of them the ratio values are consistent with ionization by young stars. The extinction pattern on the other hand is highly inhomogeneous, with a substantial amount of dust located on the northeast region of the galaxy, with / \ ratios up to 6 --- \ up to 0.7. The velocity field shows a clear overall rotation along a southeast-northwest axis and interestingly seems much like the extinction pattern seen in the / \ map.

gmw_00009804.name: Tololo 1434+032
gmw_00009804.overview: Tololo 1434+032 is a low-metallicity blue compact dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1004.2858]. It displays a clumpy morphology in both its continuum and emission lines, featuring two major continuum peaks and four star-forming knots with H II-region excitation [arXiv:1004.2858].

sources.summary: Tololo 1434+032 is presented as a low-metallicity BCD with clumpy continuum and emission morphology, several star-forming knots, normal H II-region excitation, and homogeneous extinction.

quotes.quote: Tololo 1434+032 (= SHOC 474) Tololo 1434+032 is a low-metallicity BCD Izotov2007,Kniazev2004 . B and R broad-band photometry was published in and , and \ imaging in . The PMAS FOV covers a region of 2.3 2.3 kpc, with a spatial sampling of 140 pc per spaxel. Two-dimensional maps of Tololo 1434+032 are shown in Fig. Figure:tololo1434 .

The galaxy shows a clumpy morphology both in emission lines and in the continuum. In the continuum maps we resolve two major emission peaks. All emission lines maps have the same structure: four SF knots are distributed in a roughly circular pattern, with the peak of emission located in knot A, whose position coincides with the continuum peak (knots are labeled in the \ map in Fig. Figure:tololo1434 ). The excitation maps trace the regions of star formation and display in the whole field of view values typical of H ii regions.

gmw_00009805.name: I Zw 123
gmw_00009805.overview: I Zw 123 is classified as a metal-poor blue compact dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1004.2858]. It features a compact continuum peak and a single star-forming region that is slightly offset to the north [arXiv:1004.2858].

sources.summary: I Zw 123 is characterized as a metal-poor BCD with compact continuum, an offset star-forming region, possible shock signatures in outer regions, modest extinction, and flat velocity fields.

quotes.quote: I Zw 123 (= UGCA 410, Mrk 487) The galaxy I Zw 123 is classified as a BCD in and belongs also to the metal-poor BCD class Izotov1997,Izotov1999 . I Zw 123 is a relatively well-studied object: optical surface photometry was published in several papers Cairos2001II,Cairos2001I,GildePaz2003,Caon2005,Amorin2007 , NIR photometry in . A thorough spectroscopic study, including an analysis of its stellar content in terms of population synthesis models, was published in the series of papers by , , , and . The PMAS FOV covers an area of 1.2 1.2 kpc, with a spatial sampling of 75 pc per spaxel.

In the continuum this galaxy is compact, with the intensity peak located in the center of the outer circular isophotes. All the emission-line maps have the same pattern, with a single SF region slightly displaced (2 arcsec or 150 pc) to the north of the continuum peak; the central isophotes are elongated in the south direction, resembling a small tail. The three excitation maps display the same structure, tracing the SF region; the values are typical of regions photoionized by stars, except in the [ S ii ] / \ map, where the outer regions have relatively high values (log [ S ii ] / 0.4 ); this could indicate that shocks are playing a significant role. In both the \ and [ O III ] velocity maps the velocity field appears flat (the higher velocities seen in the outermost spaxels in the [ O III ] map are most likely due to noise).

gmw_00009806.name: I Zw 159
gmw_00009806.overview: I Zw 159 is classified as a blue compact dwarf or irregular galaxy [arXiv:1004.2858]. It exhibits a boxy continuum morphology with a central star-forming knot, while its outer regions show evidence of shock excitation [arXiv:1004.2858].

sources.summary: I Zw 159 is described as a BCD/Irr with boxy continuum morphology, central star formation, outer-region shock indicators, irregular extinction, high metallicity, and clear rotation.

quotes.quote: I Zw 159 (= UGCA 412, Mrk 1499) In I Zw 159 is classified as a BCD/Irr. Optical surface brightness photometry was published in , , and . It is also included in the spectroscopy study of BCDs by , , , and , and is the only object in our sample in common with the sample of galaxies studied by means of IFS by . The PMAS FOV covers a region of 3.4 3.4 kpc, with a spatial resolution of 210 pc per spaxel.

In the continuum the galaxy shows a central intensity peak and a boxy morphology, with isophotes elongated around a northeast-southwest axis. In all the emission lines we see the same pattern: a central peak, whose position coincides with the continuum peak, surrounded by elongated isophotes. All the excitation maps trace the SF knot; whereas in the [ O iii ] 5007 / \ and [ N ii ] 6584 / \ maps the values are consistent with photoionization by young stars in the whole galaxy, in the [ S ii ] / \ and [ O i ] / \ maps the outer regions of the galaxy have higher values (log [ O i ] / 1.0 , log [ S ii ] / 0.4 ), indicating that an additional mechanism, probably shocks, is acting there. The velocity field in the \ and [ O iii ] maps shows a clear overall rotation along an axis roughly oriented south-north, with an amplitude of about 70--80 km s ^ 1 , in broad agreement with the velocity field published by .

gmw_00009807.name: J1550+1120
gmw_00009807.overview: J1550+1120 is a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy characterized by a host with large tidal arms and an exponential surface brightness profile [arXiv:1504.02772]. The galaxy also features radio jets with outer lobes that display multiple bright spots [arXiv:1504.02772].

sources.summary: J1550+1120 is presented as a Seyfert 1.5 with an asymmetric H profile, a host with tidal arms and exponential brightness profile, and radio jets with spotted lobes.

quotes.quote: J1550+1120 This is a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy, with a strongly asymmetric H profile because of a strongly redshifted broad component. The host galaxy appears to have large tidal arms and a surface brightness profile well represented with an exponential profile . It also has jets, whose outer lobes show multiple bright spots .

gmw_00009808.name: NGC 0932
gmw_00009808.overview: NGC 0932 is a galaxy that hosts active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1912.06451]. It exhibits mostly regular warm-gas kinematics, with a slight central irregularity and a fragmentary, ring-like hydrogen-alpha flux structure around its outer edge [arXiv:1912.06451].

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 0932 as an active case study, finding mostly regular warm-gas kinematics with slight central irregularity and an outer H flux ring-like structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 0932 (Current AGN activity) NGC 0932 is, on average, regular in all three parameters at all radii. In fact, it is the only galaxy in our sample whose maximum values for all three parameters fall within the regular range. A visual inspection of the gas velocity map reveals a slight irregularity in the center, which may be due to the presence of an AGN (as indicated by WHAN analysis). The map of the H flux reveals high values in the center of the galaxy as well as in a fragmentary ring-like structure around the galaxy's edge. This structure is located more than 1.5 _ e away from the center and would thus not normally be analyzed.

gmw_00009809.name: NGC 2672
gmw_00009809.overview: NGC 2672 is an early-type radio galaxy that exhibits diffuse, coreless radio emission extending over approximately 30 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2202.08593]. This large-scale emission features a ring-like structure and is characterized by a very steep low-frequency spectral slope [arXiv:2202.08593].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2672 as a diffuse, coreless extended LOFAR radio source, with large-scale emission and a steep low-frequency spectral slope.

quotes.quote: In two objects (NGC 0687 and NGC 2672) the radio emission, extending over 40 ( 15 kpc) and 105 ( 30 kpc), respectively, is diffuse and lacks of any well defined radio core. Seven of them show a very steep spectral slope ( _ 150 ^ 1400 > 1.2 , namely NGC 0080, NGC 0507, NGC 0687, NGC 0910, NGC 2672, NGC 2832, and NGC 3842), only three of them being detected (NGC 0507, NGC 2672, and NGC 3842) by the surveys at 1.4 GHz. All of them are characterized by rather weak cores, surrounded large-scale emission, often showing a diffuse structure.
NGC 2672 (Diffuse). Diffuse, ring-like structure with a diameter of 30 kpc.

gmw_00009810.name: NGC 2789
gmw_00009810.overview: NGC 2789 is a star-forming early-type galaxy that hosts a compact central radio source [arXiv:2202.08593]. This central component is associated with a pair of large-scale, low-brightness diffuse radio lobes that span over 700 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2202.08593].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 2789 as a star-forming candidate with a compact radio source, detached large low-brightness lobes, high radio-inferred SFR, and possible extended/restarted activity.

quotes.quote: The last SF candidate, NGC 2789, is a compact radio source ( 6 kpc) associated with a pair of large-scale ( 800 kpc), low-brightness diffuse lobes, detached from the central component (see Fig. low ). By excluding UGC 07115, whose radio emission is dominated by a large-scale structure powered by jets, we obtain a range between 0.1 and 0.5 M _ yr ^ 1 , with the exception of NGC 2789, for which this estimate is 8 M _ yr ^ 1 . These values compare favorably with those derived by brinchmann04 based on the analysis of their SDSS spectra, with an SFR in the 0.05 - 4 M _ yr ^ 1 range.

NGC 2789 (Ext. ?). The central component of this source is elongated in the NS direction, but the small angular size makes any classification uncertain. However, two low brightness lobes are visible in the low-resolution LOFAR image: the total size of the source exceeds 700 kpc.

gmw_00009811.name: UGC 10097
gmw_00009811.overview: UGC 10097 is a giant early-type radio galaxy that exhibits a central Fanaroff-Riley class I radio structure [arXiv:2202.08593]. Low-resolution LOFAR observations reveal two large-scale diffuse lobes extending to a radius of 500 kiloparsecs, suggesting the galaxy may be a restarted radio source [arXiv:2202.08593].

sources.summary: The paper treats UGC 10097 as a possible restarted giant early-type radio galaxy with a central FR I structure and large low-brightness diffuse lobes.

quotes.quote: For three gETGs (namely IC 5180, UGC 10097, and NGC 2789) the inspection of the low-resolution ( 25 25 , rms 250 Jy/beam) LOFAR images (see Fig. low ) reveals the presence of low-brightness lobes not (or barely) visible in the full resolution images. From the point of view of the radio morphology, among the 46 extended sources, there are eight galaxies (NGC 2789, NGC 3665, NGC 3842, UGC 10097, IC 5180, NGC7436B, UGC 12482, and MCG+05-10-007; see brienza21 for a detailed study of this source) in which a bright, small-scale structure is accompanied by large-scale diffuse emission. These gETGs can be considered as possible restarted sources with the structures observed on different scales (and of different surface brightnesses) being produced by subsequent phases of activity.
UGC 10097 (FRI). In addition to the central FR I structure, the low-resolution LOFAR images show two large-scale diffuse lobes, reaching a radius of 200 ( 500 kpc).

gmw_00009812.name: IC 5180
gmw_00009812.overview: IC 5180 is a giant early-type radio galaxy [arXiv:2202.08593]. Low-resolution LOFAR images reveal a central Fanaroff-Riley class I structure accompanied by two large, diffuse radio lobes [arXiv:2202.08593].

sources.summary: The paper includes IC 5180 among possible restarted giant early-type radio sources, with a central FR I structure and two large diffuse lobes visible in low-resolution LOFAR images.

quotes.quote: For three gETGs (namely IC 5180, UGC 10097, and NGC 2789) the inspection of the low-resolution ( 25 25 , rms 250 Jy/beam) LOFAR images (see Fig. low ) reveals the presence of low-brightness lobes not (or barely) visible in the full resolution images. From the point of view of the radio morphology, among the 46 extended sources, there are eight galaxies (NGC 2789, NGC 3665, NGC 3842, UGC 10097, IC 5180, NGC7436B, UGC 12482, and MCG+05-10-007; see brienza21 for a detailed study of this source) in which a bright, small-scale structure is accompanied by large-scale diffuse emission. These gETGs can be considered as possible restarted sources with the structures observed on different scales (and of different surface brightnesses) being produced by subsequent phases of activity.
IC 5180 (FRI). In addition to the central FR I structure, the low resolution LOFAR images show two large-scale diffuse lobes.

gmw_00009813.name: UGC 12482
gmw_00009813.overview: UGC 12482 is an early-type radio galaxy that exhibits a complex morphology [arXiv:2202.08593]. Its central region contains a 30 kpc triple structure, while long symmetric tails extending 90 kpc suggest a restarted source with remnants from a previous phase of activity [arXiv:2202.08593].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 12482 as a complex possible restarted radio source, with a central triple structure and long symmetric tails interpreted as older remnant activity.

quotes.quote: Several gETGs show a complex morphology, in at least two cases (namely, NGC 3842 and UGC 12482) presenting a central edge brightened structure and large diffuse tails, suggestive of restarted activity. From the point of view of the radio morphology, among the 46 extended sources, there are eight galaxies (NGC 2789, NGC 3665, NGC 3842, UGC 10097, IC 5180, NGC7436B, UGC 12482, and MCG+05-10-007; see brienza21 for a detailed study of this source) in which a bright, small-scale structure is accompanied by large-scale diffuse emission. These gETGs can be considered as possible restarted sources with the structures observed on different scales (and of different surface brightnesses) being produced by subsequent phases of activity.

UGC 12482 (Complex). The central region shows the presence of a triple source, extending for 30 kpc, while long symmetric tails reach a distance of 90 kpc, suggesting that this might be a restarted source and that the tails are remnants of a previous phase of activity.

gmw_00009814.name: J0035 0918, z_ abs =2.340097
gmw_00009814.overview: The damped Lyman-alpha system towards J0035 0918 is a high-redshift, metal-poor neutral gas reservoir located at a redshift of approximately 2.34 [arXiv:1406.7003, arXiv:2001.04983]. This chemically peculiar system exhibits an enhancement in carbon relative to iron and predominantly contains carbon-12 [arXiv:1406.7003, arXiv:2001.04983].

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes this very metal-poor high-redshift DLA, revising its carbon enhancement and deriving a single-component temperature, turbulence, H I column, and chemically peculiar abundance pattern.

quotes.quote: J0035 0918, z_ abs =2.340097 The DLA at redshift z_ abs 2.340 towards the quasar J0035 0918 is currently one of the most metal-poor systems known. It was initially reported by Coo11a as the first known example of a DLA with an abundance pattern resembling the nucleosynthesis expected by the first stars. In particular, this DLA was reported to have an abnormal excess of C and N relative to Fe, [ C/Fe ] =+1.53. This result was later contested by Car12 , who correctly pointed out that a contribution from thermal broadening would result in a lower overabundance of C/Fe.

By combining the Coo11a data with the recent observations described by Dut14 , we find that a single component at redshift z_ abs =2.340097 0.000001 provides an excellent fit to the data, with a turbulent Doppler parameter b_ turb =1.1 0.4 and a temperature T_ gas =7800 1600 . Using the new, high-resolution UVES spectrum we derive an \ column density from the \ transition N( H i )/ cm ^ 2 =20.43 0.04 . This estimate is lower by 0.1 dex from that reported by Coo11a , who used a medium resolution spectrum from the Magellan Echellette spectrograph to derive N( H i ) .

Overall, this DLA still exhibits an enhancement in C/Fe, greater than that exhibited by a typical very metal-poor DLA by 2.4 , however it no longer meets the generally adopted criterion defining Carbon Enhanced Metal Poor (CEMP) stars, [ C/Fe ] +0.7 Aok07 . Although this system is not quite as abundant in carbon as once thought, it is certainly a chemically peculiar system, and highlights the need to push these studies to even lower metallicities where such anomalous abundance patterns are expected to be more common CooMad14 .

sources.summary: The paper describes the DLA towards J0035 0918 as a near-pristine z~2.34 neutral-gas system with very low iron abundance, carbon enhancement, quiescent line structure, and mostly carbon-12.

quotes.quote: This system is located at a redshift z_ abs 2.34 along the line-of-sight to the quasar SDSS J003501.88 091817.6 (hereafter J0035 0918), and displays a large column density of neutral hydrogen, _ 10 N( H i )/ cm^ 2 =20.43 0.04 . Previous observations of this DLA towards J0035 0918 have shown that it is one of the least polluted reservoirs of neutral gas currently known, with a relative iron abundance almost 1/1000 that of the Sun.
The metal paucity of the DLA towards J0035 0918, alongside the originally reported overabundance of carbon, makes this an interesting environment to search for signatures of Population III stars.

We find that the gas cloud is well-modelled by a single absorption component whose broadening is entirely dominated by the thermal motions of the gas. On the basis of this model, we report a conservative 2 lower limit log_ 10 ^ 12 C/^ 13 C >+ 0.37 . We therefore conclude that this DLA predominantly contains ^ 12 C.

gmw_00009815.name: J1111 + 1332, z_ abs =2.270940
gmw_00009815.overview: J1111 + 1332, z_ abs =2.270940 is a high-redshift dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1406.7003]. It is classified as a very metal-poor damped Lyman-alpha system with a single absorption component at a redshift of 2.270940 [arXiv:1406.7003].

sources.summary: The paper presents this newly reported very metal-poor DLA, identifying it from SDSS, observing it with HIRES, and fitting its single absorption component, temperature, turbulence, and metal species.

quotes.quote: J1111 + 1332, z_ abs =2.270940 The second system reported here is the z_ abs 2.271 DLA towards the m_ 17.1 quasar J1111 + 1332. We first recognized this DLA as an excellent candidate VMP DLA from the relatively high S/N SDSS discovery spectrum, which exhibits strong \ \ absorption line together with the apparent absence of associated metal absorption lines. We performed follow-up observations of J1111 + 1332 with HIRES ( Vog94 ) on 2011 March 24 and 25 (Program ID: A152Hb).

Here, we report a detailed profile analysis which includes all of the available metal species. In total, we detect the absorption lines for ten elements and place an interesting upper limit on the Zn abundance. The absorption profiles are very well fit by a single absorption component centered at redshift z_ abs =2.270940 0.000001 with turbulent Doppler parameter b_ turb =2.2 0.1 km s ^ 1 and gas kinetic temperature T_ gas =13000 2000 K.

gmw_00009816.name: XSG2
gmw_00009816.overview: XSG2 is a massive early-type galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.06009 [arXiv:1610.03853]. It is classified as a satellite galaxy [arXiv:2104.01051] and exhibits a flatter light distribution with a Sersic index of 5.4 [arXiv:1610.03853].

sources.summary: The paper treats XSG2 as a massive early-type SPIDER galaxy with a flatter light distribution, Sersic structural fits, and evidence that its central region may have a bottom-heavy IMF.

quotes.quote: The targets of the present work are XSG1 (SDSS J142940.63+002159.0) and XSG2 (SDSS J002819.30-001446.7), two massive (M _ 1--2 10^ 11 M _ ; 300 ) ETGs at redshift z 0.05574 and z 0.06009, respectively.

XSG2 has a flatter light distribution, with b/a 0.67. Using the software 2DPHOT LBdC08 to fit the SDSS -band image of XSG2 with a two-dimensional, PSF-convolved, S ersic model (as for XSG1, see LB16), gives a best-fit S ersic n 5.4, and an effective radius of 5.4 ^ .

For what concerns XSG2 , our analysis also points to a bottom-heavy distribution in the central region. However, in this case, the data are also consistent with a lighter distribution, with 2.2, or even lower (see black star with error bar in Fig. fig:imf_rad_na ).

sources.summary: The paper uses XSG2 as one of seven nearby massive ETGs, identifies it as a satellite, and finds Mg i 1.18 index measurements reliable for IMF analysis.

quotes.quote: The sample consists of seven nearby massive ETGs ( _ 0 > 300 ) selected from SPIDER survey labarbera2010 and SDSS DR7 abazajian2009 . Most of these galaxies are brightest cluster galaxies, while two of them are satellites (XSG2 and XSG7). The high-quality spectra were obtained with the X-Shooter multi-wavelength medium resolution slit echelle spectrograph vernet2011 at the ESO Very Large Telescope (VLT). The NIR part of the spectra covers a wavelength range from 9800 to 25000 with a resolution power of 5500, which allows an accurate correction of sky lines. All galaxy spectra have SNR above 100 ^ 1 .

According to Table tab:tabB2 , for XSG2, XSG6, XSG7, and XSG10, the fraction of affected pixels for Mg i 1.18 does not exceed the maximum fraction in Table tab:tabB1 . This shows that, to perform an IMF analysis, Mg i 1.18 index measurements are reliable for these galaxies. On the other hand, this is not the case for XSG1, XSG8, and XSG9, and therefore they were not included in the upper panels of Fig. fig:fig9 . Ca i 1.95 index is reliable for analysing age and IMF slope for all galaxies in the sample but for XSG6, while it cannot be used to constrain metallicity for XSG6, XSG7, XSG9, and XSG10. Ca i 1.99a, Ca i 1.99b, and Ca i 1.99c indices fall within a clean region and none of the pixels are affected by telluric absorption or sky emission. However, for XSG8, we find that 11 of pixels in the blue bandpasses of these indices are contaminated, and thus one cannot rely on these indices to constrain metallicity of XSG8. In the case of Ca i 2.26, only for XSG6 one cannot constrain stellar population parameters, as the fraction of affected pixels within the red bandpass (5 ) is higher than the maximum fractions provided in Table tab:tabB1 (2 for age and metallicity and 3 for IMF).

gmw_00009817.name: host galaxy of the non-repeating FRB 180924B
aliases_local: host galaxy of the non-repeating FRB 180924B
gmw_00009817.overview: The host galaxy of the non-repeating FRB 180924B is a massive spiral galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z=0.32 [arXiv:2212.04027]. Observations of its carbon monoxide emission reveal two distinct velocity components and asymmetric profiles, indicating a disturbed molecular gas structure [arXiv:2212.04027].

sources.original_name: host galaxy of the non-repeating FRB 180924B
sources.summary: The paper reports ALMA CO kinematics for the massive spiral host of FRB 180924B, finding two velocity components, asymmetric CO profiles, and disturbed molecular-gas structure.

quotes.quote: Therefore, we, for the first time, report the molecular gas kinematics in the host galaxy of the non-repeating FRB 180924B at z= 0.3216 . Two velocity components of the CO (3-2) emission line are detected in its host galaxy with the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA): the peak of one component ( 155.40 km s ^ 1 ) is near the centre of the host galaxy, and another ( 7.76 km s ^ 1 ) is near the FRB position. The CO (3-2) spectrum shows asymmetric profiles with A _ peak =2.03 0.39 , where A _ peak is the peak flux density ratio between the two velocity components. The CO (3-2) velocity map also indicates an asymmetric velocity gradient from 180 km s ^ 1 to 8 km s ^ 1 . These results indicate a disturbed kinetic structure of molecular gas in the host galaxy.
The host is a massive spiral galaxy at z=0.3214 with a stellar mass of M_ =(1.32 5.1) 10^ 9 M_ and the star formation rate (SFR) of 0.88 0.26 M_ yr ^ 1 Bannister2019, Heintz2020 .
We utilize an aperture with a radius of 1.032 arcsec (5 kpc) centred at the host galaxy (RA, Dec) = (21h44m25.25s, 40^ 54 00.81 ) Bhandari2020 to extract a CO spectrum of the host galaxy.

gmw_00009818.name: Mrk 663
gmw_00009818.overview: Mrk 663 is classified as a candidate Seyfert 2 active galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0106311]. It is characterized by a weak optical continuum, absent Balmer emission, and the hardest soft-X-ray spectrum among its sample [arXiv:astro-ph/0106311].

sources.summary: The paper places Mrk 663 among weak-continuum anomalous Seyferts, noting its absent Balmer emission, hardest soft-X-ray spectrum in the sample, and status as the main persistent Seyfert 2 candidate.

quotes.quote: At the other end of the parameter space there is a group of six objects which stand out, these are: MCG+08-23-067, IC 3599, Was 61, RX J1258+23, Mrk 663 and NGC 5905. These all tend to possess stronger forbidden than permitted line emission ( / > 1) and weak (and therefore redder, due to host galaxy emission) optical continua when compared to soft X-rays ( _ OX >1.5 ). However, with the exception of Mrk 663, which shows no detectable \ emission, none of these objects have / > 3, a commonly used criterion for classifying Seyfert 2s. Only two of these objects stand out from the _ X distribution; NGC 5905 had a remarkably steep soft X-ray spectrum at the time of the \ survey, while Mrk 663 shows the hardest spectrum in the 1/4 keV sample. Mrk 663 is probably therefore the only candidate Seyfert 2 in the present sample besides the transient IC 3599.

gmw_00009819.name: faint z=2.466 galaxy
gmw_00009819.overview: The faint z=2.466 galaxy is a high-redshift galaxy located at an impact parameter of 50 kpc from a bright background QSO [arXiv:1406.4239]. A partial Lyman-limit absorption system at the galaxy's redshift suggests the presence of metal-enriched gas produced by an outflowing wind [arXiv:1406.4239].

sources.summary: The paper reports a faint 0.2 L* galaxy at z=2.466, 50 kpc from a background QSO, with a metal-enriched partial Lyman-limit absorber likely produced by an outflowing wind.

quotes.quote: Received xxxx; in original form xxxx firstpage - lastpage xxxx We report the serendipitous detection of a 0.2 L ^* , \ emitting galaxy at redshift 2.5 at an impact parameter of 50 kpc from a bright background QSO sightline. A high-resolution spectrum of the QSO reveals a partial Lyman-limit absorption system ( =10^ 16.94 0.10 ) with many associated metal absorption lines at the same redshift as the foreground galaxy. Using photoionization models that carefully treat measurement errors and marginalise over uncertainties in the shape and normalisation of the ionizing radiation spectrum, we derive the total hydrogen column density =10^ 19.4 0.3 , and show that all the absorbing clouds are metal enriched, with Z=0.1 - 0.6 Z_ . These metallicities and the system's large velocity width ( 436 ) suggest the gas is produced by an outflowing wind. Using an expanding shell model we estimate a mass outflow rate of 5 .

We have serendipitously discovered a faint, 0.2 L^* , galaxy at z=2.466 that is at a small impact parameter ( 50 kpc) from a background QSO. Based on similarities to galaxies selected as \ emitters, it likely has a halo mass 10^ 11.4 and stellar mass < 10^ 10 . A very high quality UVES spectrum of the QSO reveals a partial Lyman limit system at the galaxy redshift.

gmw_00009820.name: Andromeda NE
gmw_00009820.overview: Andromeda NE is a diffuse, low-surface-brightness structure located in the outer regions of M31 [arXiv:1311.1951]. Its exact nature remains unclear, as it may represent a very low-mass galaxy, a portion of a stellar tidal stream, or turn-off material from the M31 disk [arXiv:1311.1951].

sources.summary: The paper treats Andromeda NE as a diffuse low-surface-brightness structure in M31 outer regions, possibly a low-mass galaxy or tidal-stream material, and confirms PNe useful for kinematic and chemical studies.

quotes.quote: We identified three PN candidates at the location of Andromeda NE Zucker04a . The nature of this diffuse low-surface brightness structure in the outer regions of \ is still unclear. It may be a very low-mass and low surface-brightness galaxy, a portion of an extended stellar tidal stream, or possibly just turn-off material from the disk of . Andromeda NE has an absolute luminosity in the g band of 11.6 mag and a central surface brightness of only 29 mag arcsec ^ 2 Zucker04a . According to RB86 the number of stars n _P in any post-main-sequence phase P can be calculated with the equation n_P = L_T t_P , where is the number of stars per unit luminosity that leave the main sequence per year (between 5 10^ 12 to 2 10^ 11 yr ^ 1 L_ ^ 1 ), L_T is the total luminosity of the galaxy (5 10^6 L_ for Andromeda NE), and t_P is the duration of the evolutionary phase ( 20,000 years for PNe).

Applying this formula shows that the number of PNe that might be expected in Andromeda NE is 0.5--2. These numbers are in good agreement with the number of PN candidates that we found in this area. Both our 2.2m spectroscopy in this paper and later 3.5m (Calar Alto, Spain) and 6m Russian telescope spectroscopy (Kniazev et al., in preparation) confirm that all these candidates are real PNe, which could be used for kinematical and chemical studies of Andromeda NE.

gmw_00009821.name: G09-0902+0101
gmw_00009821.overview: G09-0902+0101 is a submillimeter galaxy that hosts a central quasar [arXiv:2308.15955]. The system exhibits broad Lyman-alpha emission with a positive velocity shift, which may indicate the presence of bulk winds [arXiv:2308.15955].

sources.summary: The paper describes G09-0902+0101 as a quasar-hosting SMG with estimated gas, stellar, and halo masses, broad Ly emission, and a positive velocity shift suggesting bulk winds.

quotes.quote: We find M_ gas =8 10^ 10 for BR1202-0725, and M_ gas =6.3 10^ 10 and M_ gas =1.9 10^ 10 for G09-0902+0101 and G15-1444-0044, respectively. We find stellar masses of M_ * =6.7^ +5.9 _ 2.1 10^ 10 for BR1202-0725, and M_ * =8.0^ +10.9 _ 2.8 10^ 10 and M_ * =2.3^ +2.7 _ 0.8 10^ 10 for G09-0902+0101 and G15-1444-0044, respectively. Therefore, the expected DM halo masses using the halo mass-stellar mass relation by Moster2018 for the targeted sources are: log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 12.3 for BR1202-0725, log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 12.5 and log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 11.9 for G09-0902+0101 and G15-1444-0044, and log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 11.9 and log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 12.0 for ALESS61.1, and ALESS65.1, respectively.

We find that all the spectra have consistent broad ( FWHM 1500 km s^ 1 ) line profiles, with the presence of absorption features. Such broad lines are not frequently seen around quasars ( FWHM 600 km s^ 1 ; e.g., Borisova2016,FAB2019 ), possibly indicating more turbulent gas reservoirs in the highly star-forming environments targeted here. Interestingly, BR1202-0725 and G09-0902+0101 show integrated lines with positive shifts of the order of 300-400 km s^ 1 with respect to the systemic redshift of each system, possibly indicating bulk winds.

gmw_00009822.name: G15-1444-0044
gmw_00009822.overview: G15-1444-0044 is a submillimeter galaxy that hosts a central quasar [arXiv:2308.15955]. The system exhibits a broad Lyman-alpha emission line profile, which suggests a turbulent gas reservoir within its highly star-forming environment [arXiv:2308.15955].

sources.summary: The paper describes G15-1444-0044 as a quasar-hosting SMG with estimated gas, stellar, and halo masses, broad Ly emission, and a similar shifted Ly peak.

quotes.quote: We find M_ gas =8 10^ 10 for BR1202-0725, and M_ gas =6.3 10^ 10 and M_ gas =1.9 10^ 10 for G09-0902+0101 and G15-1444-0044, respectively. We find stellar masses of M_ * =6.7^ +5.9 _ 2.1 10^ 10 for BR1202-0725, and M_ * =8.0^ +10.9 _ 2.8 10^ 10 and M_ * =2.3^ +2.7 _ 0.8 10^ 10 for G09-0902+0101 and G15-1444-0044, respectively. Therefore, the expected DM halo masses using the halo mass-stellar mass relation by Moster2018 for the targeted sources are: log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 12.3 for BR1202-0725, log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 12.5 and log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 11.9 for G09-0902+0101 and G15-1444-0044, and log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 11.9 and log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 12.0 for ALESS61.1, and ALESS65.1, respectively.

We find that all the spectra have consistent broad ( FWHM 1500 km s^ 1 ) line profiles, with the presence of absorption features. Such broad lines are not frequently seen around quasars ( FWHM 600 km s^ 1 ; e.g., Borisova2016,FAB2019 ), possibly indicating more turbulent gas reservoirs in the highly star-forming environments targeted here. We note that G15-1444-0044 also show a similar shift when using the maximum of its line as reference velocity (see Table table:SFRv2 ).

gmw_00009823.name: ALESS61.1
gmw_00009823.overview: ALESS61.1 is an isolated submillimeter galaxy that does not host an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2308.15955]. Observations of the galaxy show no detected extended emission around the system [arXiv:2308.15955].

sources.summary: The paper treats ALESS61.1 as an isolated high-redshift SMG with submillimeter SED data, estimated stellar and halo masses, and no detected extended emission.

quotes.quote: They are about one order of magnitude fainter than the quasar in BR1202-0725; Two isolated SMGs (with no AGN) with spectroscopic redshift from [ CII ] and CO, ALESS61.1 and ALESS65.1 Swinbank2012,Birkin2021 . ^ a Redshifts from Carilli2013 , Fu2017 and Birkin2021 . We construct the 8-1000 m spectral energy distribution using the datapoints from Omont1996a (at 1250 m with IRAM 30 m) and Iono2006 (at 900 m with the Submillimeter Array) for BR1202-0725, from Fu2017 (at 250, 350 and 500 m with Herschel/SPIRE and 870 m with ALMA) for G09-0902+0101 and G15-1444-0044, and from Swinbank2012 (at 250, 350 and 500 m with Herschel/SPIRE and 870 m with ALMA) for ALESS61.1 and ALESS65.1. Specifically, the dust masses of ALESS61.1 and ALESS65.1 are in agreement with the values estimated by Birkin2021 .

For the two SMGs, ALESS61.1 and ALESS65.1, we can rely on their stellar masses obtained through SED fitting by Birkin2021 , log( M_ * / [ M_ ] )=10.33^ +0.18 _ 0.01 and log( M_ * / [ M_ ] )=10.48^ +0.19 _ 0.13 , respectively. Therefore, the expected DM halo masses using the halo mass-stellar mass relation by Moster2018 for the targeted sources are: log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 12.3 for BR1202-0725, log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 12.5 and log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 11.9 for G09-0902+0101 and G15-1444-0044, and log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 11.9 and log( M_ DM / [ M_ ] ) 12.0 for ALESS61.1, and ALESS65.1, respectively.

Specifically, our observations comprise systems with different degrees of quasar illumination: a QSO+SMG system (BR1202-0725), two SMGs hosting a QSO (G09-0902+0101 and G15-1444-0044), and two SMGs (ALESS61.1 and ALESS65.1). We do not detect any extended emission around the two SMGs.

gmw_00009824.name: SRGE J0353+5654
gmw_00009824.overview: SRGE J0353+5654 is a high-frequency synchrotron peak blazar with an unknown redshift and a featureless optical spectrum [arXiv:2309.11266]. The source exhibits high absorption and displays abrupt flux changes in its X-ray light curve [arXiv:2309.11266].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SRGE J0353+5654 with the HSP blazar GB6 J0353+5654, noting high absorption, unknown redshift, abrupt X-ray flux changes, and spectral-model behavior.

quotes.quote: SRGE J035309.6+565431 The source SRGE J0353+5654 (no. 39) possesses the highest absorption among the objects of our sample based on the power-law model fit (see Table tab:params ). Its X-ray position is compatible with the known blazar GB6 J0353+5654, which belongs to the class of blazars with a high-frequency synchrotron peak (HSP). Its optical spectrum is featureless, the redshift is unknown crespo_2016 . No optical transients were detected in the position error circle either. The X-ray light curve of SRGE J0353+5654 (see Fig. fig:xraylc ) exhibits abrupt changes in the flux in all four surveys.

The simple thermal models with absorption describe the spectrum slightly more poorly than does the absorbed power-law spectrum, stat = +11.6 ( bbodyrad ) and stat = +5.6 ( diskbb ), with the absorption parameter for these models being consistent with the Galactic value toward the source.

gmw_00009825.name: NGC 426
gmw_00009825.overview: NGC 426 is an early-type elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1403.4627]. Its central region displays strong emission lines characteristic of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1403.4627].

sources.summary: The paper reports AGN-like central emission affecting NGC 426 dispersion measurements and derives corrected central-fiber dispersions from broader or masked spectral fitting.

quotes.quote: Finally, we note that a small number of galaxies, most notably NGC 426 and NGC 7509, have central fibers dominated by strong emission lines characteristic of AGN, which can affect measurements of dispersion.

In particular, NGC 426 and NGC 7509 show G-Band offsets of between +30 and +40 . Figure Fig:CentralFibres shows the central fibers of these two galaxies, which both display strong emission features characteristic of AGN. Our stellar templates cannot fit these features, but their presence in the MgIb and CaH+K spectra has the effect of artificially driving down the dispersion in these regions and therefore creating a discrepancy with the relatively smooth G-Band. For instance, the central fibers in NGC 7509 and NGC 426 yield MgIb dispersions of 149.8 1.5 and 207.5 5.9 respectively. However, if we fit the same region with the strongest lines between 4900 and 5120 masked out, these values become 186.7 1.2 and 230.5 3.0 , a shift of 30 in each case.
The central fibers of NGC 426 and NGC 7509, for example, yield dispersions of 190.0 1.1 and 231.1 1.4 , broadly consistent with the masked MgIb region.

gmw_00009826.name: NGC 7509
gmw_00009826.overview: NGC 7509 is an early-type elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1403.4627]. Its central region displays strong emission lines characteristic of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1403.4627].

sources.summary: The paper finds AGN-like central emission in NGC 7509 that skews small-window dispersion fits, then recovers broader-region central dispersions consistent with masked MgIb fitting.

quotes.quote: Finally, we note that a small number of galaxies, most notably NGC 426 and NGC 7509, have central fibers dominated by strong emission lines characteristic of AGN, which can affect measurements of dispersion.

In particular, NGC 426 and NGC 7509 show G-Band offsets of between +30 and +40 . Figure Fig:CentralFibres shows the central fibers of these two galaxies, which both display strong emission features characteristic of AGN. Our stellar templates cannot fit these features, but their presence in the MgIb and CaH+K spectra has the effect of artificially driving down the dispersion in these regions and therefore creating a discrepancy with the relatively smooth G-Band. For instance, the central fibers in NGC 7509 and NGC 426 yield MgIb dispersions of 149.8 1.5 and 207.5 5.9 respectively. However, if we fit the same region with the strongest lines between 4900 and 5120 masked out, these values become 186.7 1.2 and 230.5 3.0 , a shift of 30 in each case.
The central fibers of NGC 426 and NGC 7509, for example, yield dispersions of 190.0 1.1 and 231.1 1.4 , broadly consistent with the masked MgIb region.

gmw_00009827.name: UGC 4051
gmw_00009827.overview: UGC 4051 is a massive elliptical galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.0218 [arXiv:1407.5791, arXiv:1403.4627]. It is classified as a fast rotator with a sharply declining velocity dispersion profile and potentially hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1407.5791, arXiv:1403.4627].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC4051 as the SW group elliptical at the X-ray peak, associated by redshift with the merger and possibly hosting a hard X-ray AGN.

quotes.quote: Another large elliptical galaxy (UGC4051) is coincident with the X-ray surface brightness peak in the SW group. The redshifts for these three large ellipticals from Crawford99 show that these galaxies are associated with the two merging groups and not a chance superposition along the line of sight. There is a hard X-ray point source spatially coincident with UGC4051 in the SW group, which could indicate an AGN. The VLA FIRST survey does not show a radio point source coincident with this source ( Becker95 ).

Although this is not a significant difference, these results are consistent with the galaxy redshifts z=0.0218 for UGC4051 in the SW group and z=0.0236 and z=0.0228 for UGC4052 E and W, respectively ( Crawford99 ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 4051 among the most massive galaxies with a small dispersion rise, one of the sharpest declining fast rotators, and a comparison case for kinematics and morphology.

quotes.quote: However, the two most massive galaxies in our sample, NGC 677 and UGC 4051, do show significant, albeit small ( 5 above _ R_e ) rises in dispersion.
This subset includes the galaxies with the sharpest declines in : NGC 774 at low mass and UGC 4051 at high mass, both of which have 0.1 - 0.2 .
As a final comparison between the two samples, we may consider the kinematics and morphology of our fastest declining FR, UGC 4051.

gmw_00009828.name: NGC 1052-DF9
gmw_00009828.overview: NGC 1052-DF9 is a dwarf galaxy located within the NGC 1052 group [arXiv:2306.08540]. Its stellar age and total metallicity are similar to those of NGC 1052-DF2 and NGC 1052-DF4, although its recessional velocity of 1680 km/s challenges a linear projection of the proposed "bullet dwarf" collision trail [arXiv:2306.08540].

sources.summary: The paper measures DF9 with Keck/KCWI, finding a dwarf galaxy in the NGC 1052 group whose stellar populations resemble DF2/DF4 but whose velocity challenges the bullet-trail projection.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage In this study, we use Keck/KCWI spectroscopy to measure the age, metallicity and recessional velocity of NGC 1052-DF9 (DF9), a dwarf galaxy in the NGC 1052 group. We compare these properties to those of two other galaxies in the group, NGC 1052-DF2 and NGC 1052-DF4, which have low dark matter content. The three galaxies are proposed constituents of a trail of galaxies recently hypothesised to have formed as part of a ``bullet dwarf'' collision. We show that the ages and total metallicities of the three galaxies are within uncertainties of one another which may be expected if they share a related formation pathway.

However, the recessional velocity we recover for DF9 (1680 10 km s ^ 1 ) is higher than predicted for a linearly projected interpretation of the ``bullet dwarf'' trail. DF9 is then either not part of the trail or the correlation of galaxy velocities along the trail is not linear in 2D projection due to their 3D geometry.

gmw_00009829.name: XID 149S
gmw_00009829.overview: XID 149S is a broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of approximately 2.81 [arXiv:1504.03329, arXiv:0808.2414]. Its spectrum reveals a high-velocity outflow forming a P-Cygni type profile [arXiv:1504.03329], with a wind kinetic luminosity estimated up to 0.3 of its bolometric luminosity [arXiv:0808.2414].

sources.summary: XID 149S is described as a z=2.810 BAL quasar whose spectrum suggests a high-velocity outflow and whose MIR-brightness makes the source appear X-ray weak.

quotes.quote: XID 149S: This z=2.810 quasar has a column density 2 10^ 23  cm ^ 2 and a photon index 1.7 with all the models used to fit the spectrum (i.e., model 1, PLCABS and Torus); none of the models required the addition of a soft scattered power law. Although the X-ray spectrum does not show signs of any emission line, clear residuals around E  1.5--2 keV ( E  5.7--7.6 keV, rest frame) indicate the presence of a strong absorption feature forming a P-Cygni type profile, which indicates the presence of a high-velocity outflow (e.g., pounds2009, tombesi2010 ). Indeed, this source has been identified in the optical band as a BAL quasar ( szokoly2004 ). A BAL quasar interpretation would help explaining why the intrinsic X-ray luminosity of this quasar, L_ 2-10 keV, intr 2 10^ 44   , which is corrected for the X-ray absorption derived from the spectral fitting, is lower than that expected from the MIR luminosity L_ 6 m 8 10^ 45   (see Fig.

fig.l6lx ), as BAL quasars have been found to be intrinsically X-ray weak (e.g., luo2014 ). The X-ray weakness of BAL quasars is typically estimated with respect to the optical band ( _ OX ; e.g., luo2014 ). However, since BAL quasars have SEDs consistent with those of typical quasars in the optical-to-IR bands (e.g., gallagher2007 ), we expect to see X-ray weakness relative to the MIR emission too.

sources.summary: XBQ5 has VLT-FORS optical spectroscopy and a redshift consistent with prior CDFS work; its absorber modeling is stable against an X-ray-rich SED, and its wind energetics are comparatively well constrained.

quotes.quote: We obtained an optical spectrum of XBQ5 from the szokoly2004 catalogue of CDFS optical spectra http://www.mpe.mpg.de/CDFS/data/62.html ; those data were obtained using VLT-FORS appenzeller1998 , with an exposure time of 10800 s, a spectral coverage of 3600 9000 and a spectral resolution of 20 at 5600 . All spectra were normalised according to their measured B magnitudes (Table source_properties ). We measured the systemic redshifts of XBQ1 5 from their optical spectra, which are shown plotted in the restframe in Fig. all_restframe , and are listed in Table source_properties ; our redshift for XBQ5 is close to that quoted by szokoly2004 ; the values are 2.82 and 2.81 respectively.

To see whether the use of an X-ray richer SED would affect our results concerning the absorber properties, we generated a new SED for XBQ5 (for which we can obtain the best absorber constraints), multiplying the relative X-ray normalisation by the N_ ratio value for this source in Table xray_fit . We used this SED to generate a SPEX continuum model and an XSTAR ionised absorber model, and re-fitted the spectrum. We obtained almost identical values for N_ H and (well within 1 of the original results), and so our assumption of a normal SED for the X-ray fitting should make no difference to our conclusions about the XBALQSO absorber properties.

For XBQ5, which has the best-constrained values, mass outflow rate via the X-ray absorbing part of the wind is 0.04 10 times the mass accretion rate onto its black hole, and the kinetic luminosity of the wind is 0.002 0.3 of the bolometric luminosity.

gmw_00009830.name: XID 171N
gmw_00009830.overview: XID 171N is a heavily absorbed infrared quasar located at a redshift of z=1.995 [arXiv:1504.03329]. Its X-ray spectrum exhibits a reflection component and an iron K emission line, which support its classification as a Compton-thick active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1504.03329].

sources.summary: XID 171N is described as a z=1.995 heavily absorbed IR quasar whose reflection component, Fe K emission, and Torus fit support a Compton-thick AGN interpretation.

quotes.quote: Here we describe in more detail the most interesting sources amongst our X-ray detected IR quasar sample: XID 171N: From the spectral fits using model 2 and PLCABS this z=1.995 source shows a very high level of absorption 8 10^ 23  cm ^ 2 ( =1.8 ), although not at CT levels. However, the residuals in the hard band ( E 3  keV, observed frame) suggest the presence of a reflection component and an emission line at E 2  keV (observed frame), typical of CT AGN spectra. Indeed, the spectral fit using the Torus model yields a column density lower limit which is well within the CT regime. georgantopoulos2009 have also classified this source as a CT AGN. The strong emission line is consistent with the redshifted iron K line ( E=6.4  keV) with an equivalent width of EW=1.2^ +1.6 _ 1.1 \ keV ; given the limited counting statistics of the spectrum the uncertainties on the line EW are large, but still consistent with CT absorption.

gmw_00009831.name: XID 287N
gmw_00009831.overview: XID 287N is a quasar located at a redshift of z=2.737 [arXiv:1504.03329]. It exhibits an apparently unobscured spectrum with low counts, but its weak X-ray luminosity suggests possible Compton-thick absorption or intrinsic X-ray weakness [arXiv:1504.03329].

sources.summary: XID 287N is presented as a z=2.737 quasar with an apparently unobscured low-count spectrum but unusually weak X-ray luminosity, consistent with possible Compton-thick absorption.

quotes.quote: XID 287N: Similar to XID 243N, this z=2.737 quasar has an apparently unobscured spectrum (with low numbers of counts) and an X-ray luminosity that is significantly smaller compared to the intrinsic AGN luminosity measured in the MIR band. Assuming a standard value for L_ X /L_ 6 m (e.g., lutz2004,fiore2009,mateos2015 ) we estimate that the observed X-ray luminosity is consistent with being suppressed by a factor of 35 (see Table tab4 ). This suppression is consistent with the effect of CT absorption, but it is less extreme than that observed for XID 243N. Moreover, no other diagnostics have been found to confirm the intrinsic AGN luminosity measured in the MIR band, therefore the possibility of intrinsic X-ray weakness cannot be ruled out.

gmw_00009832.name: XID 307N
gmw_00009832.overview: XID 307N is a high-redshift quasar located at a photometric redshift of approximately 2.14 [arXiv:1504.03329]. Its X-ray spectrum exhibits a strong iron emission line and a high column density, indicating that it is a Compton-thick active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1504.03329].

sources.summary: XID 307N shows a strong Fe K line and hard-band residuals; spectral modeling gives Compton-thick column-density limits and implies high intrinsic X-ray luminosity.

quotes.quote: XID 307N: In the lightly binned spectrum (1 count/bin) of this quasar, clear residuals around the observed 2 keV indicate the presence of a strong iron emission line, consistent with being Fe K at the source redshift of z=2.14 . We therefore added a Gaussian component to the fit, with E=6.4  keV and line width fixed at =50  eV, to measure the strength of the line. Since the redshift of this source is photometric, we initially left the redshift parameter in the model free to vary. From the X-ray spectral fit we obtained constraints on the redshift of z=2.18^ +0.08 _ 0.08 , in good agreement with the photometric estimate reported in Table tab1 . Fixing z=2.18 and fitting the spectrum, however, produces a very flat photon index of =0.70_ 0.62 ^ +0.95 , and no constraints are found on the \ parameter.

Fixing =1.8 yields a column density of =(4.8_ 3.0 ^ +8.2 ) 10^ 23  cm ^ 2 with some residuals in the hard band ( E 2  keV), suggesting a possible contribution from a reflection component in the spectrum. This hypothesis is supported by the high EW measured for the iron line, EW=1.2^ +1.0 _ 0.8  keV (rest frame), which is typical of CT AGN. Indeed, fitting the spectrum with the Torus model results in a column density lower limit of >6 10^ 24  cm ^ 2 (see Table tab3 ), i.e., in the CT regime.

gmw_00009833.name: IGR J01017+6519
gmw_00009833.overview: IGR J01017+6519 is an active galactic nucleus at a redshift of approximately 0.085 [arXiv:1809.02949]. Optical spectroscopy indicates that the source is most likely a Seyfert 1 galaxy, displaying a broad hydrogen emission line, a narrow nitrogen line, and typical radio activity [arXiv:1809.02949].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IGR J01017+6519 as a likely Seyfert 1 galaxy: optical spectroscopy finds broad redshifted H emission, narrow N II, and radio activity typical of AGN.

quotes.quote: To clarify the nature of the source, we performed optical spectroscopic observations with the AZT-33IK telescope. The exposure time was 50 min. Despite the low flux ( 23 from the PanSTARRS data), we were able to obtain a significant spectrum of the source. A redshifted broad H_ emission line is clearly seen at a wavelength of 7123.2 , suggesting that the object being investigated is most likely the active nucleus of a Seyfert 1 galaxy at redshift z = 0.085 0.005 ( D_L 399 Mpc ) (Fig. 1). Apart from the hydrogen line, a narrow N II line is seen in the spectrum, which is also typical for objects of this class. The fact that, according to the NVSS (21 cm) data, the source exhibits a radio activity, which is quite typical for active galactic nuclei (AGNs), can serve as an additional confirmation for an extragalactic nature of the object.

gmw_00009834.name: IGR J08321-1808
gmw_00009834.overview: IGR J08321-1808 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a distance of approximately 655 Mpc [arXiv:1809.02949]. Its optical spectrum exhibits narrow oxygen emission lines and a bright, broad hydrogen-alpha emission line at a redshift of 0.135 [arXiv:1809.02949].

sources.summary: The paper localizes IGR J08321-1808, identifies a single optical counterpart, and classifies it as a Seyfert 1 galaxy from narrow oxygen lines and broad redshifted H emission.

quotes.quote: t  IGR J08321-1808 Using the archival IBIS/INTEGRAL and XRT/Swift observations (ObsID: 00046937001) of this object, we improved its localization and obtained its spectrum in a wide X-ray energy range (Table 2, Fig. 3). Note that the soft X-ray counterpart of the hard X-ray source was observed previously and is present in the catalogs of sources of the ROSAT sky survey (Voges et al. 1999) and the XRT/SWift 1SXPS catalog of soft X-ray sources (Evans et al. 2014). The high XRT positional accuracy of IGR J08321-1808 allowed the source to be unequivocally identified in the optical band using PanSTARRS sky maps. It can be seen from Fig. 3 that only one fairly bright object with 16.76 (PanSTARRS) falls into the error circle. Further observations of this object with RTT-150 allowed its spectrum to be obtained, where narrow [ O III ] 4959, 5007 lines and a bright broad emission line that can be identified with the H_ line at z = 0.135 0.001 (D_L 655 Mpc) are clearly distinguishable. All these features are typical for Seyfert 1 galaxies.

gmw_00009835.name: IGR J11299-6557
gmw_00009835.overview: IGR J11299-6557 is associated with a bright mid-infrared counterpart, WISE J112956.44-655521.8 [arXiv:1809.02949]. Based on its dereddened infrared colors and its power-law X-ray spectrum, the source is provisionally classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1809.02949].

sources.summary: IGR J11299-6557 is linked to a bright WISE infrared counterpart with AGN-like dereddened colors and a power-law X-ray spectrum, yielding a provisional AGN classification.

quotes.quote: t  IGR J11299-6557 To improve the localization of the object, we requested the XRT/Swift observations (ObsID 00010544001, February 2018), based on which we were able to determine the soft X-ray counterpart of the source and to measure its X-ray flux (Table 1). The combined broadband spectrum of the object from the XRT/Swift and IBIS/INTEGRAL data is well described by a simple power law with absorption (Table 2, Fig. 4). Owing to the high XRT localization accuracy of the source, we were able to unequivocally identify the infrared counterpart of the object WISE J112956.44-655521.8 using WISE data (see Fig. 4 and Table 2), which is a very bright object in the mid-infrared with magnitudes W2 = 9.969 0.020 and W4 = 4.475 0.021 .

To classify the object, we used the selection criterion from Stern et al. (2012) (W1-W2 > 0.8 and W2 < 15.05 ) by first correcting the (W1-W2) color of the counterpart for reddening. The reddening correction E(W1 - W2)=0.03 for the sky region toward IGR J11299-6557 was taken from the maps in Schlafly and Finkbeiner (2011). Thus, having obtained the dereddened color of the counterpart (W1-W2)_0=1.15 , we can provisionally attribute the source to the class of AGNs. It is worth noting that the same conclusion about the nature of WISE J112956.44-655521.8 was drawn by Kouzuma and Yamaoka (2010), Edelson and Malkan (2012), and Secrest et al.

gmw_00009836.name: IGR J14417-5533
gmw_00009836.overview: IGR J14417-5533 is a galaxy with a mid-infrared counterpart identified as WISE J144118.74-553335.1 [arXiv:1809.02949]. Based on its dereddened infrared colors, the object is classified as probably hosting an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1809.02949].

sources.summary: IGR J14417-5533 is localized with Swift, matched to a WISE mid-infrared counterpart, and classified as probably an AGN from its dereddened infrared colors.

quotes.quote: t  IGR J14417-5533 Owing to the XRT/Swift observations (ObsID 00010546001, February 2018), we were able to improve the localization of this source, which, in turn, allowed the counterpart of the object in the mid-infrared to be determined (WISE J144118.74-553335.1, Fig. 5). The XRT/Swift observations also allowed us to measure the flux from the source (Table 1) and, in combination with the IBIS/INTEGRAL data, to construct its broadband spectrum. The constructed spectrum is best described by a power law with absorption (Table 2, Fig. 5).

The object was classified based on the infrared colors for this source using the same algorithm as that for IGR J11299-6557. Having derived the dereddened color (W1-W2)_0=0.95 , we used the criterion from Stern et al. (2012) and obtained evidence that the object being investigated is probably an AGN (see also Edelson and Malkan (2012) and Secrest et al. (2015), where the same conclusion regarding WISE J144118.74-553335.1 was drawn).

gmw_00009837.name: IGR J16494-1740
gmw_00009837.overview: IGR J16494-1740 is identified with the galaxy ESO 586-4 [arXiv:1809.02949]. Spectroscopic observations classify it as a Seyfert 2 galaxy with an active nucleus at a redshift of z = 0.023 [arXiv:1809.02949].

sources.summary: IGR J16494-1740 is identified with ESO 586-4; RTT-150 spectra classify it as a Seyfert 2 galaxy, and the paper links its optical difficulty to absorption in an edge-on galaxy.

quotes.quote: Owing to the improvement in the position accuracy, we were abale to identify the X-ray source with the galaxy ESO 586-4 (also known as IRAS 16464-1733). Note that this object is present under the name 1SXPS J164920.9-173840 in the XRT/Swift catalog of soft X-ray sources (Evans et al. 2014). Our estimates of the X-ray flux agree well with its estimates from the above paper. At the same time, the localization accuracy of the object in our case turns out to be higher (probably due to the use of a more up-to-date version of the software). In the case under consideration, this is not so important, because the choice of an optical counterpart is obvious and unambiguous. Our spectroscopic observations of this galaxy with RTT-150 allowed us to obtain significant spectra of the object (Fig. 6) and to determine its class and redshift. In particular, a narrow H_ line and N II lines are clearly seen in the spectrum. Hence, we are dealing with a Seyfert 2 galaxy with an active nucleus at red-shift z = 0.023 0.001 (D_L 121 Mpc) .

Note that in Rojas et al. (2017), where the Swift/BAT catalog of sources with identifications is presented, this source is known under the name 2PBC J1649.3-1739 and it was also classified as a Seyfert 2 galaxy. Note that such an interesting effect as the absence of oxygen lines in the spectrum of a Seyfert 2 galaxy can be explained by significant additional absorption in the galaxy itself, because we see it edge-on. For this reason, such objects, even if they are located nearby, are fairly difficult to investigate in the optical band.

gmw_00009838.name: IGR J17098-2344
gmw_00009838.overview: IGR J17098-2344 is an active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of approximately 0.036 [arXiv:1809.02949]. Its optical spectrum exhibits an ambiguous Seyfert classification, showing characteristics of both a Seyfert 1.2 and a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1809.02949].

sources.summary: IGR J17098-2344 has a measured optical redshift and ambiguous Seyfert classification; its line ratios indicate Sy1.2, while Fe II and H width suggest a narrow-line Seyfert 1.

quotes.quote: Using our spectroscopic observations with RTT-150, we were able to determine the redshift of the optical counterpart z = 0.036 0.001 (D_L 163 Mpc) , which agrees well with the estimates of z 0.0364 from Durret et al. (2015) devoted to the spectroscopic studies of a large sample of galaxies. The study of spectral lines allowed the true class of the source to be determined. On the one hand, in contrast to the previous objects, here we ran into some ambiguity in interpreting the spectrum obtained. On the other hand, according to our observations, the line flux ratio F_ H /F_ O III for IGR J17098-2344 is about 3.7, which defines this object as an Sy1.2 galaxy. On the other hand, the equivalent width of the H line is less than 1200 km s ^ 1 and, in addition, a significant emission in Fe II lines is detected in the spectrum of IGR J17098-2344. The latter is typical for narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxies, NLSy1 (Osterbrock and Pogge 1985; Goodrich 1989).

Note also that for the best description of the source's broadband X-ray spectrum we had to add an exponential cutoff at a relatively low energy to the model of a power law with absorption, which is not characteristic for typical AGNs.

gmw_00009839.name: IGR J17422-2108
gmw_00009839.overview: IGR J17422-2108 is an extragalactic active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 0.106 [arXiv:1809.02949]. Optical and X-ray spectra classify the source as a Seyfert 1 galaxy that exhibits variability [arXiv:1809.02949].

sources.summary: IGR J17422-2108 is matched to one soft X-ray and optical counterpart; X-ray and optical spectra confirm an extragalactic, variable Seyfert 1 AGN at z=0.106.

quotes.quote: t  t  IGR J17422-2108 Owing to the fact that the sky region containing IGR J17422-2108 fell into the XMM-Newton field of view during the session of observations of the pulsar PSR J1741-2054 (ObsID 0693870101), we were able to improve the localization of this object and, having combined these data with the INTEGRAL data, to construct its broadband spectrum (Table 2, Fig. 8). It is clear from the XMM-Newton observations that only one soft X-ray source, which we will consider as a counterpart of IGR J17422-2108, falls into the INTEGRAL error circle. Despite the fact that the source is at the edge of the MOS2/XMM-Newton field of view, it is bright enough for its position to be determined with a 2 accuracy. The X-ray spectrum of this object reconstructed from the above data is well described by a power law with a slope = 1.70 0.05 supplemented by photoelectron absorption. Note that such a slope is typical for extragalactic objects.

The introduction of an additional factor equal to 1.49 0.44 in the model was required to achieve the best agreement between the data obtained by different observatories, which may suggest that this object is variable. Simultaneously two relatively faint optical objects fall into the XMM-Newton position error circle of the source (see Fig. 8). Using RTT-150, we obtained a spectrum for the optical object closer to the center of the XMM-Newton error circle of IGR J17422-2108. A broad redshifted H_ emission line and a number of other features, including narrow [ O III ] 4959, 5007 lines, are clearly seen in this spectrum. They confirm an extragalactic nature of the object and allow it to be classified as a Seyfert 1 galaxy at redshift z = 0.106 0.001 (D_L 505 Mpc) . Thus, we can compare the soft X-ray source detected by XMM-Newton with this optical object and classify IGR J17422-2108 as an AGN. Note that the soft X-ray counterpart of IGR J17422-2108 investigated in this section is also known under the name 1SXPS J174211.7-210354 (Evans et al.

gmw_00009840.name: IGR J18141-1823
gmw_00009840.overview: IGR J18141-1823 is associated with the soft X-ray source 1SXPS J181414.8-182310 [arXiv:1809.02949]. Mid-infrared colors from Spitzer data indicate that the object is likely an active galactic nucleus with broad emission lines [arXiv:1809.02949].

sources.summary: IGR J18141-1823 is associated with Swift/BAT and Swift/XRT sources; its X-ray spectrum and Spitzer colors indicate a likely broad-line AGN, though final confirmation is pending.

quotes.quote: t  t  t  t  IGR J18141-1823 Krivonos et al. (2017) pointed out that IGR J18141-1823 most likely corresponds to 4PBC J1814.1-1822 from the Swift/BAT catalog. Using the publicly accessible XRT/Swift observations of the sky region around the source (ObsID 00043949001), we improved the localization of the object, determined its flux, and reconstructed its broadband X-ray spectrum by combining them with the IBIS/INTEGRAL data (see Fig. 10, Tables 1 and 2). The source's spectrum can be described by a power law with a slope 1.95 supplemented by interstellar absorption. The soft X-ray source falling into the INTEGRAL error circle is also known under the name 1SXPS J181414.8-182310 (Evans et al. 2014).

To determine a possible counterpart of the source and its nature, we performed a further study of the object's fields in the mid-infrared using the archival data of the Spitzer space observatory (see Fig. 10). According to the data from the GLIMPSE/Spitzer sky survey, an infrared source with colors ( [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] ) = 0.874 and ( [ 5.8 ] [ 8.0 ] ) = 0.684 (without absorption) is close to the center of the error circle of the soft X-ray source that we associate with IGR J18141-1823. According to the color classification in Stern et al. (2012, ( [ 5.8 ] [ 8.0 ] ) > 0.6; ( [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] ) > 0.2 ( [ 5.8 ] [ 8.0 ] ) + 0.18; ( [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] ) > 2.5 ( [ 5.8 ] [ 8.0 ] ) - 3.5 ; see Fig. 4 in this paper), such colors correspond to an AGN with broad emission lines.

gmw_00009841.name: IGR J18544+0839
gmw_00009841.overview: IGR J18544+0839 is a source associated with a bright WISE counterpart [arXiv:1809.02949]. Its infrared colors and X-ray power-law spectrum suggest that the object is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1809.02949].

sources.summary: IGR J18544+0839 is tied to a WISE counterpart whose infrared colors and X-ray power-law spectrum suggest an extragalactic AGN, pending spectroscopic confirmation.

quotes.quote: IGR J18544+0839 The XRT/Swift (ObsID 00010173001, June 2017) and IBIS/INTEGRAL observations of the source allowed its X-ray spectrum to be constructed in a wide energy range, which is described by a power law with photoabsorption (see Fig. 12, Tables 1 and 2). According to the PanSTARRS data, only one fairly bright object falls into the XRT error circle. Using the WISE data, we can identify the PanSTARRS source with WISE J185422.29+083846.2, whose W2 magnitude is W2 = 11.264 0.028 and colors W1-W2 = 1 and W2-W3 = 2.6 . According to the criteria( W1-W2>0.8 and W2 < 15.05 ) from Stern et al.

(2012), this points to an extragalactic nature of the counterpart (note also a significant brightness of the source in the reddest WISE filter: W4 = 5.958 0.061 ) and allows the objects to be provisionally classified as an AGN. Since IGR J18544+0839 is localized not toward the Galactic bulge, the reddening corrections for the above colors were taken from the standard maps obtained by Schlafly and Finkbeiner (2011) and the extinction law from Indebetouw et al. (2005). The corrections are small (E(W1-W2) = 0.04) even for the first two filters and do not modify our preliminary conclusions about the nature of the source being investigated. Note also that the object's color (W2-W3) 2.65 , in addition to the above criterion, also suggests that IGR J18544+0839 is most likely an AGN (according to the criteria from Yan et al. (2013)).

gmw_00009842.name: MCG-02-22-003
gmw_00009842.overview: MCG-02-22-003 is a face-on galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z = 0.0144 [arXiv:1809.02949]. It is classified as a Seyfert 2 galaxy and is identified as the most probable counterpart to the hard X-ray source IGR J08215-1320 [arXiv:1809.02949].

sources.summary: MCG-02-22-003 is favored over NGC 2578 as the counterpart to IGR J08215-1320 because it is the harder X-ray source and has Seyfert 2 emission features.

quotes.quote: t  IGR J08215-1320 Two galaxies, NGC 2578 and MCG-02-22-003 (Skrutskie et al. 2006), that were marked in Krivonos et al. (2017) as possible identifications of the object fall into the INTEGRAL position error circle of IGR J08215-1320. We requested additional XRT/Swift observations of the source's fields (ObsID 00010023001, 00010023002, March 2017). According to the results obtained, both these galaxies are detected as faint X-ray sources with comparable fluxes. Thus, each of them could be a counterpart of IGR J08215-1320. However, if we compare the sky images constructed in the 0.5--3 and 6--10 keV energy bands (see Fig. 2), then it becomes clear that the galaxy NGC 2578 that we see edge-on is a significantly softer X-ray source than the galaxy MCG-02-22-003 that we see face-on. Thus, the most probable counterpart of IGR J08215-1320 is the galaxy MCG-02-22-003 (PGC 023449) that, according to the 6dF spectroscopic database (g0821335-132104; Jones et al. 2009), has a redshift z = 0.0144 (D_ L 62 Mpc) . According to this database, it possesses a set of characteristic features, such as narrow Balmer, N II, and O III emission lines, and is a Seyfert 2 galaxy.

gmw_00009843.name: Arp144
gmw_00009843.overview: Arp144 is a galaxy system that lacks a clear ring in both ionized gas and continuum bands [arXiv:0807.1477]. Its companion galaxy shows signs of interaction, including stripped stars on the side opposite to the partial ring [arXiv:0807.1477].

sources.summary: The paper describes Arp144 as lacking a clear ring in either ionized gas or continuum, with an interacting companion and a structure inconsistent with the classical collisional-ring scenario.

quotes.quote: Arp144: A clear ring is not seen either in \ or in continuum bands and the definition of the ellipse was based on the partial ring that can be traced in the . The companion galaxy shows signs of interaction, in the form of stripped stars on the side opposite to the ring galaxy. The system does not appear to belong to the classical lyn76 scenario, and is often considered to be the result of stripping of the gaseous disk from a spiral galaxy during the collision with an intergalactic HI cloud hig88 following a proposal by fre74 .

gmw_00009844.name: Arp145
gmw_00009844.overview: Arp145 is a ring galaxy featuring a broad continuum ring that is brighter and bluer on its southwestern side [arXiv:0807.1477]. Weak emission is restricted to the nucleus and a few knots, with substantial dust extinction concentrated on the northeastern side of the ring [arXiv:0807.1477].

sources.summary: The paper finds Arp145 has a broad, asymmetric continuum ring, weak emission confined to the nucleus and knots, and substantial dust on the northeast side.

quotes.quote: Arp145: The ring is broad in the continuum, especially on the southwestern side, where it is also considerably brighter and bluer and than the opposite side. The \ emission is weak, and is confined to the nucleus and a few other knots in the ring. A visual extinction value of >4.0 mag is inferred for this galaxy by comparing the SFR derived by \ and FIR. Color profiles suggest that most of this dust lies on the northeast side of the ring.

gmw_00009845.name: IIZw28
gmw_00009845.overview: IIZw28 is a ring galaxy with a ring that can be traced in both ionized and continuum light [arXiv:0807.1477]. Most of this emission originates from the northern segment of the galaxy, which contains its nucleus [arXiv:0807.1477].

sources.summary: The paper reports that IIZw28 has a ring visible in both ionized and continuum light, with most emission from the northern segment including the nucleus.

quotes.quote: IIZw28: The ring can be traced both in the \ and continuum bands of this galaxy. Most of the \ emission comes from the northern segment that includes the nucleus. There is no spectroscopically confirmed companion to this galaxy. We identify the most likely companion by G1 .

gmw_00009846.name: Arp141
gmw_00009846.overview: Arp141 is a ring galaxy characterized by an elliptical ring of compact emission knots and an underlying circular disk [arXiv:0807.1477]. The galaxy features an offset nucleus located outside the ring, along the line connecting the ring to its companion [arXiv:0807.1477].

sources.summary: The paper describes Arp141 as an unusual ring galaxy with compact emission knots, an offset nucleus, asymmetric disk geometry, and a tidally disturbed companion participating in the collision.

quotes.quote: Arp141: The ring in this galaxy is defined by compact knots of \ emission. Several regions of extended emission are seen inside this ring, which also have corresponding continuum emission. The underlying disk is circular, whereas the ring is clearly elliptical (eccentricity=0.16). The ring on the eastern side almost coincides with the edge of the disk, whereas it is only half way through the disk on the western side. The nucleus is seen outside the ring, along the line joining the ring with the companion.

gmw_00009847.name: IIHz4
gmw_00009847.overview: IIHz4 is a classical double-ring galaxy featuring a well-defined main ring and a faint second continuum ring to its north [arXiv:0807.1477]. While the second ring lacks emission, an additional emission region is located just south of the main ring [arXiv:0807.1477].

sources.summary: The paper describes IIHz4 as a classical double-ring galaxy with well-defined main ring emission, a faint second continuum ring, and an extra emission region south of the ring.

quotes.quote: IIHz4: Ring is very well defined both on the \ and continuum images of this classical double ring galaxy. The faint second ring can be seen to the north of the main ring on the continuum images, and is denoted by R2 . \ emission is not detected from the second ring. Nucleus of both the rings is identified. There is an \ emitting region without noticeable continuum emission, outside the ring, just south of it.

gmw_00009848.name: NGC2793
gmw_00009848.overview: NGC2793 is a ring galaxy featuring a ring traced by emission knots [arXiv:0807.1477]. Its nucleus is situated at the southern end of a bar-like elongation, which exhibits patchy emission and faint diffuse emission throughout the internal ring [arXiv:0807.1477].

sources.summary: The paper reports that NGC2793's ring is traced by emission knots, with its nucleus at a bar-like elongation and diffuse internal ring emission.

quotes.quote: NGC2793: The ring in this galaxy is traced by the \ emitting knots. The nucleus is located at the southern end of a bar-like elongation. Patchy \ emission is detected all along the bar. A compact knot of emission appears on the southern part of the disk outside the ring. Faint diffuse \ emission can be detected throughout the internal part of the ring.

gmw_00009849.name: Arp142
gmw_00009849.overview: Arp 142 is an interacting galaxy pair located 100 Mpc away, consisting of the spheroidal NGC 2937 and the spiral NGC 2936 [arXiv:1907.02193]. The system is approximately 50 million years post-pericenter [arXiv:1907.02193], following a high-speed head-on collision that produced a distorted disk and an off-center ring-like structure [arXiv:2106.15041].

sources.summary: The paper describes Arp 142, the Penguin, as a 100 Mpc interacting E+S pair whose morphology and kinematics match a roughly 50 Myr post-pericenter GalMer scenario.

quotes.quote: In this work, we analyze the spectacular interacting pair Arp 142, focusing on the link between the interaction, the newly formed regions, the gas, and the star formation feedback. The interacting system Arp 142 Arp:1966rp is located at a distance of 100 Mpc (corrected by the influence of Virgo, Great Attractor and Shapley, Mould:2000pb ) in the direction of the Hydra constellation. The system is composed by the spheroidal NGC 2937 and the spiral galaxy NGC 2936 that displays a complex morphology.

By comparing the radial velocity profiles and morphological characteristics of Arp 142 with a library of numerical simulations, we conclude that the current stage of the system would be about 50 25 Myr after the first pericenter passage.

Using GalMer simulations we are able to reproduce the observed radial velocity profiles and observed galaxy morphologies only considering the interaction of an elliptical and a spiral galaxy, discarding, as a first approach a third member on the interaction. The current stage of the system would be about 50 25 Myr after the first pericenter passage.
