In addition, the bar and the two spiral arms could not be fitted and a ^2_ value of 1.91 was obtained.

gmw_00009919.name: NGC 895
gmw_00009919.overview: NGC 895 is a late-type, grand-design Scd spiral galaxy characterized by two spiral arms [arXiv:2307.07803, arXiv:2206.03427]. The young stellar populations and star-forming regions within these arms exhibit regular spatial distributions with characteristic separation scales of approximately 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2307.07803, arXiv:2206.03427].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 895 as a distant two-arm grand-design Scd galaxy where regular chains and multiple characteristic distances appear in both spiral arms.

quotes.quote: The first study gusev2022 considered the distribution of the young stellar galaxy in the spiral arms of the Scd galaxies NGC 895, NGC 5474, and NGC 6946.

NGC 895 is a typical two-arm grand design galaxy; NGC 5474 is an asymmetric galaxy with three arms on one side of the center; the four-armed NGC 6946 appears to be flocculent, although its spirals are of a wave nature kendall2011,ghosh2016 (Fig. figure:fig1 ). In NGC 895, the most distant galaxy ( d=32.7 Mpc) among those considered in this section, found a regular chain of six stellar complexes with a characteristic distance of 1.4 kpc in only one of the two spiral arms. The analysis carried out in revealed spatial regularities in the distribution of the young stellar population in both spiral arms.

The first arm confirmed the 1.4-1.5 kpc scale obtained in , and a characteristic distance 1.0 kpc was found. The Fourier analysis also indicated the presence of the 500 pc scale (Figs. figure:fig2 , figure:fig3 ). In the second spiral arm, the characteristic distance scales of 1.0 , 1.5 , and 2.0 kpc were found, with the latter being confidently confirmed by the Fourier analysis as well.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 895 as a grand-design two-arm spiral whose star-forming regions show characteristic separations near 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 kpc.

quotes.quote: For this study we selected three late type spiral galaxies: NGC 895, NGC 5474, and NGC 6946 (Table table:sample ). Despite the similar morphological type, Scd, these galaxies have different structural features. NGC 895 is a classic grand gesign two-arms spiral (Fig. figure:map895 ).

Our analysis shows the presence of regularities with scales of 1 , 1.5, and 2 kpc, including a chain of four star formation regions with l=840 pc at s=9-12 kpc ( =5.2-6.7 kpc; Fig. figure:separate ). These three characteristic scales are confirmed by the corresponding peaks in the histogram in Fig. figure:hist . The mean separations in these three subsets, corresponding to the pairs with separations of 1 , 1.5 , and 2 kpc, are 0.87 0.23 , 1.45 0.21 , and 2.07 0.20 kpc, including the intrinsic errors.

gmw_00009920.name: 0000-D6
gmw_00009920.overview: 0000-D6 is a bright starburst galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 2.96 [arXiv:astro-ph/9707200]. It exhibits large-scale outflows and a P Cygni-type Lyman-alpha profile originating from an expanding envelope around its star-forming region [arXiv:astro-ph/9707200].

sources.summary: The paper presents 0000-D6 as a bright z~3 galaxy with strong Ly-alpha velocity structure, large-scale outflows, resolved nebular-line velocity dispersion, and inferred virial mass.

quotes.quote: The bright galaxy 0000-D6, reproduced in Figure 4, is a good example. The zero point of the velocity scale in Figure 4 is at z = 2.960 , the redshift of the strong interstellar absorption lines in 0000-D6. The peak of \ emission is at a relative velocity of 800 km s ^ 1 , and while the red wing extends to 1500 km s ^ 1 , the blue wing is sharply absorbed. This P Cygni-type profile can be understood as originating in an expanding envelope around the H II region; the unabsorbed \ photons we see are those back-scattered from the receding part of the nebula. In agreement with this picture, we find that the systemic velocity of the star-forming region is 400 km s ^ 1 , as measured from the wavelengths of weak photospheric lines from O stars (S V 1501.96 and O IV 1343.35 ), which can be discerned in this high S/N spectrum, and of [ O III ] 5007 which we have detected in the K band (see below).

Large scale motions of the type we have found in 0000-D6 could be the main reason for the strengths of the interstellar absorption lines in high- z galaxies which, with typical equivalent widths of 2-3 , are often greater than their counterparts in nearby starbursts.

A similar velocity dispersion is also found in the other two cases, 0000-D6 and cB58. (Incidentally, the fact that cB58 shows the same velocity dispersion as the other two z 3 galaxies, even though it is 10 times brighter, is another indication of its gravitationally lensed nature). If we combine = 70 km s ^ 1 with the half-light radii of 2 kpc deduced for both 0201-C6 and 0000-D6 from HST WFPC2 images, we obtain virial masses of 1.2 10^ 10 .

gmw_00009921.name: 0201-C6
gmw_00009921.overview: 0201-C6 is a high-redshift starburst galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9707200] with a half-light radius of 2 kiloparsecs [arXiv:astro-ph/9707200]. Its resolved nebular emission lines indicate a velocity dispersion of 70 kilometers per second [arXiv:astro-ph/9707200], yielding an estimated virial mass of 1.2 billion solar masses [arXiv:astro-ph/9707200].

sources.summary: The paper reports nebular-line detections in 0201-C6, measures resolved line velocity dispersion, estimates virial mass, and compares H-alpha and UV star-formation rates.

quotes.quote: Pilot observations which we carried out with the CGS4 spectrograph on UKIRT in September 1996 were successful in detecting \ and/or [ O III ] emission lines in both z 3 galaxies targeted, 0000-D6 and 0201-C6 ; a third measurement---in cB58---has been obtained by Gillian Wright (private communication). Figure 5 shows a portion of the K band spectrum of 0201-C6. \ and the weaker member [ O III ] doublet are both detected at the 5 level, whereas the stronger [ O III ] line is lost in the nearby sky emission. As can be seen from Figure 5, both \ and [ O III ] emission lines are resolved; after correcting for the instrumental resolution, we measure = 70 20 km s ^ 1 .

If we combine = 70 km s ^ 1 with the half-light radii of 2 kpc deduced for both 0201-C6 and 0000-D6 from HST WFPC2 images, we obtain virial masses of 1.2 10^ 10 . This is comparable to the mass of the Milky Way bulge and to the dynamical mass within the central = 2 kpc of an L^ elliptical galaxy. However, the total masses involved are likely to be substantially greater, given that the present IR observations sample only the innermost cores of the galaxies, where the star formation rates are presumably highest.

The \ flux of 0201-C6 (Figure 5), (2.6 0.6) 10^ 17 h_ 70 ^ 2 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 , corresponds to a luminosity L_ H = (2.3 0.5 ) 10^ 42 h_ 70 ^ 2 erg s ^ 1 . Adopting an / \ ratio of 2.75 and Kennicutt's calibration SFR ( yr ^ 1 ) = L_ H (erg s ^ 1 )/ 1.12 10^ 41 which is appropriate for a Salpeter IMF from 0.1 to 100 , we deduce a star formation rate SFR _ H = (55 13) h_ 70 ^ 2 \ yr ^ 1 . For comparison SFR _ UV = (20 - 35) h_ 70 ^ 2 yr ^ 1 , depending on whether the age of the starburst is 10^9 or 10^7 years respectively. Estimates of the star formation rate from \ emission and the UV continuum do not normally agree to better than a factor of 2 in local starbursts (e.g. ); the agreement in 0201-C6 is further improved when account is taken of the small amount of reddening ( E(B-V) 0.1 ) implied by the slope of the UV continuum ( = 0.35 ) using the prescription by Calzetti . We reach similar conclusions in 0000-D6 and cB58; in all three cases values of ultraviolet extinction as high as proposed by Meurer et al.

gmw_00009922.name: NGC 2695
gmw_00009922.overview: NGC 2695 is an S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1611.09859] classified as a massive early-type galaxy [arXiv:2109.14632]. It is one of the brightest members of a galaxy group that also includes the elliptical galaxy NGC 2699 [arXiv:1611.09859].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2695 as a primary S0 target, using deep radial spectra to find the same strong IMF gradient seen across the six early-type galaxies.

quotes.quote: The three primary targets of our study are the giant elliptical galaxies NGC 1407 and NGC 1600 and the S0 galaxy NGC 2695. NGC 1407 is the dominant member of the eponymous NGC 1407 group at 24 Mpc, which also includes the elliptical galaxy NGC 1400 ( Brough et al. 2006; Romanowsky et al. 2009). NGC 1600, at 50 Mpc, is generally considered an isolated elliptical, with an extensive system of satellite galaxies ( Smith et al. 2008). NGC 2695 is one of the brightest galaxies in a group that also contains the elliptical galaxy NGC 2699.

Using newly obtained spectroscopy for six galaxies, including deep data extending to 1R_ e for the galaxies NGC 1407, NGC 1600, and NGC 2695, we find that the IMF varies strongly with galactocentric radius. For all six galaxies the IMF is bottom-heavy in the central regions, with average mass-to-light ratio ``mismatch'' parameter (M/L)/(M/L)_ MW 2.5 at R=0 . The IMF rapidly becomes more bottom-light with increasing radius, flattening off near the Milky Way value ( 1.1 ) at R>0.4R_ e .

sources.summary: The paper measures stellar Lyman-alpha emission in NGC 2695 and uses its lower Lyman-alpha-to-i-band ratio relative to NGC 1407 as an IMF variation test.

quotes.quote: We use the Cosmic Origins Spectrograph on the Hubble Space Telescope to measure the \ line in the centers of the massive early-type galaxies NGC 1407 and NGC 2695. We detect \ emission in both galaxies and demonstrate that it originates in stars. We find that the \ to i band flux ratio is a factor of 2.0 0.4 higher in NGC 1407 than in NGC 2695, in agreement with the difference in their IMFs as previously determined from gravity-sensitive optical absorption lines. Although a larger sample of galaxies is required for definitive answers, these initial results support the hypothesis that the IMF is not universal but varies with environment.

gmw_00009923.name: 2MASX J12520684 + 2701352
gmw_00009923.overview: 2MASX J12520684 + 2701352 is an emission-line galaxy [arXiv:2207.06624]. It hosts an amorphous radio source with a very steep integrated spectral index and a larger spatial extent at lower frequencies [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper describes this Coma emission-line galaxy as an amorphous low-frequency radio source with larger 250-500 MHz extent and a very steep integrated spectrum.

quotes.quote: 1: 2MASX J12520684 + 2701352 (a.k.a. KUG 1249 + 272B) An emission-line galaxy KentandGunn . The radio morphologies in the 250--500 MHz and the 550--850 MHz bands are amorphous. The low-frequency, 250--500 MHz image has larger extent as compared to the 550--850 MHz image, probably due to synchrotron cooling. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 0 45 ( 12.6 kpc). The integrated spectral index is very steep, (400--700 MHz) = 1.23 0.13.

gmw_00009924.name: Mrk 53
gmw_00009924.overview: Mrk 53 is an Sa-type galaxy featuring a bright optical knot to its south [arXiv:2207.06624]. Its radio emission is asymmetric, displaying one-sided tails that extend toward the northwest beyond the optical host [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 53 as an Sa galaxy with one-sided northwest radio tails extending beyond the optical host and measured host-tail spectral indices.

quotes.quote: 3: Mrk 53 (a.k.a. KUG 1253 + 279) The optical host is Sa with a bright knot to the south KentandGunn . The radio emission at both 250--500 MHz and 550--850 MHz bands is one-sided showing asymmetric tails toward the northwest, extending beyond the optical host galaxy. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 1 23 ( 34.5 kpc). The host galaxy (D _ 25 = 19 0) and the tail have spectral index, (400--700 MHz), 0.64 0.03 and 0.43 0.04, respectively.

gmw_00009925.name: Mrk 55
gmw_00009925.overview: Mrk 55 is an Sa-type star-forming galaxy that displays signatures of a low-ionization nuclear emission-line region [arXiv:2207.06624]. Its radio morphology features emission associated with the optical host, two east-west aligned knots, and faint, low-frequency diffuse emission [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 55 as an Sa star-forming LINER-like galaxy whose radio morphology includes host emission, east-west knots, and faint low-frequency diffuse emission.

quotes.quote: 6: Mrk 55 (a.k.a. KUG 1254 + 276) The optical host is Sa with no signs of interaction and is classified as a star-forming galaxy showing signatures of low-ionization nuclear emission-line region galaxy KentandGunn,MillerOwen2002 . The radio morphology shows emission associated with the optical host and two additional knot-like (lobes) emission aligned along the east-west. There is also very low-surface brightness diffuse radio emission detected at 250--500 MHz. Although the radio morphology at the 550--850 MHz band is identical to the 250--500 MHz band, it has smaller extent with no extended very low-surface brightness diffuse radio emission. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 0 57 ( 16.0 kpc).

gmw_00009926.name: 2MASX J12581865 + 2718387
gmw_00009926.overview: 2MASX J12581865 + 2718387 is an irregular, emission-line galaxy [arXiv:2207.06624] located southwest of the Coma Cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0109316]. It is rich in neutral hydrogen [arXiv:2207.06624] with a regular gas disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0109316] and displays one-sided radio tails extending toward the southeast [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper describes this irregular, H I rich emission-line galaxy as having one-sided southeast radio tails, a 19.9 kpc projected extent, and a very flat spectrum.

quotes.quote: 9: 2MASX J12581865 + 2718387 (a.k.a. KUG 1255 + 275) The optical host is an irregular galaxy showing emission lines. More specifically, it is a H I rich galaxy with regular gas distribution KentandGunn,Gavazzi1989 . The radio emission at both bands, 250--500 MHz and 550--850 MHz, is one-sided, showing asymmetric tails toward the southeast, extending beyond the optical host galaxy with the 550--850 MHz image showing smaller extent than the 250--500 MHz image. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 0 71 ( 19.9 kpc). The integrated spectral index is very flat, (400--700 MHz) = 0.22 0.10.

sources.summary: The paper places KUG 1255+275 in southwest Coma near NGC 4839, with a regular extended gas disk, group-like velocity, and no evident strong ICM or tidal disturbance.

quotes.quote: IC 3913, KUG 1255+275, and Mrk 057 These three galaxies were detected in the SW of Coma, between 17 and 25 south of the cD galaxy NGC 4839. They display several common features: they are rich galaxies with regular gas distributions, where disks extend well beyond the optical (Figs. 1, 3 and 4). Systemic velocities for these galaxies are between 7400 and 7650 , close to the value of the NGC 4839 group, 7339 (Colless Dunn, 1996). Because of their position, lying far from the cluster center and outside the X-ray emission, no strong interaction with the ICM is expected. Although these three galaxies are likely to be part of the infalling NGC 4839 group (Neumann 2001), we see no signs of a gravitational interaction either optically or in or the radio emission.

gmw_00009927.name: Mrk 56
gmw_00009927.overview: Mrk 56 is an S0 optical host galaxy that exhibits emission lines [arXiv:2207.06624]. Its radio emission is one-sided, featuring asymmetric tails that extend toward the northwest beyond the optical host [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 56 as an S0 emission-line galaxy with one-sided northwest radio tails, smaller 550-850 MHz extent, and a very flat integrated spectrum.

quotes.quote: 10: Mrk 56 (KUG 1256 + 275) The optical host is an S0 with emission-lines MazzarellaandBalzano . The radio emission at both bands, 250--500 MHz and 550--850 MHz, is one-sided showing asymmetric tails towards the northwest, extending beyond the optical host galaxy. The 550--850 MHz image shows a smaller extent than the 250--500 MHz image. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 1 21 ( 33.9 kpc). The integrated spectral index is very flat, (400--700 MHz) = 0.11 0.08.

gmw_00009928.name: Mrk 57
gmw_00009928.overview: Mrk 57 is an irregular, hydrogen-rich H II galaxy located in the southwest region of the Coma Cluster [arXiv:2207.06624, arXiv:astro-ph/0109316]. It features a regular extended gas disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0109316] and asymmetric radio tails that extend toward the south beyond its optical host [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 57 as an irregular H II, H I rich galaxy with southern radio tails and a steeper tail spectrum than the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: 11: Mrk 57 The optical host is an irregular H II galaxy with emission-lines KentandGunn . It is H I rich with a H I distribution displaying an extension to the north-south BravoAlfaro . The radio emission at both bands, 250--500 MHz and 550--850 MHz, is one-sided, showing asymmetric tails toward the south, extending beyond the optical host galaxy with the 550--850 MHz image showing smaller extent than the 250--500 MHz image. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 0 90 ( 25.2 kpc). The host galaxy (D _ 25 = 29 25) and the tail have a spectral index, (400--700 MHz), 0.57 0.03 and 1.12 0.05, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper places Mrk 057 in southwest Coma near NGC 4839, with a regular extended gas disk, group-like velocity, and no evident strong ICM or tidal disturbance.

quotes.quote: IC 3913, KUG 1255+275, and Mrk 057 These three galaxies were detected in the SW of Coma, between 17 and 25 south of the cD galaxy NGC 4839. They display several common features: they are rich galaxies with regular gas distributions, where disks extend well beyond the optical (Figs. 1, 3 and 4). Systemic velocities for these galaxies are between 7400 and 7650 , close to the value of the NGC 4839 group, 7339 (Colless Dunn, 1996). Because of their position, lying far from the cluster center and outside the X-ray emission, no strong interaction with the ICM is expected. Although these three galaxies are likely to be part of the infalling NGC 4839 group (Neumann 2001), we see no signs of a gravitational interaction either optically or in or the radio emission.

gmw_00009929.name: IC 3949
gmw_00009929.overview: IC 3949 is an edge-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:2207.06624] classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2207.06624] and a ram pressure stripped jellyfish galaxy [arXiv:2207.06624, arXiv:2210.16013]. It features a southwest asymmetric radio tail [arXiv:2207.06624] and enhanced star formation on its leading half due to compressed interstellar gas [arXiv:2210.16013].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 3949 as an edge-on SA0 spiral/RPS galaxy whose low-frequency radio image shows a southwest asymmetric tail beyond the optical host.

quotes.quote: 12 (h): IC 3949 (a.k.a. KUG 1256 + 281) The optical host is SA0, an edge-on spiral galaxy and the radio emission is dominated by star formation in its disk with a more quiescent central bulge BothunandDressler . The source is classified as an AGN by KentandGunn and it is also classified as the RPS galaxy see Sec. tab:tab3-rps Chenetal . The radio emission at both 250--500 MHz and 550--850 MHz is one-sided. Only the 250--500 MHz image shows asymmetric tails toward the southwest, extending slightly beyond the optical host galaxy, whereas the 550--850 MHz image has smaller extent and is coincident with the extent of the optical host. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz is 0 50 ( 14.0 kpc).

sources.summary: The paper presents IC3949 as a jellyfish-galaxy case study where ALMA data show leading-half dense gas and enhanced star formation consistent with ram-pressure compression.

quotes.quote: We also present a case study on the distribution of molecular gas in one jellyfish galaxy from our sample, IC3949, using ALMA CO J=1-0, HCN J=1-0, and HCO ^+ J=1-0 observations from the ALMaQUEST survey. The H_2 depletion time (as traced by CO) in IC3949 ranges from 1 Gyr in the outskirts of the molecular gas disk to 11 Gyr near the galaxy center. IC3949 shows a clear region of enhanced star formation on the leading half of the galaxy where the average depletion time is 2.7 Gyr , in line with the median value for the galaxy on the whole. Dense gas tracers, HCN and HCO ^+ , are only detected at the galaxy center and on the leading half of IC3949. Our results favour a scenario in which ram pressure compresses the interstellar medium, promoting the formation of molecular gas that in turn fuels a localized increase of star formation.

gmw_00009930.name: 2MASX J12594009 + 2837507
gmw_00009930.overview: 2MASX J12594009 + 2837507 is an Sc-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:2207.06624] that hosts a wide-angle-tail radio source [arXiv:2207.06624]. Its radio emission extends well beyond the host galaxy to a projected linear size of 20.2 kpc [arXiv:2207.06624], displaying an irregular spectral-index map [arXiv:2310.20417].

sources.summary: The paper describes this Sc optical host as a wide-angle-tail source whose radio emission extends well beyond the galaxy, with a 20.2 kpc projected extent.

quotes.quote: 17: 2MASX J12594009 + 2837507 (a.k.a. KUG 1257 + 288B) The optical host is Sc type KentandGunn . The 250--500 MHz band image suggests that the source has a wide-angle tail morphology, whose radio emission extends much beyond the extent of the host galaxy. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 0 72 ( 20.2 kpc). The source was not covered in the 550--850 MHz band pointings.

sources.summary: The paper says KUG1257+288B has an irregular spectral-index map, including flat emission on the leading half and another flat northern region following a spiral arm.

quotes.quote: KUG1257+288B shows an irregular spectral index map. There is a region of flat emission on the leading half and then a second region of flat emission to the north that appears to follow a spiral arm in the galaxy (see Fig. fig:example_imgs_KUG1257+288B ). We also note that the short frequency spacing ( 144 MHz to 400 MHz ), and the relatively low S/N detection of KUG1257+288B at 400 MHz , may be contributing to the irregular map in Fig. fig:galaxy_specmaps .

gmw_00009931.name: 2MASX J13001780 + 2723152
gmw_00009931.overview: The optical host of 2MASX J13001780 + 2723152 is an irregular face-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:2207.06624]. It hosts a radio source with a projected linear extent of 50.2 kpc, featuring two symmetrical radio lobes of similar surface brightness and an offset core [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper describes this irregular face-on spiral as a radio source with two similar lobes near an offset core and a 50.2 kpc projected extent.

quotes.quote: 18: 2MASX J13001780 + 2723152 (a.k.a. KUG 1257 + 276) The optical host is an irregular face-on spiral galaxy KentandGunn . The 250--500 MHz band image suggests that the radio source emerges from the host galaxy and has two radio lobes, one just northeast of the radio core and the other east. Both radio lobes have similar surface brightnesses, and the radio core is offset from the two symmetrical radio lobes. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 1 79 ( 50.2 kpc). The source was not covered in the 550--850 MHz band pointings.

gmw_00009932.name: 2MASX J13004067 + 2831116
gmw_00009932.overview: The edge-on spiral galaxy 2MASX J13004067 + 2831116 is a member of the Coma Cluster [arXiv:2310.20417] with a systematic blue color [arXiv:astro-ph/0109316]. It exhibits a normal rotation pattern [arXiv:astro-ph/0109316] and a tailed radio morphology that extends far beyond the host galaxy [arXiv:2207.06624, arXiv:2310.20417].

sources.summary: The paper identifies KUG 1258+287 with an infalling Coma group, normal extended gas, blue color, high velocity, normal rotation, and nearby H I emission without an optical counterpart.

quotes.quote: KUG 1258+287, FOCA 195 and 098 These objects are part of a group dominated by 098 (see Biviano 1996). As all the galaxies in Coma detected in outside the X-ray emission, this group shows a normal content and a regular gas distribution (Figs. 16, 18 and 20), with the more extended than the optical disk and coinciding with the optical position. Particular features of these galaxies are their systematic blue m _ UV -b color (see Table 2 and Fig. 7 in Paper I), and their high radial velocity (between 8400 and 8880 ), indicating a fast accretion of the group towards the main cluster body. Normal rotation patterns are shown by the bluest objects KUG 1258+287 and FOCA 195 (see Figs. 17 and 19). emission equivalent to 2.2 10 ^ 8 was detected at 1.4 W of KUG 1258+287 but no optical counterpart is observed.

sources.summary: The paper includes KUG1258+287 because it has a one-sided 40 kpc 144 MHz tail and a spectroscopic redshift and velocity offset consistent with Coma membership.

quotes.quote: KUG1258+287 has a one-sided, 40 kpc tail at 144 MHz in LoTSS (see Fig. fig:example_imgs_KUG1258+287 ) but lacks an SDSS redshift, presumably due to its close proximity to a bright foreground star. The roberts2021_LOFARclust sample relies on SDSS spectroscopic redshifts and therefore does not include KUG1258+287. However, KUG1258+287 does have a spectroscopic redshift of z = 0.0298 from edwards2011 , and thus has a projected velocity offset of 1700 km s^ 1 from the systemic velocity of Coma. The velocity offset, alongside a projected cluster-centric radius of 0.34 R_ 180 , places KUG1258+287 as a Coma member galaxy according to the criteria of roberts2021_LOFARclust , and therefore we include it in our ram pressure galaxy sample.

sources.summary: The paper describes this edge-on spiral as a tailed radio source whose emission extends far beyond the host galaxy, reaching 26.9 kpc at 250-500 MHz.

quotes.quote: 21: 2MASX J13004067 + 2831116 (a.k.a. KUG 1258 + 287) The optical host is an edge-on spiral galaxy KentandGunn . The 250--500 MHz band image suggests that the source has tailed morphology, whose radio emission extends much beyond the extent of the host galaxy. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 0 96 ( 26.9 kpc). The source was not covered in the 550--850 MHz band pointings.

gmw_00009933.name: 2MASX J13004385 + 2824586
gmw_00009933.overview: 2MASX J13004385 + 2824586 is a radio galaxy hosted by an optical elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2207.06624]. Its radio structure spans a projected linear extent of 51.8 kpc and features two knotty jets with disturbed, east-bent lobes [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper describes this elliptical host as a two-jet radio galaxy with knotty jets, disturbed east-bent lobes, projection effects, and 51.8 kpc extent.

quotes.quote: 22: 2MASX J13004385 + 2824586 The optical host is an elliptical galaxy KentandGunn . The radio morphology in both bands, 250--500 MHz and 550--850 MHz, is nearly identical. The two jets traveling toward north and south show knotty features. In addition, the north and south radio lobes show disturbed morphologies, which are bent toward the east, suggesting that the motion of the host galaxy in the ICM is possibly responsible for these extensions. Furthermore, the northern jet is of larger extent than the southern radio jet, and the southern jet is also more bent, suggesting that projection effects are likely at play. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 1 85 ( 51.8 kpc).

gmw_00009934.name: NGC 4927
gmw_00009934.overview: NGC 4927 is a bright early spiral galaxy of type Sb [arXiv:2207.06624] that is possibly interacting with its neighbor, DRCG 27-62 [arXiv:2207.06624]. Its radio morphology features emission associated with the optical host and two lobes located just outside the galaxy [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4927 as a bright early spiral, possibly interacting, with optical-host radio emission, two lobes, and a northeast extension implying ICM motion.

quotes.quote: 24: NGC 4927 The optical host is a very bright giant early spiral of type Sb KentandGunn and is possibly interacting with its neighbor DRCG 27--62 BravoAlfaro . The radio morphology shows emission associated with the optical host and two additional radio lobes along the northwest and southeast directions. Both radio lobes are of nearly identical surface brightnesses and are located just outside the optical host galaxy. The northern radio lobe shows slight extensions of radio emission toward the northeast, suggesting motion of the optical host with respect to the ICM is at play. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 0 66 ( 18.5 kpc). The source was not covered in the 550--850 MHz band pointings.

gmw_00009935.name: KUG 1257 + 278
gmw_00009935.overview: KUG 1257 + 278 is a radio galaxy that exhibits extensions in its radio emission toward the east [arXiv:2207.06624]. This emission extends much beyond the optical host, reaching a largest projected linear size of 10.6 kpc [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper describes KUG 1257 + 278 as an RPS radio source with eastward emission beyond the optical host, 10.6 kpc extent, and consistent integrated spectrum.

quotes.quote: (k): KUG 1257 + 278 (a.k.a. GMP 3271) Both the 250--500 MHz and 550-250 MHz band images show extensions in radio emission toward the east, much beyond the extent of the optical host. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 10.6 kpc. The integrated spectral index is (400--700 MHz) = 0.86 0.29, which agrees, within uncertainties with the measurement at 1.4 GHz see Table tab:tab3-rps , Chenetal .

gmw_00009936.name: NGC 57
gmw_00009936.overview: NGC 57 is a galaxy located within a very sparse group environment [arXiv:2307.01863]. Due to the sparse nature of this group, its environmental measurements, such as its virial radius, are considered highly uncertain [arXiv:2307.01863].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 57 as one of two sample galaxies in sparse groups, uses lower-density group data, and flags its group measurements as uncertain.

quotes.quote: Thirteen of the fifteen galaxies in our sample appear in both the HDC and LDC, while the remaining two, NGC 57 and NGC 4914, are in very sparse groups and appear only in the LDC. This work used HDC data when available, and LDC data only for NGC 57 and NGC 4914. It should be noted that NGC 57's group virial radius from is quite large compared to those for the rest of our sample; because of this and the more relaxed LDC inclusion criteria, we should be cautious when working with group data for NGC 57 and NGC 4914. To characterize the density of each host galaxy's environment more directly, we used the coordinates provided by to make two n th-nearest-neighbor measurements in projection, defined as in : _n = n D^2_ p,n where D_ p,n is the projected distance to the n th galaxy from the galaxy of interest. We made a calculation for part of the galaxy sample, excluding NGC 57 and NGC 4914, with n=5 , a standard compromise value of n that allows for both measurements of small galaxy groups and avoidance of ultra-small number statistics, and then a measurement for the whole sample with n=2 , motivated by the NGC 4914 group, the smallest in the sample with three galaxies including NGC 4914 itself.

gmw_00009937.name: NGC 4914
gmw_00009937.overview: NGC 4914 is a galaxy located within a very sparse galaxy group [arXiv:2307.01863]. This group is the smallest in its sample, consisting of only three member galaxies including NGC 4914 itself [arXiv:2307.01863].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4914 as a sparse-group sample galaxy, uses LDC group data for it, and bases a nearest-neighbor choice on its small group.

quotes.quote: Thirteen of the fifteen galaxies in our sample appear in both the HDC and LDC, while the remaining two, NGC 57 and NGC 4914, are in very sparse groups and appear only in the LDC. This work used HDC data when available, and LDC data only for NGC 57 and NGC 4914. It should be noted that NGC 57's group virial radius from is quite large compared to those for the rest of our sample; because of this and the more relaxed LDC inclusion criteria, we should be cautious when working with group data for NGC 57 and NGC 4914. To characterize the density of each host galaxy's environment more directly, we used the coordinates provided by to make two n th-nearest-neighbor measurements in projection, defined as in : _n = n D^2_ p,n where D_ p,n is the projected distance to the n th galaxy from the galaxy of interest. We made a calculation for part of the galaxy sample, excluding NGC 57 and NGC 4914, with n=5 , a standard compromise value of n that allows for both measurements of small galaxy groups and avoidance of ultra-small number statistics, and then a measurement for the whole sample with n=2 , motivated by the NGC 4914 group, the smallest in the sample with three galaxies including NGC 4914 itself.

gmw_00009938.name: IC 485
gmw_00009938.overview: IC 485 is a spiral galaxy [arXiv:1702.06545] classified as a LINER or Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:2310.08998] with an optical nucleus [arXiv:1702.06545]. It hosts a luminous, multi-component water megamaser [arXiv:1702.06545] that is considered a candidate edge-on disk-maser system [arXiv:2310.08998, arXiv:2302.02572].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 485 as an Sa LINER/Seyfert galaxy hosting bright water masers, maps its nuclear maser components, and argues it is a candidate edge-on disk-maser galaxy.

quotes.quote: IC 485 , which is classified as a LINER or Seyfert galaxy, hosts a bright maser whose nature is still under debate.

Here, we report multi-epoch (six epochs) and multi-band (three bands: L, C, and K) observations, taken in 2018, with linear scales from 3 to 0.2 pc. We detected two 22 maser components separated in velocity by 472 , with one centred at the systemic velocity of the nuclear region of IC 485 and the other at a red-shifted velocity. We measured for the first time the absolute positions of these components with an accuracy better than one milliarcsecond.

In addition, a recent survey made by of 183 maser emission from AGN known to host 22 megamaser has shown that a significant fraction of the sample, including many known disk masers and among which IC 485, hosts emission from both transitions. Additionally, some of the targets has triple-peaked spectra also at higher frequency. While in IC 485, 183 GHz emission is detected only close to the systemic velocity, likely due to the expected weakness of the satellite lines. The detection of the two water masers transition in may still support and encourage our hypothesis of disk-maser. Analysing the position of the systemic feature and that of the red-shifted components, we assume that they are two of the three typical components present in the full spectrum of a water maser associated with a disk. A simple portrayal of our idea is presented in Fig. fig:modellino .

sources.summary: The paper reports IC 485 as a spiral galaxy with a broad multi-component luminous maser, faint unresolved 20 GHz continuum, LINER nucleus, and no significant maser-continuum offset.

quotes.quote: IC 485 is a spiral galaxy detected in the water maser line in 2006 in GBT program GBT06C-035. footnote:maser_url The maser has a 80 mJy peak and shows a broad profile. zhu2011 lists this object as a maser nondetection. Our VLA observations show a broad, multi-component maser with a similar peak ( 78 2 mJy) and a high luminosity, L_ iso = 868 46 \ L_ (Figure fig:IC485 and Table tab:masers ).

The 20 GHz continuum is detected but unresolved and faint ( 77 15 Jy beam ^ 1 peak flux density; Table tab:continuum ). This galaxy was also detected at 1.4 GHz (4.4 mJy), and its dominant radio energy source was classified as star formation by condon2002 . This classification does not exclude the presence of an AGN: liu2011 classify the optical nucleus of IC 485 as a LINER.

sources.summary: IC 485 is discussed as a disk-maser candidate whose 183 GHz spectrum contains a central systemic complex but no high-velocity features.

quotes.quote: IC 485 The 22 GHz maser system in IC 485 was discovered by the MCP and identified as a disk maser system because of its spectral structure Pesce_2015 , though the faintness of the high-velocity features makes confirmation of this system's disk nature with VLBI observations difficult (Ladu et al. in prep. ). Our 183 GHz spectrum of IC 485 is shown in the middle lefthand panel of fig:hex1 , along with a 22 GHz spectrum reproduced from Pesce_2015 (see Fig. 1 in that paper). The 22 GHz spectrum is an average of multiple epochs of spectral monitoring observations taken with the GBT.

The only component seen in the 183 GHz spectrum of IC 485 is the central set of systemic maser features, which span a velocity range of 60 around a central velocity of 8355 . The peak flux density of the systemic features is 8.4 mJy, which is a factor of 8 fainter than the typical peak flux density of the 22 GHz systemic masers. No high-velocity maser features are detected at 183 GHz, consistent with their relative faintness at 22 GHz (where they are more than an order of magnitude fainter than the systemic features).

gmw_00009939.name: J0847-0022
gmw_00009939.overview: J0847-0022, also known as IRAS 08452 0011, is an active galaxy located at a distance of approximately 200 Mpc [arXiv:1910.14314]. It hosts a triple-peaked water megamaser system consistent with a Keplerian rotating disk [arXiv:1910.14314], though its 183 GHz emission lacks high-velocity features [arXiv:2302.02572].

sources.summary: J0847-0022 is described as an unconfirmed disk maser candidate whose 183 GHz detection is only a faint systemic complex without high-velocity features.

quotes.quote: J0847-0022 The 22 GHz maser system in J0847-0022 was discovered by the MCP and identified as a disk maser system because of its spectral structure Pesce_2015 , though to date there have been no published VLBI observations to confirm the disk maser nature of this system. Our 183 GHz spectrum of J0847-0022 is shown in the middle righthand panel of fig:hex1 , along with a 22 GHz spectrum reproduced from Pesce_2015 (see Fig. 1 in that paper). The 22 GHz spectrum is an average of multiple epochs of spectral monitoring observations taken with the GBT.

The only component seen in the 183 GHz spectrum of J0847-0022 is the central set of systemic maser features, which span a velocity range of 70 around a central velocity of 15295 . The peak flux density of the systemic features is 6.3 mJy, which is a factor of 5 fainter than the typical peak flux density of the 22 GHz systemic masers. No high-velocity maser features are detected at 183 GHz despite them being more prominent than the systemic features at 22 GHz; the high-velocity features must thus be at least an order of magnitude fainter at 183 GHz than at 22 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 08452 0011 as a distant triple-peaked water megamaser galaxy with a Keplerian disk, VLBI kinematics, and an inferred black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: We present VLBI images and kinematics of water maser emission in three active galaxies: NGC 5728, Mrk 1, and IRAS 08452 0011. IRAS 08452 0011, at a distance of 200 Mpc, is a triple-peaked H _ 2 O megamaser, consistent with a Keplerian rotating disk, indicating a black hole mass of (3.3 0.2) 10 ^ 7 M_ .

Also, we provide the VLBI image and kinematics of a triple-peaked maser galaxy, IRAS 08452 0011, which has a distance of 213 15 Mpc This is the 3K CMB distance adopted from the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED) in its classic form, which uses H_ 0 = 73 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 for evaluating the Hubble distance. .
The bottom-right panel shows the spectrum of IRAS 08452 0011, which clearly shows the triple-peaked profile of a Keplerian disk maser.

gmw_00009940.name: ESO 269-G012
gmw_00009940.overview: ESO 269-G012 is a galaxy located at a distance of approximately 72 Mpc [arXiv:2302.02572]. It is identified as a disk-maser candidate, hosting both 22 GHz and 183 GHz water maser systems with triple-peaked spectral structures that suggest an edge-on disk [arXiv:2302.02572].

sources.summary: ESO 269-G012 is presented as a disk-maser candidate with rich 183 GHz triple-peaked structure, including systemic and high-velocity complexes matching the 22 GHz spectrum.

quotes.quote: ESO 269-G012 The 22 GHz maser system in ESO 269-G012 was discovered by Greenhill_2003b and identified as a disk maser system because of its spectral structure, though VLBI observations have not been carried out to confirm the disk maser nature of this system. Our 183 GHz spectrum of ESO 269-G012 is shown in the upper righthand panel of fig:hex2 , along with a 22 GHz spectrum reproduced from Greenhill_2003b (see Fig. 1 in that paper). The 22 GHz spectrum was observed with the 70 m Deep Space Network dish at Tidbinbilla.

The 183 GHz spectrum of ESO 269-G012 shows three prominent sets of maser features, coinciding in velocity with the three groups of maser features seen in the 22 GHz spectrum and matching the expected spectral structure for an edge-on disk system. The line complexes in the 183 GHz spectrum of ESO 269-G012 are relatively rich compared to many of the other spectra in our sample, with each of the three sets of lines containing multiple identifiable maser features. The peak flux density of the systemic features in the 183 GHz spectrum is 17.2 mJy, which is comparable to the peak flux density of the 22 GHz systemic masers. The peak flux density of the high-velocity features is 30.8 mJy, which is a factor of 3 fainter than the peak flux density seen in the 22 GHz high-velocity masers. Adopting a Hubble law distance of 72 Mpc, the isotropic luminosity of the 183 GHz maser emission from ESO 269-G012 is L_ iso 1337 L _ .

gmw_00009941.name: PLCK G138.6 + 62.0
gmw_00009941.overview: PLCK G138.6 + 62.0 is a compact, high-redshift starburst galaxy [arXiv:1506.01962]. It is strongly gravitationally lensed by a massive foreground source and exhibits an average far-infrared color [arXiv:1506.01962].

sources.summary: The paper treats PLCK G138.6 + 62.0 as a compact, strongly lensed high-redshift galaxy with very bright submillimetre flux and average far-infrared color among the sample.

quotes.quote: These figures clearly show that our sources are either single, compact objects at 2 \ resolution (as is the case for, e.g., PLCK G244.8 + 54.9 and PLCK G138.6 + 62.0), or giant arcs (e.g., PLCK G145.2 + 50.9 and PLCK G080.2 + 49.8). PLCK G244.8 + 54.9 and PLCK G138.6 + 62.0 have flux densities of 1054 10 and 664 8 mJy, respectively, making it implausible that these are individual high-redshift galaxies if not benefitting from a strong boost from gravitational lensing through a massive foreground source. At a 2 \ beam, intrinsic source sizes of unlensed galaxies would be at most 16 kpc, making it implausible to see a small group of high-redshift galaxies within a single beam.

In Fig.  fig:noagn we illustrate the impact of fiducial AGN contribution of 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 L_ FIR on the example of G138.6 + 62.0, a source with rather average FIR brightness and 250-to-350  m color in our sample.

gmw_00009942.name: PLCK G080.2 + 49.8
gmw_00009942.overview: PLCK G080.2 + 49.8 is a high-redshift starburst galaxy [arXiv:1506.01962]. It is strongly gravitationally lensed by a massive foreground source and appears as a giant arc in submillimeter observations [arXiv:1506.01962].

sources.summary: The paper presents PLCK G080.2 + 49.8 as a strongly lensed high-redshift galaxy observed at higher SMA resolution and classified among the giant-arc sources.

quotes.quote: All sources but PLCK G080.2 + 49.8 were observed through programme 2013B-S050 in the compact configuration, with a beam of about 2 2 . PLCK G080.2 + 49.8 was observed through DDT programme 2013A-S075 in the extended configuration, giving a beam of 0.8 0.5 . Data were calibrated in IDL using the MIR package, and analysis and imaging utilized the MIRIAD package. A full discussion of the interferometry is beyond the scope of the present analysis, and will be presented in a subsequent publication. Here we only use the 850 m morphologies to illustrate that these are indeed strongly gravitationally lensed galaxies.

These figures clearly show that our sources are either single, compact objects at 2 \ resolution (as is the case for, e.g., PLCK G244.8 + 54.9 and PLCK G138.6 + 62.0), or giant arcs (e.g., PLCK G145.2 + 50.9 and PLCK G080.2 + 49.8). PLCK G244.8 + 54.9 and PLCK G138.6 + 62.0 have flux densities of 1054 10 and 664 8 mJy, respectively, making it implausible that these are individual high-redshift galaxies if not benefitting from a strong boost from gravitational lensing through a massive foreground source. At a 2 \ beam, intrinsic source sizes of unlensed galaxies would be at most 16 kpc, making it implausible to see a small group of high-redshift galaxies within a single beam.

gmw_00009943.name: PLCK G145.2 + 50.9
gmw_00009943.overview: PLCK G145.2+50.9 is a gravitationally lensed galaxy that forms a giant arc and a near-complete Einstein ring around a red foreground object [arXiv:2009.13538, arXiv:1506.01962]. Its diagnostics are dominated by star formation [arXiv:2009.13538], with any active galactic nucleus contribution to its far-infrared luminosity limited to under 30 percent [arXiv:1506.01962].

sources.summary: The paper describes PLCK G145.2+50.9 as a lensed GEMS giant arc with low dense-gas ratios, the weakest tracer ratios in the sample, and star-formation-dominated diagnostics.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The phase centers of the observations were located at =16:09:17.76 and =+60:45:21.0 for PLCK G092.5+42.9, and at =10:53:22.56 and =+60:51:49.0 for PLCK G145.2+50.9.

quotes.quote: In particular, most of this paper will focus on the three brightest GEMS, namely PLCK G092.5+42.9, PLCK G145.2+50.9, and PLCK G244.8+54.9 (the ``Ruby''), the latter being a maximal starburst Forming most of its stellar mass within one to a few dynamical times, with star-formation intensities approaching the highest values expected for a vertically-supported, self-gravitating gas disk e.g., thompson05 . at z=3 with local star-formation intensities of up to 2000 M _ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 , but rather moderate SFEs of 1--10 Defined as the fraction of the available gas mass converted into stars per free-fall time. akin to giant molecular clouds in the Milky Way canameras17b . Our high-resolution PdBI, SMA, and ALMA ^ 12 CO and dust continuum interferometry indicate that PLCK G092.5+42.9 and PLCK G145.2+50.9 form giant arcs canameras15 , while PLCK G244.8+54.9 forms a partial Einstein ring canameras17a .

PLCK G092.5+42.9 and PLCK G145.2+50.9 have comparably low HCN/CO and HNC/CO ratios in the J=5 -4 transition (Table tab:lineratios ) and their dense-gas SLEDs likely peak at lower J levels than those of CO. However, for PLCK G244.8+54.9, the J=4 -3 and J=5 -4 ratios are consistent within 1 , suggesting very excited SLEDs in this extreme source. Variations in the line ratios demonstrate the need to carefully account for the excitation of high-density tracer molecules in order to obtain a deep understanding of the gas phase directly fueling star formation. We emphasize that, since line emissions are not resolved in our dataset, these ratios are consistently probing source-averaged quantities. Figure fig:lineratios shows that, for each rotational level, line ratios in the three GEMS broadly agree with previous studies of lensed SMGs. In general, HCN/CO and HCO ^+ /CO for J_ up =4 and 5 are slightly higher than the stack of SPT SMGs spilker14 . Only upper limits for PLCK G092.5+42.9 and PLCK G145.2+50.9 approach the low HCN/CO(5--4) ratio of 0.013 in the stack. danielson11 and oteo17 obtain higher HCN/CO and HCO ^+ /CO ratios in the Cosmic Eyelash, SDP9, and SDP11 than for the GEMS; this is not surprising since these studies use J=3 -2 and J=1 -0 ratios, which, in low-redshift ULIRGs, are generally higher than for J 4 due to the rapid decrease of the dense-gas SLEDs at J 3 papadopoulos14 . The spatially-integrated HNC/CO ratio in PLCK G244.8+54.9 is remarkably similar to the SPT stack, and it drops in PLCK G092.5+42.9 and PLCK G145.2+50.9 closer to the value of 0.01 in SPT0551 50 uncorrected for the CN(4--3) contribution, bethermin18 . For each tracer, PLCK G145.2+50.9 exhibits the lowest ratios among the GEMS.

In this context, HCO ^+ /HCN ratios and lower limits measured in PLCK G092.5+42.9 and PLCK G145.2+50.9 (see Table tab:denseratios ) are consistent with star-formation dominated systems, as expected from the lack of a strong AGN contribution to dust heating (C15). The ratios of PLCK G244.8+54.9 are more ambiguous as they lie just at the limit between the two regimes. While this also corroborates the lack of XDR imprint on the high-to-low- J CO line ratios in canameras18b , we emphasize that an enhanced HCO^+/HCN is not sufficient to firmly rule out the presence of XDRs see privon15 . The elevated cosmic-ray fields expected from the intense star-formation activity of the GEMS may also play an important role in setting the HCO ^+ /HCN ratios in dense cloud cores shielded from UV fields, as argued for instance by schirm16 for the Antennae overlap region. The abundance of HCO ^+ is most strongly affected by variations in the cosmic-ray ionization rates and the resulting abundance of free electrons, but could be either enhanced or decreased according to the literature e.g., papadopoulos07 . Given this on-going debate and the possible influence of metallicity braine17 or additional mechanisms see discussion in oteo17 , the HCO ^+ /HCN ratio unfortunately cannot provide definitive ISM diagnostics for the GEMS. Diagnostics from HNC/HCN The characterization of HNC/HCN provides additional constraints, in particular regarding the identification of PDRs and XDRs. Detailed analyses of Galactic molecular cloud cores acting as typical PDRs find HNC/HCN 0.1 -1.0 hirota98 and, more recently, hacar20 obtained a similar range in Orion and highlighted that this line ratio strongly decreases for higher gas kinetic temperatures. Interestingly, mid- J HNC/HCN ratios are systematically below unity in classical PDR models meijerink07 . loenen08 and costagliola11 proposed that additional mechanical heating from supernova explosions might be needed to explain the lowest range, HNC/HCN < 0.5 . The lower limit HNC/HCN > 0.54 in PLCK G145.2+50.9 and the ratios in PLCK G244.8+54.9 are thus consistent with the regime where PDRs dominate (see Fig.

sources.summary: The paper describes PLCK G145.2 + 50.9 as a giant-arc source forming a near-complete Einstein ring around a red foreground object, with AGN contribution below 30 percent.

quotes.quote: These figures clearly show that our sources are either single, compact objects at 2 \ resolution (as is the case for, e.g., PLCK G244.8 + 54.9 and PLCK G138.6 + 62.0), or giant arcs (e.g., PLCK G145.2 + 50.9 and PLCK G080.2 + 49.8). PLCK G244.8 + 54.9 and PLCK G138.6 + 62.0 have flux densities of 1054 10 and 664 8 mJy, respectively, making it implausible that these are individual high-redshift galaxies if not benefitting from a strong boost from gravitational lensing through a massive foreground source. At a 2 \ beam, intrinsic source sizes of unlensed galaxies would be at most 16 kpc, making it implausible to see a small group of high-redshift galaxies within a single beam.

The only target associated with a foreground object for which we currently have no good redshift estimate is G145.2 + 50.9, which forms a near-complete Einstein ring with a diameter of 10 \ around a very red foreground source, which we currently only detect in the J-band. For this galaxy, our optical/NIR photometry is not yet complete, and we must therefore defer a detailed analysis of the foreground source to a later publication.

Fig.  fig:decompir shows that we can rule out a bolometrically dominant AGN contribution from buried quasars, in all cases. Only three galaxies have best-fit results formally inconsistent with pure starbursts. In PLCK G092.5 + 42.9 and PLCK G244.8 + 54.9, the putative AGN contribution to the FIR luminosity is below 10 , and it is below 30 in PLCK G145.2 + 50.9. In all other cases, DecompIR finds the best fit for a pure starburst SED.

gmw_00009944.name: ID85001674
gmw_00009944.overview: ID85001674 is a starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z=3.62 [arXiv:1906.00040]. It is resolved with a size of approximately 3.1 kiloparsecs and exhibits a very cold temperature relative to the cosmic microwave background [arXiv:1906.00040].

sources.summary: The paper finds ID85001674 is resolved at z=3.62, very cold relative to the CMB, and plausibly affected by optically thick dust emission rather than simple cold dust reservoirs.

quotes.quote: Only the lowest redshift galaxy, ID85001674 at z=3.62 , is resolved with a FWHM size of 0''.42 0''.04 ( 3.1 0.3 kpc), implying a SFR surface density ( _ SFR ) of 22_ 9 ^ +13 M_ yr^ 1 kpc^2 .

We can verify this directly for the galaxy ID85001674 at z=3.623 . This objects is the coldest and closest in relative terms of the CMB temperature, and shows a combined size 0''.42 0''.04 from ++ observations. The sizes measured from / data alone are 0''.32 0''.07 and 0''.38 0''.04 , both about 1--1.5 lower than the combined size. This is contrary to what expected if CMB was affecting sizes, showing that indeed any CMB-induced size bias should be quite small.

A simple calculation for ID85001674 assuming a spherical geometry with constant density of dust within the measured radius R_e , using the measured dust mass from the thin models, and using from the opacity models would imply an optical depth ( = R_e ) at 100 m rest (the observed SED peak) towards the center of about 1.

gmw_00009945.name: ID85000922
gmw_00009945.overview: ID85000922 is a dusty starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z=4.44 [arXiv:1906.00040]. It has a compact size limit of less than 2.8 kiloparsecs and is associated with a very faint infrared counterpart [arXiv:1906.00040].

sources.summary: The paper treats ID85000922 as a z=4.44 dusty galaxy, giving its ALMA size limit, faint IRAC counterpart, and unusually steep but uncertain observed Rayleigh-Jeans slope.

quotes.quote: ID85000922 at z=4.4 has a size limit of <2.8 kpc, and the other two galaxies at z>5 appear to be more compact with FWHM sizes <1.9 kpc.

ID85000922 at z=4.44 has a very faint SPLASH IRAC 3.6 m counterpart at its ALMA position, while the blue source with 2''.0 offset appears to be unrelated having a well constrained z_ phot =0.86 Laigle2016 .

Note that the galaxy ID85000922 ( z=4.44 ) has also an abnormally steep _ obs =3.7 but with very large uncertainty. When including the CMB we still fit its SED with a fixed =2.3 , which seems required for producing both a good fit to the data and a conservatively cold dust temperature T_ dust =30 4 K. However, it should be noted that its dusty SED is not well constrained given the weak signal in the Herschel bands and we suspect that the SCUBA2 850 m photometry is boosted by noise.

gmw_00009946.name: ID20010161
gmw_00009946.overview: ID20010161 is a dusty, high-redshift starburst galaxy originally detected in the VLA 3 GHz radio catalog [arXiv:1906.00040]. It features a secure single line detection at 95.2 GHz, though its exact redshift solution remains less certain [arXiv:1906.00040].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ID20010161 as an ALMA-followed dusty galaxy with a secure single line and radio detection, while noting that its redshift solution is less certain.

quotes.quote: We singled out four galaxies among these candidates for further ALMA follow-up, based on their highest photometric redshifts z_ phot, FIR >6 that were derived using the z=6.3 HFLS3 template Riechers2013Nature . Among these four galaxies, ID20010161 was originally detected in the VLA 3 GHz catalog Smolcic2017 , while the others were found in residual images from the SCUBA2 850 m maps from , after subtracting all known sources in our super-deblending process (see details in Sec. 4.5 in Jin2018cosmos ). The four sources have no detection in HST nor in UltraVISTA images McCracken2012UVISTA,Laigle2016 , while we found red IRAC counterparts in the Spitzer Large Area Survey with Hyper-Suprime-Cam (SPLASH, P. Capak et al., in preparation) imaging datasets, well-aligned with their VLA/SCUBA2 positions with offsets <3'' . The subsequent ALMA observations with sub-arcsec beam, described in the next sections, leave no further doubts on the correctness of the IRAC counterpart identification.

The ID20010161 has a single line detection at 95.2 GHz with 5.97 significance and a spurious probability of 10^ 5 (Table tab:1 ), hence it is highly secure. However the redshift identification is less secure, as there are multiple solutions given the lack of a second line.

The ID20010161 and ID85001674 galaxies are detected in the 3 GHz image with 5.0 and 3.6 Smolcic2017,Daddi2017 , showing infrared-to-1.4 GHz radio luminosity ratio q_ IR =2.3 and 2.4, which agree well with the evolution of q_ IR (z) = (2.88 0.03)(1 + z)^ −0.19 0.01 for star forming galaxies in , while the other sources are either marginally or not detected in the radio ( <2.8 ).

gmw_00009947.name: SDSSJ083843.63+385350.5
gmw_00009947.overview: SDSSJ083843.63+385350.5 is an emission-line Green Pea galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.147 [arXiv:1908.03234]. Spatially resolved kinematical analysis reveals three distinct features linked to a rotating component, a strong outflow, and a turbulent mixing layer [arXiv:1908.03234].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the Green Pea galaxy SDSSJ083843.63+385350.5 with IFU spectroscopy, identifying multiple kinematic components linked to rotation, a strong outflow, and turbulent mixing.

quotes.quote: We perform a spatially resolved kinematical study of a single Green Pea (GP) galaxy, SDSSJ083843.63+385350.5, using a new method for analyzing Integral Field Unit (IFU) observations of emission line spectra. The method considers the presence of multiple components in the emission-line profiles and makes use of a statistical indicator to determine the meaningful number of components to fit the observed profiles. We are able to identify three distinct kinematic features throughout the field and discuss their link with a rotating component, a strong outflow and a turbulent mixing layer. We also derive an updated star formation rate for and discuss the link between the observed signatures of a large scale outflow and of the Lyman continuum (LyC) leakage detected in GP galaxies.

In this paper we present 3D spectroscopic data of SDSSJ083843.63+385350.5 (hereafter ), an emission-line galaxy located at _ J2000 = 08:38:43.6 and _ J2000 = 38:53:50.0 (see Fig. \ sloan ) at a redshift of z=0.147. This galaxy is one of the brightest objects of a large sample of GPs selected by Amor in et al. \ (in prep.) from the SDSS-DR7 for detailed analyzes using low and high-dispersion spectroscopy.

gmw_00009948.name: NGC 1090
gmw_00009948.overview: NGC 1090 is classified as a spiral galaxy [arXiv:2005.11316]. It is characterized as a low-surface-brightness galaxy, representing an extreme case where the rotation curve deviates from the baryonic contribution at relatively small radii [arXiv:2005.11316].

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 1090 as a low-surface-brightness spiral whose rotation curve is fit with a modest amplification factor despite prior dark-matter interpretations.

quotes.quote: NGC 1090 In Fig. fig:1090 we have modelled the observed RC of spiral galaxy NGC 1090 shown in Fig. fig:1090jpg quite successfully as V_ mult2D (R) under the assumption of an amplification factor F = 1.5 and an exponent = 1.15 . In this case, the high exponent might be caused by the small amplification factor F and a relatively great _0 indicating fewer multiple interactions of gravitons. LovKie write that there is a clear need for CDM for explaining the RCs of NGC 1090 (and other galaxies), however, its distribution is unclear. KadJoWei define a radius R_ X , where the relative contribution of DM to the observed RC V_ obs equals that of the baryonic mass M_ bar . For NGC 1090 they find R_ X = 23.2 and emphasize the findings of e.g. PerSal and PeSaSt that for LSB galaxies, such as NGC 1090 as extreme case, cf. Geetal04 and also http://www.kopernik.org/images/archive/n1090.htm, the RCs deviate from the baryonic contribution at smaller radii than for HSB ones.

gmw_00009949.name: UGC 3205
gmw_00009949.overview: UGC 3205 is classified as an early-type, high-surface-brightness disk galaxy [arXiv:2005.11316]. It features a weak bar structure and exhibits a declining rotation curve at large radii [arXiv:2005.11316].

sources.summary: The paper presents UGC 3205 as an early-type high-surface-brightness disk with declining outer rotation curve, comparing MOND and the authors multiple-interaction fit.

quotes.quote: UGC 3205 In Figs. fig:3205bar to fig:3205DB , we plot RCs of the early-type (HSB) disk galaxy UGC 3205 with data taken from SanNoo , which are slightly modified in the next two figures. In Fig. fig:3205disk the bulge component is not included and in Fig. fig:3205DB the bulge component is distributed over the disk. The observed RC is declining at large radii. SanNoo present a fit of UGC 3205 with MOND and find deviations from the observed velocities below 10 kpc, tentatively attributed to the weak bar of this galaxy, but quite good agreement at large radii. We apply our multiple interaction model in Fig. fig:3205bar and obtain a good fit even at small radii.

gmw_00009950.name: SDSS J2257+2349
gmw_00009950.overview: SDSS J2257+2349 is a lensed quasar system at a redshift of 2.11 [arXiv:1706.01506]. The system features two point-source images separated by 1.67 arcseconds on either side of an E/S0 lens galaxy [arXiv:1706.01506].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J2257+2349 as a lensed quasar system with two images around an E/S0 galaxy, source redshift, and SIE parameters matching the lens light.

quotes.quote: The images show two point sources separated by 1.67 ^ on either side of an E/S0 galaxy. Spectra show that both point sources correspond to the same quasar at redshift z_s = 2.11 , as indicated by the Si IV + O IV ] , C IV, and C III ] emission lines. The SIE fit with fluxes infers an ellipticity e = 0.357_ 0.003 ^ +0.002 and ellipticity position angle 31.7 0.01 degrees. These values agree with the orientation of the lens galaxy as seen in the right panels of Figure fig:images and the mass centroid from the fit matches the light centroid from the images. When fluxes are not included as constraints, the position angle is only constrained with an upper bound, but when the fluxes are used, all parameters are well constrained.

gmw_00009951.name: NGC 3499
gmw_00009951.overview: NGC 3499 is classified as an early-type galaxy [arXiv:1111.4241]. Its gas is polar relative to the stellar kinematics, yet it remains aligned with a faint stellar disc and a dust lane [arXiv:1111.4241].

sources.summary: The survey describes NGC 3499 as an early-type galaxy whose gas is polar rather than stellar-aligned, yet still associated with a faint stellar disc and dust lane.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:pv shows position-velocity diagrams of all d 's along the stellar kinematical major axis given in Paper II (perpendicular to it in the case of NGC 3499 -- see below). Comparing these diagrams with the stellar velocity fields published in Paper II we see that the \ rotation is aligned with that of the stars in all galaxies except NGC 3032, where the \ is counter-rotating, and NGC 3499, where the \ is polar. Even in these two cases a connection between \ and stellar body exists.
In the latter the \ is aligned with a faint stellar disc and a dust lane visible in the SDSS image.

gmw_00009952.name: MGTC J095959.63+024608.6
gmw_00009952.overview: MGTC J095959.63+024608.6 is a giant radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.1656 [arXiv:2012.05759]. It has a physical size of 2.42 Mpc and features newly detected diffuse outer lobes and radio jets [arXiv:2012.05759].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MGTC J095959.63+024608.6 as GRG1, a z=0.1656 giant radio galaxy whose diffuse lobes and jets are newly detected by MIGHTEE, with a 2.42 Mpc size and LERG elliptical host.

quotes.quote: Following IAU conventions, we name these sources MGTC J095959.63+024608.6 (hereafter GRG1) and MGTC J100016.84+015133.0 (hereafter GRG2).

MGTC J095959.63+024608.6 (GRG1) MIGHTEE data GRG1 is shown in Figures fig:grgs-Lband , fig:grg1-optical and fig:grg1-radio . The two diffuse outer lobes and jets are detected for the first time in MIGHTEE, thus newly identifying this object as a GRG. The core of GRG1 is located at R.A. = 09h59m59.63s and Dec = +02d46m08.6s and has an elongated structure in MIGHTEE. It is associated with a host galaxy identified in optical and near-infrared data (see Figure fig:grg1-optical inset) with a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.1656 , according to the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Data Release 14 (SDSS DR14; abolfathi18 ).

Summing the distance between the outer edges of each lobe and the central core, the total projected angular size of GRG1 is 13.8 arcmin. It therefore has a physical size of 2.42 Mpc, which places it solidly in the regime of giant radio galaxies.

gmw_00009953.name: MGTC J100016.84+015133.0
gmw_00009953.overview: MGTC J100016.84+015133.0 is a giant radio galaxy at a redshift of z=0.3363 with a projected linear size of 2.04 Mpc [arXiv:2012.05759]. Its host is a massive, quiescent elliptical galaxy that behaves as a radiatively-inefficient low-excitation radio galaxy [arXiv:2012.05759].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MGTC J100016.84+015133.0 as GRG2, a z=0.3363 giant radio galaxy with newly detected MIGHTEE lobes, 2.04 Mpc extent, an elliptical host, and weak radiative AGN signatures.

quotes.quote: MGTC J100016.84+015133.0 (GRG2) MIGHTEE data GRG2 is shown in Figures fig:grgs-Lband , fig:grg2-optical and fig:grg2-radio . The core is situated at R.A.=10h00m16.84s and Dec=+01d51m33.0s. The associated host galaxy has a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.3363 according to the zCOSMOS Bright catalogue ( lilly07 , lilly09 ). Both lobes and a potential hot-spot within the northern lobe are clearly detected for the first time in MIGHTEE, as is the entire northern jet and some weak emission from the southern jet.
Based on these data, GRG2 has a projected angular size of 6.8 arcmin and therefore a projected linear size of 2.04 Mpc.

The host galaxy of GRG2 has a stellar mass of M_ * =10^ 10.8 M _ and a SFR of 0.5 M _ yr ^ 1 , placing it well below the main sequence of star formation. Its quiescent nature is further supported by its classification as `red' based on its ( NUV- ) and ( J ) colours in COSMOS2015. We therefore conclude that GRG2, like GRG1, is a radiatively-inefficient LERG with a massive, red, passive elliptical host.

gmw_00009954.name: NGC 3808B
gmw_00009954.overview: NGC 3808B is a nearly edge-on spiral galaxy interacting with its companion, NGC 3808A [arXiv:1511.05166]. Gas transferred from NGC 3808A via a tidal bridge is forming a polar ring around NGC 3808B, triggering the birth of young, massive compact stellar systems [arXiv:1511.05166].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 3808B as an interacting polar-ring galaxy in formation, fed by gas from NGC 3808A and hosting young massive clusters along its ring and tidal debris.

quotes.quote: NGC 3808 is a double interacting system between two spiral galaxies (see Fig. fig:selection , left). The main galaxy is NGC 3808A (face-on) and its interacting companion is NGC 3808B (nearly edge-on). The gravitational interaction of the two systems is evidenced by the tidal bridge via which gas is being transferred from the spiral arms of NGC 3808A to NGC 3808B. This gas transfer leads to the formation of a narrow ring around NGC 3808B, which rotates on a nearly orthogonal plane .

The interacting system, NGC 3808A-B, is at an early stage in the formation of its polar ring. This is evidenced by the low surface brightness stream connecting the two galaxies (cf. Fig. fig:selection and fig:projdist ). The size of the forming ring around NGC 3808 B is comparable to the optical diameter of the host. We observe the formation of young and massive compact stellar systems as a consequence of this tidal interaction evidenced by their presence not only in the main galaxy body, but also along the polar ring and the tidal bridge.

gmw_00009955.name: NGC 3808A
gmw_00009955.overview: NGC 3808A is a face-on spiral galaxy that is part of a double interacting system [arXiv:1511.05166]. It is gravitationally interacting with its nearly edge-on companion, NGC 3808B, transferring gas to it via a tidal bridge and experiencing tidally triggered star formation [arXiv:1511.05166].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3808A as the face-on main galaxy donating gas to NGC 3808B, with star-formation peaks that support tidally triggered cluster formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 3808 is a double interacting system between two spiral galaxies (see Fig. fig:selection , left). The main galaxy is NGC 3808A (face-on) and its interacting companion is NGC 3808B (nearly edge-on). The gravitational interaction of the two systems is evidenced by the tidal bridge via which gas is being transferred from the spiral arms of NGC 3808A to NGC 3808B.

NGC 3808A has two well-pronounced peaks of star formation at around 1 Gyr ( 130M_ /yr) and 400 Myr ( 120M_ /yr). The strongest peak of SFR at around 1-2 Gyr also corresponds with the strongest peak of SFR in NGC 3808B (SFR 208M_ /yr). The temporal coincidence of the two peaks is in support of the tidal interaction triggered star formation.

gmw_00009956.name: NGC 4650
gmw_00009956.overview: NGC 4650 is a spiral companion galaxy located at the same radial distance as the polar ring galaxy NGC 4650A [arXiv:1511.05166, arXiv:astro-ph/0301391]. It is a candidate donor galaxy whose tidal accretion may have formed NGC 4650A's massive polar ring [arXiv:astro-ph/0301391].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4650 as the nearby spiral companion to NGC 4650A, comparing FUV-derived star formation and considering whether a past fly-by shaped the polar ring.

quotes.quote: The nearby spiral galaxy (NGC 4650) has L _ FUV =1.56 10^ 26 erg/s , yielding SFR _ FUV =0.022 M_ /yr. There is a difference in the FUV-based SFR estimates, where the current SFR of the PRG NGC 4650 A is 30 that of the NGC 4650. If we normalize to the total, galaxy baryon mass (stellar plus HI) translates to a specific SFR (sSFR) of 1.56 10^ 13 yr^ 1 for NGC 4650 A and 3.93 10^ 14 yr^ 1 for its companion. Therefore, we find that the sSFR is larger for the polar disk galaxy than for NGC 4650.

Finding multiple peaks in the age distribution of the star cluster population, as opposed to a more continuous age distribution or a single peak, could suggest that NGC 4650 A may be on a closed (eccentric) orbit around NGC 4650. With this in mind, we can calculate what might be the orbital periods assuming that both galaxies are gravitationally bound (for a 65 Kpc separation, the period is P 2Gyr). If the orbital period matches the peak age of the star clusters' age distribution this would imply that it is not a single fly-by tidal interaction event, but rather episodic.

sources.summary: The paper identifies spiral galaxy NGC 4650 as close to NGC 4650A and a plausible massive donor whose tidal accretion could explain NGC 4650A's massive polar ring.

quotes.quote: NGC 4650A and the spiral galaxy NGC 4650 are at the same radial distance (the radial velocity of NGC 4650 is 2909 km.s ^ 1 ). The real distance between both galaxies is only about 6 times the optical radius of NGC 4650. A tidal accretion event may then have form the polar ring from NGC 4650, and may easily explain why the ring is massive, since NGC 4650 is much more massive than NGC 4650A host galaxy. NGC 4650 is thus a candidate donor galaxy.

gmw_00009957.name: NGC 3808 B-8
gmw_00009957.overview: NGC 3808 B-8 is a young ultracompact dwarf galaxy candidate with a photometric age of approximately 1 Gyr [arXiv:1511.05166]. The object is projected on tidal debris and is believed to have formed during a galactic tidal interaction [arXiv:1511.05166].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 3808 B-8 as a young UCD candidate projected on tidal debris, measuring its size, mass, age, and expected passive evolution into the UCD region.

quotes.quote: We identify one such young UCD candidate, NGC 3808 B-8, and measure its size of r_ eff =25.23^ +1.43 _ 2.01 pc.

NGC 3808 B-8 mass ( M =4.6 10^6M_ ) and size ( r_ F555W, eff =25.2 pc) places this object in the region of observed massive and young UCDs and/or UCD progenitors. NGC3808 B-8 has a photometric age of t 1 Gyr, which implies that for an assumed solar metallicity and a Kroupa IMF, the CB07 SSP models give a mass loss of about 27 due to stellar evolution from 1 to 14 Gyr. That is, the passive evolution of NGC3808 B-8 will result in a final cluster mass of 3.3 10^6M_ as well as a decrease in size by about 30 Baumgardt03 , i.e., r_ F555W, eff 17.5 pc. This will place this object in the area of observed UCDs and will support the idea that they can form during galactic tidal interactions Hilker09,Mieske12,Norris14,Taylor15 .

gmw_00009958.name: SDSS J085431.18+173730.5
gmw_00009958.overview: SDSS J085431.18+173730.5 is a compact early-type galaxy [arXiv:1603.07832] located in a nearly isolated environment [arXiv:1603.07832]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1603.07832] with a central black hole [arXiv:1603.07832, arXiv:2107.02141] and exhibits broad emission lines in its optical spectrum [arXiv:1603.07832, arXiv:2107.02141].

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSS J085431.18+173730.5 as a compact early-type galaxy with broad-line emission, a 2.1 million solar-mass black hole, and an isolated environment.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a rare compact early-type galaxy, SDSS J085431.18+173730.5 (hereafter cE AGN). It has an half light radius of R _ e = 490 pc and a brightness of M _ = 18.08 mag. Optical spectroscopy available from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) reveals the presence of prominent broad-line emissions with the H broad component width of FWHM=2400 km/s. The black hole (BH) mass, as estimated from the luminosity and width of the broad H emission, is 2.1 10 ^ 6 M _ .

In this work, we report the discovery of another cE type galaxy, SDSS J085431.18+173730.5, located in a nearly-isolated environment that possesses a prominent broad-line emission in the optical spectrum; a likely sign of nuclear accretion by the central BH. In this work we assume a cosmology with H _ 0 = 71 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , _ M = 0.3, and _ = 0.7. SDSS J085431.18+173730.5 (hereafter cE AGN) has been serendipitously discovered in SDSS-DR12 Alam15 in the course of searching for low-luminosity early-type galaxies in isolated or low density environments. It is located at z = 0.014 with no significantly massive neighbor.

sources.summary: The paper treats J085431.18+173730.5 as a compact elliptical with a reported black hole, identifies it as a broad-line quasar, and finds it consistent with an intrinsic origin.

quotes.quote: The number of cEs with reported BHs is even lower (e.g. M32, vanderMarel1997 ; J085431.18+173730.5, Paudel2016 ; or NGC 741, Schellenberger2017 ).

6 of them also fulfill the signal-to-noise criteria required for the [ OIII ] /H _ vs. [ SII ] /H _ diagram (right panel), and one (J085431.18+173730.5) is a known quasar with broad emission lines Paudel2016 .

NGC 741 is a clear outlier in both panels but instead J085431.18+173730.5 falls on top of the scaling relation with the stellar mass. This suggests that for this cE an intrinsic nature is plausible, which is in agreement with the original discovery paper Paudel2016 .

gmw_00009959.name: Hyd1
gmw_00009959.overview: Hydrus I is an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy located approximately 28 kiloparsecs away [arXiv:2001.01107]. It is a very metal-poor system [arXiv:2001.01107] whose orbit and kinematics strongly suggest it is a satellite associated with the Large Magellanic Cloud [arXiv:1804.10230, arXiv:2001.01107].

sources.summary: Hydrus I is described as a newly announced satellite with M2FS spectroscopy, four usable confirmed members, and an orbit that tracks the LMC closely.

quotes.quote: The most recently-found dwarfs (Carina II, Carina III, and Hydrus I) were identified in imaging surveys near the south celestial pole that are not currently public, so for those objects I use the coordinates and magnitudes provided in the discovery papers.

The most recently discovered Milky Way satellite is Hydrus I (Hyi I), announced just one week ago. In the discovery paper, koposov18 presented Magellan/M2FS spectroscopy of a sample of 30 Hyi I member stars. Combining the spectroscopic and RR Lyrae lists, I have 4 confirmed Hyi I members and 5 confirmed non-member stars brighter than g=20 .

Viewed in three dimensions and integrated backward in time, the orbits of Hor I, Hyi I, Car III, and Tuc II track that of the LMC quite closely, suggesting that these objects are probably associated with the Magellanic system. deason15 and sales17 both consider Hor I and Tuc II to be the most likely dwarfs of Magellanic origin, which is consistent with the results reported here. As discussed earlier, the sample of members stars currently available in Hyi I is only 3.

sources.summary: The paper uses Koposov membership probabilities for Hydrus I, keeps 30 candidates, tests a low-probability member, and agrees with Kallivayalil and Simon.

quotes.quote: Hydrus I In the case of Hydrus I a spectroscopic catalog with continuous membership probabilities is published in the electronic table of Koposov_18 . Of the 33 candidates, 30 remain after our cuts. One remaining member has a relatively low membership probability of 56 ; if excluded, the motion changes by 0.1/0.4 in R.A./Dec. Kallivayalil_18 added some more photometric candidate members. Our motion agrees with their motion and also with Simon_18 within about 1 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Hydrus I as a very metal-poor UFD and likely LMC satellite, recovering two known RR Lyrae and adding two new outer members.

quotes.quote: Hydrus I (Figure fig:HydrusI ) htb Hydrus I (Hyd I) is a UFD satellite with M_V 4.7 mag discovered by koposov18 using Dark Energy Camera (DECam) data. The kinematics for 30 stars revealed the nature of this system as a galaxy. Hyd I is a very metal-poor galaxy with a mean metallicity [ Fe/H ] = 2.5 , located between the LMC and SMC, at 28 kpc from us. Its position on the sky and its line of sight velocity, makes Hyd I a strong candidate to be a LMC satellite. The color-magnitude diagram (see Figure 2 in koposov18 ) shows a few stars in the HB. Particularly, two OGLE RRLs ( ab type) were associated by koposov18 to this galaxy: OGLE-SMCRRLYR-6316 and OGLE-SMC-RRLYR-6325, with periods of 0.67 and 0.73 d. In this work, we recover the two OGLE stars, and find two additional RRL members for Hyd I (Figure fig:HydrusI ).
