We also noticed that the model magnitudes are significantly brighter ( 0.4 mag in g ) than the Petrosian magnitudes. We thus performed our own aperture photometry of the galaxy in the SDSS images with the phot task in IRAF, using a local background (measured at the same radius from the bright star as the galaxy) and removing the contribution of the foreground stars. We obtained magnitudes in g , , and i that are within a few hundreths of a magnitude of the model magnitudes for the galaxy, so we have adopted the latter for our calculations.

gmw_00010065.name: J1109+6123
gmw_00010065.overview: J1109+6123 is a low-mass spiral galaxy [arXiv:1408.4451] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1408.4451, arXiv:2103.06289]. The galaxy is dominated by its disk at nearly all radii, except for a central point source and a small, faint inner Sersic component [arXiv:2103.06289].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1109+6123 as a known low-mass spiral AGN host overlapping the Barth et al. IMBH sample and notes agreement with revised stellar-mass estimates.

quotes.quote: The other known AGN is J1109+6123, a low-mass spiral galaxy that was included in the IMBH survey of Barth et al. \ (2008) and examined in detail by Thornton et al. \ (2009).

J1109+6123 : As mentioned above, this is the one galaxy that is common to our sample and that of Barth et al. \ (2008). Note that because our methods for estimating distances, stellar masses, and emission-line luminosities are not identical, the parameters listed in Tables 1 and 2 differ somewhat with those published by Barth et al. Our value of M_ agrees closely with the revised stellar mass computed by Thornton et al. \ (2009).

sources.summary: The paper highlights RGG 15 as disk-dominated at nearly all radii, with only a small faint inner Sersic component and a central PSF.

quotes.quote: Additionally, RGG 15 is notable for being completely dominated by the disk at all radii except for the central PSF (see Figure fig:appendix2 in Appendix), although we do detect a small and faint inner S e rsic component with n=0.5 . The half-light radius of the inner component is only 0.3 kpc, compared to 2 kpc for the disk. The inner component is also 3.5 magnitudes fainter than the disk of this galaxy.

gmw_00010066.name: J1207+4307
gmw_00010066.overview: J1207+4307, also known as NGC 4117, is a low-mass Seyfert galaxy powered by black-hole accretion [arXiv:1408.4451]. The galaxy exhibits weak He II emission, strong [O I] emission, and high X-ray absorption that places it on the boundary between Seyferts and LINERs [arXiv:1408.4451].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1207+4307/NGC 4117 as a low-mass Seyfert with weak He II, strong [O I], X-ray absorption, and clear black-hole accretion.

quotes.quote: J1207+4307 : Also known as NGC 4117, this object was identified early on as a Seyfert galaxy (Huchra et al. \ 1982) despite having a rather low (compared to classical Seyferts) [ N 2 ] /H ratio. In our sample, it has the weakest detected He 2 emission, but its [ O 1 ] strength (relative to H ) is second only to that of NGC 4395. In fact, on the [ O 1 ] /H plot in Figure 5, NGC 4117 falls on the boundary between Seyferts and LINERs proposed by Kewley et al. \ (2006). It is, however, a bright X-ray source with an absorption column density ( N_ H = 4 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 ; Cardamone et al. \ 2007) similar to that commonly observed in the X-ray spectra of luminous type 2 Seyfert nuclei. Thus, there is no question that this object is powered by black-hole accretion.

gmw_00010067.name: J1225+0519
gmw_00010067.overview: J1225+0519 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy that formally falls within the coordinate boundaries and velocity range of the Virgo cluster [arXiv:1408.4451]. However, it is likely located behind the cluster at an infall-corrected distance of 33.4 Mpc [arXiv:1408.4451].

sources.summary: The paper discusses whether J1225+0519/VCC 764 belongs to Virgo and adopts a behind-Virgo distance that makes its luminosity and host properties consistent with the AGN sample.

quotes.quote: J1225+0519 : This galaxy formally falls within the coordinate boundaries and velocity range often used to define membership in the Virgo cluster (Mould et al. \ 2000). Hence, it is also known as VCC 764. However, if the galaxy were in the cluster and at a distance of 14 Mpc, it would have by far the faintest absolute magnitude and lowest stellar mass of any object our AGN sample. Given that the SDSS redshift is close to the upper limit assumed for Virgo membership, we considered the possibility that the galaxy is instead behind Virgo and computed its infall-corrected distance accordingly. This distance, 33.4 Mpc, implies a nuclear luminosity and host-galaxy properties that are much more in line with the rest of our sample, so we have adopted it here.

gmw_00010068.name: J1448+1227
gmw_00010068.overview: J1448+1227, also known as NGC 5762, is a star-forming galaxy with a blue disk studded with H II regions [arXiv:1408.4451]. Its nuclear spectrum is dominated by an older stellar population and exhibits emission-line flux ratios characteristic of a type 2 Seyfert [arXiv:1408.4451].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1448+1227/NGC 5762 as a previously studied H I and star-forming galaxy whose SDSS nuclear spectrum clearly has type 2 Seyfert line ratios.

quotes.quote: J1448+1227 : This galaxy, also known as NGC 5762, has been studied because of its 21 cm neutral hydrogen emission (Lewis et al. \ 1985; Rosenberg et al. \ 2000) and active star-formation (e.g., Kewley et al. \ 2002; note its blue, H 2 region-studded disk in Fig. 4). Interestingly, little attention has been given to its nuclear properties. The continuum in the SDSS spectrum is dominated by light from an older stellar population, and the emission-line flux ratios are clearly those of a type 2 Seyfert.

gmw_00010069.name: J1605+0850
gmw_00010069.overview: The galaxy J1605+0850 has a total optical extent of approximately 12 arcseconds [arXiv:1408.4451]. It is located within 1 arcminute of a bright foreground star, which may have affected its sky subtraction in early SDSS data releases [arXiv:1408.4451].

sources.summary: The paper explains that J1605+0850's SDSS DR7 magnitudes undercount its optical extent near a foreground star, so DR8 Petrosian magnitudes were adopted.

quotes.quote: J1605+0850 : Although the DR7 Petrosian and model magnitudes for this galaxy are similar, the radius of the region used for the aperture photometry is much smaller than its total optical extent ( 4'' vs. \ 12'' ), meaning all of the DR7 magnitudes are too faint. As with J0948+0958, this galaxy lies within 1' of a bright foreground star, which may have affected the sky subtraction. Regardless, the problem seems to have been corrected in the DR8 --- our manually measured magnitudes (following the method described above for J0948+0958) agree well with the DR8 Petrosian magnitudes, so we have adopted the latter for our analysis.

gmw_00010070.name: J100131.5
gmw_00010070.overview: J100131.5 is a galaxy in Group 1 with a highly disturbed optical morphology [arXiv:1811.12405]. It is a strong candidate for a late-stage merger, as a common neutral hydrogen envelope appears to trace both J100131.5 and a nearby candidate galaxy [arXiv:1811.12405].

sources.summary: The paper treats J100131.5 as a newly spectroscopically confirmed Group 1 member with disturbed optical morphology and a common H I envelope, making it a late-stage merger candidate.

quotes.quote: The two galaxies without extended emission are J100120.3 (Figure mom0a b) and J100131.5 (Figure mom0a d). The former is only very marginally resolved, and has an \ distribution that does not extend beyond the stellar disk. One possibility is that this galaxy has a truncated \ disk, because it has been living in Group 1 for a longer period of time and undergone multiple gravitational interactions or has had the gas in the outer disk removed by the intragroup medium. The latter has a very disturbed optical morphology and may be currently undergoing a merger. It is well resolved in . Indeed, we note that J100131.5 (Figure mom0a d) is actually detected as two galaxies in the COSMOS 20k and G10/COSMOS catalogues.

It could be two galaxies observed in projection, however, given that the optical morphology of the main galaxy at z_ spec = 0.121 looks disturbed; that the photometric redshift of the other galaxy candidate is very close ( z_ phot =0.13 ); that the \ appears to trace a common envelope around them; that the peak of the HI intensity map falls on the object which only has a photometric redshift; and that the integrated profile of the \ envelope covering both sources is very narrow, we believe this is an excellent candidate for a late stage merger within the group.

gmw_00010071.name: J100053.1
gmw_00010071.overview: The galaxy J100053.1 is located roughly 660 kiloparsecs further away than the brightest group galaxy of Group 300, suggesting it is not actually a member of that group [arXiv:1811.12405]. It exhibits asymmetric structure, including continuous low-level H I emission to the north [arXiv:1811.12405].

sources.summary: The paper finds J100053.1 likely is not a Group 300 member, places it farther than the BGG, and notes asymmetric low-level H I emission.

quotes.quote: However we measure an \ redshift for J100053.1 that suggests it is not in fact a group member. In the original Knobel 20k catalog, this galaxy had a PA of 0.85. The \ redshift puts it roughly 660 \ further away from the BGG. The two roughly face-on galaxies in Figure mom0c both have \ extending to the south, but since the galaxies are separated by 2000 , it is unlikely they have interacted with one another. The last member of the group is more than an order of magnitude smaller in stellar mass, and lies to the north outside of the image. While J100053.1 appears to be quite asymmetric, continuous low level emission to the north is visible by-eye in the data cube but is not picked up by the source finder.

gmw_00010072.name: J100119.9
gmw_00010072.overview: J100119.9 is a large face-on spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.17 [arXiv:1811.12405]. It is comparatively gas-poor for its color and features a northern hydrogen gas cloud that extends from its eastern spiral arm [arXiv:1811.12405].

sources.summary: The paper describes J100119.9 as a large face-on spiral probably outside Group 618, comparatively gas-poor for its color, with a northern H I cloud continuing an eastern spiral arm.

quotes.quote: Meanwhile J100119.9+022310.1, the large face-on galaxy at z=0.17 (top right of Figure mom0d ), appears comparatively gas poor for its color. The northern gas cloud is a continuation of the eastern spiral arm, but deeper observations are likely to reveal even more emission to the south that was too faint to be well characterized by the SoFiA source finder.

The photometric candidate is a large face-on spiral (J100119.9), but its \ redshift strongly suggests it does not belong to the group as it differs by z=0.003 ( 900 ) from the other two members. The galaxy has an \ cloud to the north that is an extension of the eastern spiral arm, and deeper observations will likely reveal more \ emission to the south that was too faint to be picked up by the source finder.

gmw_00010073.name: J100121.9
gmw_00010073.overview: J100121.9 is a very small optical galaxy with an extended neutral hydrogen disk and a tail extending to the southwest [arXiv:1811.12405]. It is gas-rich and exhibits a high star formation rate for its stellar mass, which may be fueled by a large gas reservoir [arXiv:1811.12405].

sources.summary: The paper describes J100121.9 as a small optical galaxy with extended H I, high star formation for its mass, and a possibly large gas reservoir fueling activity.

quotes.quote: J100121.9+022246.8 (left of Figure mom0d ) is a very small optical galaxy with an \ tail extending to the southwest. The tail is detected at low significance and, as discussed in Section membership , the total \ mass of the galaxy may be overestimated. However, it is also interesting to note that this galaxy has a high star formation rate for its stellar mass compared (Figure span ) to the rest of the \ detections. This star formation rate may be fueled by a large \ reservoir.

J100121.9 is a very small optical galaxy, which shows an extension to the southwest towards the undetected group member. Even after taking into account beam-smearing, it appears that the \ presumed to be in the disk of the galaxy is quite extended with respect to the optical disk. As mentioned in Section props , it is quite gas-rich relative to ALFALFA and to the rest of this CHILES sample. We may be overestimating the \ mass, or it may in fact have a large gas reservoir fueling its star formation.

gmw_00010074.name: J100123.2
gmw_00010074.overview: J100123.2 is a disturbed galaxy that is not part of a group [arXiv:1811.12405]. It has likely undergone an interaction with a close, edge-on companion galaxy that is separated by 353 km/s in velocity, which has pulled gas from one of the systems [arXiv:1811.12405].

sources.summary: The paper presents J100123.2 as a disturbed non-group galaxy whose H I morphology suggests interaction with a close edge-on companion, despite the pair falling outside group definitions.

quotes.quote: J100123.2 (Figure mom0b b) has an edge-on companion very close in projection. In this case, the spatial separation is 6 arcseconds, but the velocity separation is 353 . While outside the definition of a group or pair in the Knobel catalogue, the \ morphology suggests that they have undergone an interaction in which the gas is being pulled from one of the galaxies, while the other was undetected in \ at our current limits. The \ we detect in this galaxy is only very marginally unresolved, and we may only be seeing the highest density gas in the system.

Of the remaining two galaxies J100123.2 ((b) in Figures mom0b and disperse ) was shown to have likely undergone an interaction with an edge-on companion 353 \ away from its current position, so is not particularly isolated.

gmw_00010075.name: J100120.5
gmw_00010075.overview: J100120.5 is a massive, well-resolved non-group galaxy [arXiv:1811.12405]. It exhibits a disturbed neutral hydrogen morphology, which may be the result of a recent tidal interaction with the nearby galaxy J100116.7 [arXiv:1811.12405].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J100120.5 as a massive, well-resolved non-group galaxy whose disturbed H I morphology may reflect a recent tidal interaction with J100116.7.

quotes.quote: Figure mom0e shows two more non-group galaxies with disturbed morphologies, J100120.5 and J100116.7. Both are massive with 10^ 9.9 and 10^ 10.4 \ of , respectively, and they are both well resolved in the \ observations. The galaxies are separated by approximately 1.3 arcmin ( 170 kpc) and 420 \ (Figure mom0e ). Again, the velocity difference is too great to be considered a pair in the group catalogue. However, based on their positions and relative velocities, they may have undergone an interaction in the last 40 Myr, which may explain their \ morphologies if they underwent a tidal interaction leading to tidal tails on opposite sides of both galaxies (e.g. Toomre72,Hibbard96 ).

gmw_00010076.name: J100116.7
gmw_00010076.overview: J100116.7 is a massive galaxy with a disturbed morphology [arXiv:1811.12405]. It features a southern tidal tail with a faint, continuous stellar component, which may indicate a recent tidal interaction with the nearby galaxy J100120.5 [arXiv:1811.12405].

sources.summary: The paper discusses J100116.7 with J100120.5 as a massive, well-resolved system whose H I disturbance and southern tidal tail may trace a recent tidal encounter.

quotes.quote: Figure mom0e shows two more non-group galaxies with disturbed morphologies, J100120.5 and J100116.7. Both are massive with 10^ 9.9 and 10^ 10.4 \ of , respectively, and they are both well resolved in the \ observations. The galaxies are separated by approximately 1.3 arcmin ( 170 kpc) and 420 \ (Figure mom0e ).

Again, the velocity difference is too great to be considered a pair in the group catalogue. However, based on their positions and relative velocities, they may have undergone an interaction in the last 40 Myr, which may explain their \ morphologies if they underwent a tidal interaction leading to tidal tails on opposite sides of both galaxies (e.g. Toomre72,Hibbard96 ). The tidal tail south of J100116.7 has a faint stellar component which is continuous along the length of the tail.

gmw_00010077.name: J100157.4
gmw_00010077.overview: J100157.4 is a highly asymmetric non-group galaxy with a stellar distribution that shows asymmetry extending to the southeast [arXiv:1811.12405]. Its marginally resolved neutral hydrogen disk also extends in this same direction, though no clear candidate for a recent tidal interaction has been identified [arXiv:1811.12405].

sources.summary: The paper describes J100157.4 as a highly asymmetric non-group galaxy whose H I and stellar disk extend along a filament, though the detection is marginally resolved.

quotes.quote: The fourth galaxy to show extreme asymmetry is J100157.4 (Figure mom0b ). Interestingly, the stellar distribution shows asymmetry in the same direction as the \ with a lower surface brightness component extending to the southeast, relative to the bright central disk. We caution however that the \ is only marginally resolved and the clean beam is elongated in a similar orientation to the galaxy. There are several galaxies seen close in projection, but they appear to belong to background collections at z 0.346 or z 0.526 . Unfortunately, we have not identified a clear candidate for a recent tidal interaction.

gmw_00010078.name: J100111.6
gmw_00010078.overview: J100111.6 is an isolated galaxy that features an extended, relatively symmetric distribution of neutral hydrogen [arXiv:1811.12405]. It exhibits a low star formation rate and a high gas-to-stellar mass fraction, suggesting its evolution is regulated by secular processes [arXiv:1811.12405].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J100111.6 as the most isolated CHILES detection, with extended symmetric H I, low star formation, high gas fraction, and likely secular evolution.

quotes.quote: Other non-group galaxies --- Of the remaining two non-group members, we note that J100111.6 in particular has quite an extended \ envelope compared to the optical extent that is visible in the HST image (Figure mom0b a). As we will show in Section hifilament , it is also the most isolated of our \ detections.

Finally, J100111.6 ((a) in Figures mom0b and disperse ) remains as the most isolated galaxy in the sample. Interestingly, it also shows the most extended, and relatively symmetric \ distribution of the sample. It has a low star formation rate relative to other \ detections at z=0.11 -0.12, and a high relative to-stellar mass fraction, falling just above the 1 range from ALFALFA (Figure span a). Taken together, this galaxy appears to be less disturbed and producing stars more passively than galaxies in the wall. Its evolution may be regulated by secular rather than environmental processes.

gmw_00010079.name: J100209.4
gmw_00010079.overview: J100209.4 is a massive, non-group galaxy with a strong H I detection [arXiv:1811.12405]. It features resolved major-axis structure and a hint of low-level emission extending beyond its stellar disk [arXiv:1811.12405].

sources.summary: The paper describes J100209.4 as a massive non-group galaxy with strong H I detection, possible low-level emission beyond its stellar disk, and resolved major-axis structure.

quotes.quote: Finally, J100209.4 (Figure mom0b d) is massive in both its \ and stellar components, but not unusually so relative to other \ detected samples. It is a strong \ detection with a hint of low level emission outside the stellar disk. Both these non-group galaxies are resolved with 3 beams across the major axis.

gmw_00010080.name: SDSS J084828.13+220333.4
gmw_00010080.overview: SDSS J084828.13+220333.4 is a quiescent, barred lenticular galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.07897 [arXiv:1809.07446, arXiv:2002.02498]. It features a central bulge containing a supermassive black hole, a blue outer disk with northern blue clumps, and no signs of recent star formation or active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1809.07446, arXiv:2002.02498].

sources.summary: The host of iPTF15af is a quiescent SDSS galaxy at z=0.07897 with substantial bulge mass, a black-hole mass estimate, low current star formation, and evidence against recent AGN activity.

quotes.quote: The transient's host galaxy SDSS J084828.13+220333.4 is located inside of the footprint of SDSS DR12 SDSSDR12 . An archival spectrum of the host was obtained on MJD 53379 as part of the SDSS spectroscopic survey. We do not identify major lines associated with star formation or AGNs. The spectroscopic redshift of the galaxy is z=0.07897 0.00004 , which corresponds to a luminosity distance D_L=358 Mpc (distance modulus =37.8 mag).

The stellar mass for the host and its bulge, log (M_ * / M _ )=10.360^ +0.099 _ 0.138 and log (M_ b / M _ ) = 9.99_ 0.15 ^ +0.12 , show that 40 of the mass is concentrated in the bulge of the galaxy Mendel2014ApJS . Galaxies with enhanced stellar density cores are common among TDE hosts Graur2018ApJ , showing unusually high B/T values Law-Smith2017ApJ . High-resolution spectroscopy of the galaxy has allowed us to measure the velocity dispersion in the host, leading to an M- estimate of log _ 10 (M_ BH / M _ ) = 6.88^ 0.38 _ 0.38 Wevers2017MNRAS . The low specific star formation rate (sSFR) of the host, log _ 10 ( sSFR/yr )=-14.87^ +3.55 _ 0.10 Chang2015ApJS shows that at the present time the galaxy is not actively forming stars.
Based on the lack of AGN features in the spectrum, the lack of variability, and no mid-IR excess, we can rule out previous AGN activity in the host at least 10 yr before the discovery of the source.

sources.summary: The iPTF15af host is a barred lenticular quiescent Balmer-strong galaxy with a blue outer disk, northern blue clumps, and a high profile index.

quotes.quote: The host galaxies of iPTF15af and PTF09ge are barred lenticular galaxies; both have blue disk components in their outskirts in addition to bright central bulges.
The host of iPTF15af has a series of blue clumps in its outer disk to the north.

The host galaxy of iPTF15af also has a high index, higher than typical early type galaxies and consistent with post-starbursts; the other quiescent Balmer-strong host of ASASSN-14ae has a index consistent with early type galaxies.

gmw_00010081.name: AGC 111164
gmw_00010081.overview: AGC 111164 is a low surface brightness dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:0808.1789] located in a void [arXiv:2105.05100]. It is paired with the more massive neighbor NGC 784 at a separation of 140 kpc, where mutual gravitational interactions may affect their evolution [arXiv:2105.05100].

sources.summary: The paper describes AGC 111164 as a dwarf irregular with a central HII region, an old dominant stellar component, and a recent burst about 1 Myr ago.

quotes.quote: AGC 111164: this dIrr galaxy shows one central HII region with F(H )=6.72 10^ 15 erg/cm ^ 2 /s, which is 68 of the total line flux measured from this galaxy. The model predictions indicate that the first SF burst took place more than 10 Gyr ago contributing 65 10 of the present starlight, while the second burst took place 1 Myr ago. The blue image shows a low surface brightness disk with a definite brighter star-like center. No traces of spiral structure are seen, but it is possible that the disk has other, fainter, condensations.

sources.summary: AGC 111164 is described as a SHIELD dwarf in a void, paired with the more massive NGC 784 at 140 kpc, where mutual gravitational effects may matter.

quotes.quote: AGC 111164 appears to be paired with a slightly more massive neighbor and resides in a void Pustilnik2016 .

One SHIELD galaxy is within 200 kpc of its nearest neighbors and warrants additional discussion. AGC 111164 is located 140 kpc from NGC 784, the main galaxy in one of the aforementioned groups. NGC 784 is a starburst dwarf galaxy with a stellar mass of 6 10^8 \ McQuinn2010b . At this 3D physical separation, the evolution of each of the systems in this dwarf galaxy pair may be impacted by the gravitational presence of the other.

gmw_00010082.name: 3C 59
gmw_00010082.overview: The radio galaxy 3C 59 is classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1003.5700]. It exhibits broad, double-peaked hydrogen emission lines that display long-term variability driven by discrete lumps of excess emission [arXiv:1003.5700].

sources.summary: The paper monitors 3C 59 as one of seven double-peaked AGNs, finding broad H emission-line variability driven by excess-emission lumps that change shape and drift over years.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present results for seven objects whose long-term variability has not been studied in any detail: 3C 59, 1E 0450.3--1817, Pictor A, CBS 74, PKS 0921--213, PKS 1020--103, and PKS 1739+18C.
The primary goal of this paper is to characterize the observed variability patterns in these seven objects in a model-independent way.

In this paper we have characterized, in a model-independent way, the variability of the broad, double-peaked H emission lines in seven objects (Pictor A, PKS 0921--213, 1E 0450.3--1817, CBS 74, 3C 59, PKS 1739+18, and PKS 1020--103) which have been monitored over the past decade. As indicated by the rms profiles, the greatest variability occurs at larger projected velocities, and in particular many objects show a peak in the rms profile at large negative projected velocities. Difference spectra showed that the variability is caused primarily by discrete lumps of excess emission that change in morphology and amplitude on timescales of a few years and drift in projected velocity on longer timescales.

gmw_00010083.name: 1E 0450.3--1817
gmw_00010083.overview: The active galactic nucleus 1E 0450.3--1817 is characterized by broad, double-peaked H̑ emission lines [arXiv:1003.5700]. Long-term monitoring reveals that these lines exhibit variability driven by discrete lumps of excess emission that change shape and drift in velocity over several years [arXiv:1003.5700].

sources.summary: The paper monitors 1E 0450.3--1817 as one of seven double-peaked AGNs, finding broad H emission-line variability driven by excess-emission lumps that change shape and drift over years.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present results for seven objects whose long-term variability has not been studied in any detail: 3C 59, 1E 0450.3--1817, Pictor A, CBS 74, PKS 0921--213, PKS 1020--103, and PKS 1739+18C.
The primary goal of this paper is to characterize the observed variability patterns in these seven objects in a model-independent way.

In this paper we have characterized, in a model-independent way, the variability of the broad, double-peaked H emission lines in seven objects (Pictor A, PKS 0921--213, 1E 0450.3--1817, CBS 74, 3C 59, PKS 1739+18, and PKS 1020--103) which have been monitored over the past decade. As indicated by the rms profiles, the greatest variability occurs at larger projected velocities, and in particular many objects show a peak in the rms profile at large negative projected velocities. Difference spectra showed that the variability is caused primarily by discrete lumps of excess emission that change in morphology and amplitude on timescales of a few years and drift in projected velocity on longer timescales.

gmw_00010084.name: PKS 0921--213
gmw_00010084.overview: PKS 0921--213 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1003.5700] that exhibits broad, double-peaked H-alpha emission lines [arXiv:1003.5700]. These emission lines display long-term variability driven by discrete lumps of excess emission that change in morphology and drift in velocity over several years [arXiv:1003.5700].

sources.summary: The paper monitors PKS 0921--213 as one of seven double-peaked AGNs, finding broad H emission-line variability driven by excess-emission lumps that change shape and drift over years.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present results for seven objects whose long-term variability has not been studied in any detail: 3C 59, 1E 0450.3--1817, Pictor A, CBS 74, PKS 0921--213, PKS 1020--103, and PKS 1739+18C.
The primary goal of this paper is to characterize the observed variability patterns in these seven objects in a model-independent way.

In this paper we have characterized, in a model-independent way, the variability of the broad, double-peaked H emission lines in seven objects (Pictor A, PKS 0921--213, 1E 0450.3--1817, CBS 74, 3C 59, PKS 1739+18, and PKS 1020--103) which have been monitored over the past decade. As indicated by the rms profiles, the greatest variability occurs at larger projected velocities, and in particular many objects show a peak in the rms profile at large negative projected velocities. Difference spectra showed that the variability is caused primarily by discrete lumps of excess emission that change in morphology and amplitude on timescales of a few years and drift in projected velocity on longer timescales.

gmw_00010085.name: PKS 1020--103
gmw_00010085.overview: PKS 1020--103 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1003.5700] that exhibits broad, double-peaked H̸ emission lines [arXiv:1003.5700]. This emission displays long-term variability driven by discrete lumps of excess emission that change shape and drift in velocity over several years [arXiv:1003.5700].

sources.summary: The paper monitors PKS 1020--103 as one of seven double-peaked AGNs, finding broad H emission-line variability driven by excess-emission lumps that change shape and drift over years.

The primary goal of this paper is to characterize the observed variability patterns in these seven objects in a model-independent way.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1020 103 as a polarization outlier with a very high fitted synchrotron fraction but little optical polarization, possibly because line contamination bends its continuum.

quotes.quote: Firstly, 1020 103 has a very high synchrotron fraction ( 95 ), but has very little optical polarisation ( P=0.58 ). In this case, the source has a power law continuum with a slight curvature, which is fit well by an almost pure synchrotron curve. It may be that this curvature is due to other effects than synchrotron, which explains the lack of polarisation. A possible candidate is contamination from the very strong line, which would boost the continuum level in the centre of the SED.

gmw_00010086.name: PKS 1739+18C
gmw_00010086.overview: PKS 1739+18C is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1003.5700] that exhibits broad, double-peaked H̑ emission lines [arXiv:1003.5700]. Long-term monitoring reveals that these emission lines vary over timescales of a few years due to changing lumps of excess emission [arXiv:1003.5700].

sources.summary: The paper monitors PKS 1739+18C as one of seven double-peaked AGNs, finding broad H emission-line variability driven by excess-emission lumps that change shape and drift over years.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present results for seven objects whose long-term variability has not been studied in any detail: 3C 59, 1E 0450.3--1817, Pictor A, CBS 74, PKS 0921--213, PKS 1020--103, and PKS 1739+18C.
The primary goal of this paper is to characterize the observed variability patterns in these seven objects in a model-independent way.

In this paper we have characterized, in a model-independent way, the variability of the broad, double-peaked H emission lines in seven objects (Pictor A, PKS 0921--213, 1E 0450.3--1817, CBS 74, 3C 59, PKS 1739+18, and PKS 1020--103) which have been monitored over the past decade. As indicated by the rms profiles, the greatest variability occurs at larger projected velocities, and in particular many objects show a peak in the rms profile at large negative projected velocities. Difference spectra showed that the variability is caused primarily by discrete lumps of excess emission that change in morphology and amplitude on timescales of a few years and drift in projected velocity on longer timescales.

gmw_00010087.name: J0108 0400
gmw_00010087.overview: The active galaxy J0108 0400 exhibits a 42-parsec offset between its VLBA and Gaia positions [arXiv:2307.06363]. This spatial offset is most likely caused by a small-scale radio jet with a steep-spectrum diffuse lobe structure [arXiv:2307.06363].

sources.summary: The paper finds a 42 pc VLBA-Gaia offset in J0108 0400 and attributes it most likely to a small-scale radio jet with a steep-spectrum diffuse lobe.

quotes.quote: J0108 0400 exhibits a 5.0 0.5 mas (corresponding to 42 pc) VLBA-Gaia offset. The steep spectral index ( _ C-X =-1.57 ) of the radio component and its extension toward the Gaia position suggest the presence of a diffuse lobe structure. Therefore, the offset is most likely caused by a small-scale radio jet.

gmw_00010088.name: J1044+2959
gmw_00010088.overview: J1044+2959 is a candidate dual quasar system [arXiv:2307.06363]. It exhibits a 25-parsec offset between its Gaia optical position and its compact, flat-spectrum VLBA radio component [arXiv:2307.06363].

sources.summary: The paper treats J1044+2959 as a candidate dual quasar, with a 25 pc VLBA-Gaia offset, compact flat-spectrum radio component, and optical-radio centroid explanation.

quotes.quote: J1044+2959 shows a 3.2 0.5 mas (corresponding to 25 pc) VLBA-Gaia offset. The radio component is compact and has a flat spectral index of 0.13, suggesting a self-absorbed core. J1044+2959 is likely a candidate dual quasar, which could explain the offset if one nucleus is radio-bright but the other is optically bright (Panel b, Figure fig:cartoon ). Alternatively, both quasars could be optically bright, but only one is radio-bright (Panel a, Figure fig:cartoon ), resulting in an offset between the light centroid of the total optical flux from the radio centroid.

gmw_00010089.name: J1051+2119
gmw_00010089.overview: The active galactic nucleus J1051+2119 exhibits a consistent position between its VLBA and Gaia observations [arXiv:2307.06363]. It features a secondary eastern radio component with an elongated structure connecting to the primary source, which suggests the presence of a small-scale jet [arXiv:2307.06363].

sources.summary: The paper finds J1051+2119 aligned between VLBA and Gaia but showing a second eastern radio component whose elongated connection suggests a small-scale jet.

quotes.quote: J1051+2119 shows a consistent position between VLBA and Gaia, but it presents a second radio component located toward the east. The second component shows an elongated structure connecting to the primary component, which suggests that it is a small-scale jet. To confirm the jet scenario, a spectral index analysis derived from multi-band images is necessary, though we only have an archival C-band image.

gmw_00010090.name: J1259+5140
gmw_00010090.overview: The active galactic nucleus J1259+5140 exhibits a consistent position between its VLBA and Gaia observations [arXiv:2307.06363]. It features multiple western radio components whose configuration suggests the presence of a small-scale jet [arXiv:2307.06363].

sources.summary: The paper finds J1259+5140 has multiple western radio components despite VLBA-Gaia positional agreement, interpreting their configuration as a likely small-scale jet.

quotes.quote: J1259+5140 shows a consistent position between VLBA and Gaia, but it presents several radio components positioned towards the west. The configuration of these components aligns with the direction of the small VLBA-Gaia offsets, indicating that they may constitute a small-scale jet. Nevertheless, confirming the jet hypothesis requires spectral index analysis derived from multi-band images, and we only have an archival C-band image.

gmw_00010091.name: J1557+1236
gmw_00010091.overview: The active galactic nucleus J1557+1236 exhibits a VLBA-Gaia offset of 62.0 milliarcseconds, which corresponds to a physical distance of 485 parsecs [arXiv:2307.06363]. Its radio component is weakly detected at approximately 6 sigma and shows a slightly extended structure [arXiv:2307.06363].

sources.summary: The paper reports a significant 485 pc VLBA-Gaia offset in J1557+1236, but with only a weak, slightly extended radio detection the offset origin remains unclear.

quotes.quote: J1557+1236 exhibits a significant VLBA-Gaia offset of 62.0 1.2 mas (corresponding to 485 pc). The radio component is a weak detection at around 6 and shows slight extension. Without deeper images or additional images in other bands, the origin of the offset is unclear.

gmw_00010092.overview: 0217-0052 is classified as a steep-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1505.03394]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations reveal a dual structure consisting of a probable core and a western radio jet, with missing flux density indicating the presence of more extended emission [arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: 0217-0052 shows dual VLBI structure with a probable core and western radio jet; missing 5 GHz flux suggests extended emission, and the source is classified as a steep-spectrum radio quasar.

quotes.quote: 0217-0052 . Source on both 5- and 8.4-GHz maps has a dual structure. Component C with a spectral index ^ 5 _ 8.4 = 0.32 is probably a core. Western component is likely to be a radio-jet. Our 5 GHz image account only for a 35 of the total flux density reported in the single dish observations, what implies the existence of more extended emission resolved out during the observations. guai classified this object as a steep spectrum radio quasar (SSRQ).

gmw_00010093.overview: The radio source 0756+3714 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1304.3021, arXiv:1505.03394]. High-resolution observations resolve the source into multiple components [arXiv:1304.3021, arXiv:1505.03394], which are interpreted as hot spots in the mini-lobes of a young radio source [arXiv:1304.3021, arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: The paper resolves 0756+37 into two similar VLBA components, interprets them as hot spots in mini-lobes of a young radio source, and notes possible unsampled emission.

quotes.quote: 0756+37 In both VLBA bands we resolve the radio source into two components, separated by 1.8 mas ( 15 pc at the redshift of the object) at the highest resolution, in p.a. 60^ . Both components have similar sizes and spectral indexes ( 0.38 0.27 for A and 0.37 0.27 for B). Since the overall spectrum peaks at 2.5 GHz (8.7 GHz rest frame), and it is steep in the optically thin (power-law, not self-absorbed) part of the emission, we can infer that we have detected two hot spots in the mini-lobes of a typically young radio source.

The VLBA can account for a fraction of the total flux density measured in earlier VLA observations ( 65 at 5 GHz and 85 at 8.4 GHz): considering the variability significance as defined by , the discrepancy is significant at 5 GHz, with a _ var =7.0, while at 8 GHz we obtain _ var <3 . The time interval between VLA and VLBA observations is about seven months. Thus, either the source is variable -- but this would be inconsistent with the hot-spot scenario -- or, more likely, there is some emission on angular scales not sampled by the VLBA (tens of mas or more).

sources.summary: 0756+3714 is unresolved at 1.7 GHz but has three components at higher frequencies, a bright C2 component, double-morphology interpretations, and an SED peak suggesting youth.

quotes.quote: 0756+3714 . This source was observed with the VLBA at 5 GHz as part of the VIPS project helm07 . Our 1.7-GHz EVN observations did not resolve the object. The 5-GHz VIPS image and our 8.4-GHz observations show this source has three components, with C2 ( ^ 5 _ 8.4 = 0.68) being the brightest. Previous 5-GHz VLBA observations of this source made by bruni13 suggest double morphology, which they interpret as two flat spectrum hot spots in the mini-lobes of a young radio source. Spectral energy distribution (SED) of 0756+3714 modelled between 408 MHz and 8.4 GHz peaks at 2.5 GHz, which suggest a young age.

gmw_00010094.overview: 0815+3305 is an active galactic nucleus classified as an absorption-line quasar [arXiv:1505.03394]. Its radio structure is resolved into two main components separated by 6 kpc, showing a steep, non-peaked spectrum and possible bridge emission [arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: 0815+3305 is the largest AI quasar in the sample, with two 6 kpc-separated components, possible bridge emission, additional unresolved structure, and a steep non-peaked spectrum.

quotes.quote: 0815+3305 . The map at 1.7 GHz shows a structure resolved into two main components, which are separated by 6 kpc. This source is the largest one in our whole sample of AI quasars. Both, the northern and southern parts consists of 3 components and are elongated in the direction which suggest there might be an extended bridge of emission between them. At 5 and 8.4-GHz only the more compact southern part is detected with components C1.2 or C1.3 being probably a radio core.

Indeed, the EVN and VLBA observations of 0815+3305 can account only for 43 and 20 of the total flux at 1.7 and 5 GHz respectively indicating the existence of additional structure on angular scales not sampled by the EVN/VLBA. The overall spectrum of 0815+3305 is steep and does not present a peak in the gigahertz range. The classification of this source is difficult and thus we marked it as 'Other' in Table observations .

gmw_00010095.overview: The radio source 1013+4918 is a quasar characterized by a core-jet morphology [arXiv:1505.03394]. It features an inverted-spectrum radio core and a one-sided jet with three northern steep-spectrum components, resulting in a steep overall spectrum [arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: 1013+4918 has VLBI radio structure consistent with VIPS imaging: an inverted-spectrum core, northern one-sided jet components, a steep overall spectrum, and core-jet morphology.

quotes.quote: 1013+4918 . The radio structure of 1013+4918 visible in our 1.7- and 8.4-GHz observations is consistent with that obtained in the VIPS project helm07 and we present all three images in Fig. images1 . The inverted-spectrum C1 component is a radio core and three northern steep spectrum components (N1, N2, N3) are parts of the one-sided radio jet. The overall spectrum of 1013+4918 is steep. Its morphology can be classified as core-jet.

gmw_00010096.overview: The source 1042+0748 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1505.03394]. It exhibits a complex radio structure at 5 GHz with a steep overall spectrum, making its morphological classification difficult [arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: 1042+0748 was observed at 5 GHz with complex radio structure, comparable integrated VLBA and single-dish flux, a steep spectrum, and difficult morphology classification.

quotes.quote: 1042+0748 . This source has been observed only at 5 GHz and it has been complex radio structure. Integrated flux density from the 5-GHz VLBA image is comparable with single dish measurement (Table basic ) and thus we have detected its whole structure. The overall spectrum of 1042+0748 is steep. The classification of this source is difficult and thus we marked it as 'Other' in Table observations .

gmw_00010097.overview: 1057+0324 is a core-jet radio source with a steep overall spectrum [arXiv:1505.03394]. Its structure features three C-band components, including a likely flat-spectrum radio core, though it exhibits a potential flux deficit that could stem from intrinsic variability [arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: 1057+0324 has three C-band components, a likely flat-spectrum core, missing 5 GHz flux, uncertain variability, a steep spectrum, and core-jet classification.

quotes.quote: 1057+0324 . This source has three components on the C-band map which are resolved out in 8.4-GHz observations. The flat spectrum component C1 is likely a radio core. The 5-GHz integrated flux density is significantly smaller ( 27 ) compared to a single dish observations (Table basic ). However, we are unable to unambiguously determine whether it is a result of luck of short spacings or due to intrinsic variability.
The overall spectrum of 1057+0324 is steep. Based on its structure and spectral index we classified this source as a core-jet.

gmw_00010098.overview: The source 1103+0232 is a quasar that has been observed at radio frequencies of 5 and 8.4 GHz [arXiv:1505.03394]. Its structure is classified as a core-jet morphology, consisting of a bright radio core and steep-spectrum components that likely form a radio jet [arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: 1103+0232 was observed at 5 and 8.4 GHz; its brightest component is a radio core, with steep-spectrum components forming a probable jet and core-jet morphology.

quotes.quote: 1103+0232 . This source was observed only at 5 and 8.4-GHz with VLBA (Fig. images2 ). The brightest component C1 is radio core and steep spectrum components C2 and C3 are probably parts of a radio jet. Its morphology can be classified as a core-jet.

gmw_00010099.overview: 1432+4103 is a core-jet radio source with a steep overall spectrum [arXiv:1505.03394]. Its double structure at 1.7 GHz resolves into a complex structure at higher frequencies, featuring a probable radio core and a one-sided jet [arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: 1432+4103 has double 1.7 GHz structure resolved into complex higher-frequency structure, a probable radio core, one-sided jet components, missing flux, and steep spectrum.

quotes.quote: 1432+4103 . This source posses double structure in the 1.7-GHz EVN map which has been resolved into complex structure in VLBA 5- and 8.4-GHz observations. Component C1 with the most flat spectrum of all detected parts is probably a radio core, the other components are parts of the one-sided jet. We lost ( 23 ) of the 1.7-GHz total flux density probably due to poor uv coverage. The overall spectrum of 1432+4103 is steep. We have classified this source as a core-jet.

gmw_00010100.name: UGC 3672
gmw_00010100.overview: UGC 3672 is a triplet of gas-rich dwarf galaxies located near the center of the Lynx-Cancer void [arXiv:1611.01271]. The system is embedded in a common neutral hydrogen envelope, with high-resolution observations revealing filamentary tidal tails and bridges that indicate merger activity [arXiv:1611.01271].

sources.summary: UGC 3672 is presented as a Lynx-Cancer void triplet of gas-rich dwarfs in a common H I envelope, with high-resolution data revealing tidal tails, bridges, and merger activity.

quotes.quote: We present \ and optical observations of UGC 3672 which is located near the centre of the nearby Lynx-Cancer void. We find that UGC 3672 consists of an approximately linearly aligned triplet of gas rich dwarfs with large scale velocity continuity along the triplet axis. The faintest component of the triplet is extremely gas-rich M _ HI /L_ B 17 ) and also extremely metal deficient ( 12+ (O/H) 7.0 ). The metallicity of this dwarf is close to the 'floor' observed in star forming galaxies. Low resolution \ images show that the galaxy triplet is located inside a common \ envelope, with fairly regular, disk like kinematics.

At high angular resolution however, the gas is found to be confined to several filamentary tidal tails and bridges. The linear alignment of the galaxies, along with the velocity continuity that we observe, is consistent with the galaxies lying along a filament. We argue that the location of this highly unusual system in an extremely low density environment is not a coincidence, but is a consequence of structure formation proceeding more slowly and also probing smaller scales than in regions with average density. Our observations also indicate that wet mergers of galaxies flowing along filaments is a possible pathway for the formation of gas rich disks.

gmw_00010101.name: UGC 3672A
gmw_00010101.overview: UGC 3672A is a faint, gas-rich companion dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1611.01271]. It is embedded in a common envelope and has been involved in tidal interactions that produced filamentary tidal tails [arXiv:1611.01271].

sources.summary: UGC 3672A is identified as a faint gas-rich companion dwarf embedded in UGC 3672's common envelope and implicated in tidal tails, though it is much less massive.

quotes.quote: This emission has also been identified as arising from a companion dwarf galaxy by Paper7 based on the spectra obtained with the SAO 6m telescope. As discussed further below, our \ data also supports this conclusion. We henceforth refer to this galaxy as component A or UGC 3672A.

Tidal interaction between these, as well as the third galaxy UGC 3672A has resulted in the filamentary structures, which are essentially tidal tails and arms. As we saw above, the star forming component D is associated with the intersection of two of these tidal tails. A possible alternate scenario is one where there are only two galaxies, viz. UGC 3672 and the companion UGC 3672A. In such a scenario one would require that the material that forms component B has been tidally stripped from the main body of UGC 3672 by the companion UGC 3672A. This seems an unlikely scenario given that UGC 3672A is significantly less massive than UGC 3672.

gmw_00010102.name: IRAS 23578-5307
gmw_00010102.overview: IRAS 23578-5307 is an ultraluminous infrared galaxy remnant that features tidal tails [arXiv:astro-ph/0607468]. Image deconvolution reveals that the system contains two nuclei separated by a projected distance of approximately 1.4 kiloparsecs [arXiv:astro-ph/0607468].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IRAS 23578-5307 as a ULIRG remnant with tidal tails and two nuclei separated by about 1.4 kpc after image deconvolution.

quotes.quote: For one of the remaining 2 sources, IRAS 23578-5307, the large elongation and the tidal tails indicate the possible presence of two components (see Fig. fig:onesource ; left panel). To investigate this possibility, we deconvolve our acquisition image with its point-spread-function (PSF) which has a FWHM of 4.1 pixels.

The deconvolution increases the resolution of the image by a factor of 2. The resulting image (see Fig. fig:onesource ; right panel) confirms the presence of a second nucleus and constrains its projected separation to be 1.4 0.4 kpc.

gmw_00010103.name: COSMOS 110446
gmw_00010103.overview: COSMOS 110446 is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 1.5 [arXiv:2109.10614]. It is classified as a rotating disk galaxy that exhibits a smooth, monotonic velocity gradient [arXiv:2109.10614].

sources.summary: The paper classifies COSMOS 110446 as a z~1.5 star-forming disk with smooth velocity gradients in both natural-seeing and adaptive-optics data, despite position-angle misalignment.

quotes.quote: We used the reduced datacubes of the galaxies COSMOS 110446, COSMOS 171407, COSMOS 130477, COSMOS 127977 and UDS 78317 from the KMOS Galaxy Evolution Survey (KGES; KGES_alfie ). This survey comprises a sample of 288 mass-selected bright ( K < 22.7 ) star-forming galaxies with known spectroscopic redshifts at z 1.5 in the ECDFS, UDS and COSMOS fields targeting their redshifted H and [ N ii ] emission lines.

COSMOS 110446 (disk): The data shows a smooth monotonic velocity gradient with consistent position angles in the two resolutions and a v/ =1.8 in the case of NS and v/ =2.2 in the case of the AO data. Despite a 30^ misalignment between the kinematic and photometric position angles, we classify this galaxy as a rotating disk as it fulfils the rest of the requirements listed in Section subsection:Kinematic state of the galaxies .

gmw_00010104.name: COSMOS 171407
gmw_00010104.overview: COSMOS 171407 is a star-forming galaxy at a redshift of approximately 1.5 [arXiv:2109.10614]. Although it is classified as a minor merger with a complex H-alpha intensity distribution, it is treated as a disk galaxy due to its ordered rotation [arXiv:2109.10614].

sources.summary: The paper treats COSMOS 171407 as a disk for angular-momentum analysis, while noting adaptive-optics clumps and kinematic complexity consistent with a minor merger.

quotes.quote: We used the reduced datacubes of the galaxies COSMOS 110446, COSMOS 171407, COSMOS 130477, COSMOS 127977 and UDS 78317 from the KMOS Galaxy Evolution Survey (KGES; KGES_alfie ). This survey comprises a sample of 288 mass-selected bright ( K < 22.7 ) star-forming galaxies with known spectroscopic redshifts at z 1.5 in the ECDFS, UDS and COSMOS fields targeting their redshifted H and [ N ii ] emission lines.

COSMOS 171407 (minor merger treated as a disk): After smoothing the original adaptive optics datacube with a median filter, the gain in resolution decreases to about 2. The adaptive optics data shows the presence of a large redshifted clump on the blueshifted side of the disk and a more complex H intensity distribution. These features are also visible in the HST data which differs significantly from the exponential disk approach. We classify this galaxy as a minor merger but treat it as a disk given its ordered rotation (with v/ =2.8 in the AO data) and matching kinematic and morphological centres.

gmw_00010105.name: COSMOS 130477
gmw_00010105.overview: COSMOS 130477 is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 1.5 [arXiv:2109.10614]. It is classified as a disk galaxy with kinematics that are clearly dominated by rotation [arXiv:2109.10614].

sources.summary: The paper classifies COSMOS 130477 as a rotation-dominated disk where adaptive-optics data reveal a rapidly rising inner rotation curve despite limited high-SNR pixels.

quotes.quote: We used the reduced datacubes of the galaxies COSMOS 110446, COSMOS 171407, COSMOS 130477, COSMOS 127977 and UDS 78317 from the KMOS Galaxy Evolution Survey (KGES; KGES_alfie ). This survey comprises a sample of 288 mass-selected bright ( K < 22.7 ) star-forming galaxies with known spectroscopic redshifts at z 1.5 in the ECDFS, UDS and COSMOS fields targeting their redshifted H and [ N ii ] emission lines.

COSMOS 130477 (disk): This galaxy fulfils all the conditions to be classified as a disk, with kinematics clearly dominated by rotation with v/ =3 and v/ =4.8 in NS and AO respectively. Despite the spatial smoothing on the original high-resolution datacube, there are very few pixels with enough SNR to retrieve a value in the velocity field and thus the kinematic centre is less reliable. For that reason, the natural seeing map has a higher weight in the fit. However, the adaptive optics data contribution is still evident in the rapid rise of the rotation curve.

gmw_00010106.name: COSMOS 127977
gmw_00010106.overview: COSMOS 127977 is a star-forming disk galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 1.5 [arXiv:2109.10614]. It exhibits a ratio of rotation velocity to velocity dispersion that supports its classification as a disk [arXiv:2109.10614].

sources.summary: The paper finds COSMOS 127977 has good data in both resolutions, satisfies disk criteria, and shows beam-smearing differences between natural-seeing and high-resolution rotation curves.

quotes.quote: We used the reduced datacubes of the galaxies COSMOS 110446, COSMOS 171407, COSMOS 130477, COSMOS 127977 and UDS 78317 from the KMOS Galaxy Evolution Survey (KGES; KGES_alfie ). This survey comprises a sample of 288 mass-selected bright ( K < 22.7 ) star-forming galaxies with known spectroscopic redshifts at z 1.5 in the ECDFS, UDS and COSMOS fields targeting their redshifted H and [ N ii ] emission lines.

COSMOS 127977 (disk): The data quality is good in both resolutions and in this particular case, the extent of the high-resolution data is similar to the seeing limited one. It fulfils all conditions to be classified as a disk with v/ =2.4 (NS) and v/ =3.1 (AO). The model of the NS data shows a slowly rising rotation curve as compared to the high-resolution data which is consistent with the expected effects of beam smearing.

gmw_00010107.name: UDS 78317
gmw_00010107.overview: UDS 78317 is a high-redshift, mass-selected star-forming galaxy [arXiv:2109.10614]. While natural-seeing observations suggest a smooth rotating disk, high-resolution adaptive optics data reveal that the system is actually a complex galaxy merger [arXiv:2109.10614].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UDS 78317 as a high-redshift merger whose adaptive-optics maps reveal complex kinematics hidden by smoother natural-seeing data.

quotes.quote: We used the reduced datacubes of the galaxies COSMOS 110446, COSMOS 171407, COSMOS 130477, COSMOS 127977 and UDS 78317 from the KMOS Galaxy Evolution Survey (KGES; KGES_alfie ). This survey comprises a sample of 288 mass-selected bright ( K < 22.7 ) star-forming galaxies with known spectroscopic redshifts at z 1.5 in the ECDFS, UDS and COSMOS fields targeting their redshifted H and [ N ii ] emission lines.

UDS 78317 (merger): This galaxy is a very clear example of how low resolution can affect the correct classification of galaxies at high redshift. The seeing limited maps suggest a smooth rotating disk with a clear rotation axis despite a low v/ ratio of 1.3. On the other hand, the adaptive optics data clearly shows a complex kinematic state in both the kinematic and H intensity maps which resemble that of a merging system or a highly disrupted disk with v/ =1.2 . In the analysis of this galaxy at both resolutions conducted by , they also concluded that this galaxy is a merger.

gmw_00010108.name: COSMOS 08515
gmw_00010108.overview: COSMOS 08515 is classified as a disk galaxy [arXiv:2109.10614]. Observations of the galaxy reveal ordered rotation, making it ideal for velocity field determination [arXiv:2109.10614].

sources.summary: The paper classifies COSMOS 08515 as a disk with ordered rotation in both adaptive-optics and natural-seeing data, making the combined data useful for velocity-field determination.

quotes.quote: The rest of the galaxies (COSMOS 110446, COSMOS 130477, COSMOS 127977, COSMOS 08515, GOODS-S 29868 and GOODS-S 42930) are classified as disks.

COSMOS 08515 (disk): The adaptive optics gain in resolution is 6 but the radial extent of adaptive optics data is much lower than the seeing limited one so the combination is ideal for a good determination of the velocity field. The two datasets clearly show an ordered rotation with v/ =3.5 (NS) and v/ =3.0 (AO), and fulfil all criteria to be classified as a disk.

gmw_00010109.name: GOODS-S 40218
gmw_00010109.overview: GOODS-S 40218 is a compact high-redshift galaxy classified as a merger [arXiv:2109.10614]. It exhibits a lack of ordered rotation and has low velocity dispersion ratios [arXiv:2109.10614].

sources.summary: The paper classifies GOODS-S 40218 as a merger, cautioning that compact size and low-SNR pixels make its angular-momentum measurement unreliable.

quotes.quote: GOODS-S 40218 (merger): The results from this galaxy are the most defective from our sample since very few pixels have enough SNR for a reliable Gaussian fit and due to its compact size. We classify this galaxy as a merger given the lack of ordered rotation and low v/ ratios (1.0 for NS and 0.9 for AO). The angular momentum measurement is unreliable and should only be taken for reference. However, by measuring the uncertainty on this target (by MCMC resampling), we have an indication of the limits of the code for future work where we have similar data.

gmw_00010110.name: GOODS-S 42930
gmw_00010110.overview: GOODS-S 42930 is classified as a disk galaxy [arXiv:2109.10614]. It exhibits coherent ordered rotation with kinematic and photometric centers and position angles that coincide [arXiv:2109.10614].

sources.summary: The paper classifies GOODS-S 42930 as a confident disk with coherent rotation, matching centers and position angles, and improved combined-resolution rotation-curve modeling.

quotes.quote: The rest of the galaxies (COSMOS 110446, COSMOS 130477, COSMOS 127977, COSMOS 08515, GOODS-S 29868 and GOODS-S 42930) are classified as disks.

GOODS-S 42930 (disk): This galaxy has a coherent ordered rotation in both datasets and shows clearly the difference in the shape of rotation curves from low- and high- resolution (in the latter, the rotation curve rises more rapidly). The combination of the datasets improves the measurement since it yields a more realistic model where the rotation curve rises fast but reaches a consistent velocity at large radii. The kinematic and photometric centres and position angles coincide and the v/ ratios are large (3.3 for NS and 3.4 for AO) so we confidently classify this galaxy as a disk.

gmw_00010111.name: GOODS-S 33639
gmw_00010111.overview: GOODS-S 33639 is classified as a disk galaxy [arXiv:2109.10614]. It exhibits moderately ordered rotation at both natural and adaptive optics resolutions, although its continuum map lacks a clear major axis [arXiv:2109.10614].

sources.summary: The paper classifies GOODS-S 33639 as a disk with moderately ordered rotation, though noisy high-resolution pixels and continuum-axis ambiguity make its angular momentum uncertain.

quotes.quote: GOODS-S 33639 (disk): The high-resolution data contains many pixels where the direction of the velocity differs from that of the best velocity field, however the error bar from the Gaussian fit in some of these points is large so they are likely to be dominated by noise rather than signatures from the H emission. Overall the galaxy shows a moderately ordered rotation in both resolutions with v/ =3.8 (NS) and v/ =3.2 (AO), similar kinematic position angles at both resolutions, and similar photometric and kinematic centres so we classify it as a disk. There is also a significant misalignment with the photometric axis in both cases because the continuum map does not show a clear major axis. The measurement of the angular momentum content of the galaxy is largely uncertain as evidenced by the large errors quoted in tab:results .

gmw_00010112.name: HSC J090705.64 + 020955.8
gmw_00010112.overview: HSC J090705.64 + 020955.8 is a blue-excess dust-obscured galaxy located at a systemic redshift of z = 2.258 [arXiv:2211.16748]. The galaxy exhibits optical-band excesses and large emission-line equivalent widths that significantly affect its observed colors [arXiv:2211.16748].

sources.summary: The paper observes HSC J090705.64 + 020955.8 as a BluDOG at z=2.258, with optical-band excesses and large emission-line equivalent widths affecting its colors.

quotes.quote: By using FOCAS, we observed HSC J090705.64 + 020955.8 (hereafter J0907) on October 8th in 2019, with airmass 1.76 and seeing 0.5 arcsec. We used the 300B grism and the SY47 filter to cover _ obs 4700--9200 , with the resultant spectral resolution of R 800 for the used 0 .8-width slit.
The determined systemic redshifts of J0907, J1202, J1207, and J1443 are 2.258 0.002, 2.830 0.002, 2.511 0.001, and 3.317 0.006, respectively.

The excesses of the g band magnitudes for J0907, J1202, J1207, and J1443 are 1.27, 1.13, 1.48, and 0.88 mag, and those for the band excesses are 0.47, 0.44, 0.65, and 0.40 mag, respectively. Furthermore, we estimate the effect of the strong emission lines, based on their observed-frame EWs and the band widths (BW) of the HSC g and bands. The BWs of the HSC g and bands are 1468 and 1508 \ 2018PASJ...70...66K , respectively. By taking all of the emission lines (Tables tab:dlineJ0907 - tab:dlineJ1443 ) covered by the HSC g band (4000--5500 ) and band (5500--7000 ) into account, the total observed-frame EWs for J0907, J1202, J1207, and J1443 in the g band are 604, 258, 608, and 1380 \ respectively, while those in the band are 129, 836, 329, and 721 (Table tab:EVEWS ).

gmw_00010113.name: HSC J120200.84 011846.4
gmw_00010113.overview: HSC J120200.84 011846.4 is a blue-excess dust-obscured galaxy located at a systemic redshift of z=2.830 [arXiv:2211.16748]. It exhibits strong emission-line contributions to its photometry, which produce measured excesses in both the g and r bands [arXiv:2211.16748].

sources.summary: The paper observes HSC J120200.84 011846.4 as a BluDOG at z=2.830, measuring optical-band excesses and strong emission-line contributions to its HSC photometry.

quotes.quote: By using FORS2, we observed HSC J120200.84 011846.4, HSC J120728.71 005808.4, and HSC J144333.84 000830.3 (hereafter J1202, J1207, and J1443, respectively) between February 27th and March 8th, 2019. We used the GRISM 600RI +19 and the GG435 filter to cover _ obs 5200--8000 , which results in the spectral resolution of R 1500 with 0 .7-width slit.
The determined systemic redshifts of J0907, J1202, J1207, and J1443 are 2.258 0.002, 2.830 0.002, 2.511 0.001, and 3.317 0.006, respectively.

The excesses of the g band magnitudes for J0907, J1202, J1207, and J1443 are 1.27, 1.13, 1.48, and 0.88 mag, and those for the band excesses are 0.47, 0.44, 0.65, and 0.40 mag, respectively. Furthermore, we estimate the effect of the strong emission lines, based on their observed-frame EWs and the band widths (BW) of the HSC g and bands. The BWs of the HSC g and bands are 1468 and 1508 \ 2018PASJ...70...66K , respectively. By taking all of the emission lines (Tables tab:dlineJ0907 - tab:dlineJ1443 ) covered by the HSC g band (4000--5500 ) and band (5500--7000 ) into account, the total observed-frame EWs for J0907, J1202, J1207, and J1443 in the g band are 604, 258, 608, and 1380 \ respectively, while those in the band are 129, 836, 329, and 721 (Table tab:EVEWS ).

gmw_00010114.name: HSC J120728.71 005808.4
gmw_00010114.overview: HSC J120728.71 005808.4 is a high-redshift blue-excess dust-obscured galaxy located at a systemic redshift of z=2.511 [arXiv:2211.16748]. The galaxy exhibits a substantial g-band magnitude excess of 1.48 magnitudes and strong emission-line equivalent widths [arXiv:2211.16748].

sources.summary: The paper observes HSC J120728.71 005808.4 as a BluDOG at z=2.511, finding the largest listed g-band excess and substantial emission-line equivalent widths.

quotes.quote: By using FORS2, we observed HSC J120200.84 011846.4, HSC J120728.71 005808.4, and HSC J144333.84 000830.3 (hereafter J1202, J1207, and J1443, respectively) between February 27th and March 8th, 2019. gmw_00010115.name: HSC J144333.84 000830.3
gmw_00010115.overview: HSC J144333.84 000830.3 is a high-redshift blue dust-obscured galaxy located at a redshift of z=3.317 [arXiv:2211.16748]. It exhibits strong optical-band excesses and high emission-line equivalent widths in both the g and r bands [arXiv:2211.16748].

sources.summary: The paper observes HSC J144333.84 000830.3 as a BluDOG at z=3.317, with strong optical-band excesses and emission-line equivalent widths.

quotes.quote: By using FORS2, we observed HSC J120200.84 011846.4, HSC J120728.71 005808.4, and HSC J144333.84 000830.3 (hereafter J1202, J1207, and J1443, respectively) between February 27th and March 8th, 2019. We used the GRISM 600RI +19 and the GG435 filter to cover _ obs 5200--8000 , which results in the spectral resolution of R 1500 with 0 .7-width slit.

gmw_00010116.name: SMM J09431
gmw_00010116.overview: SMM J09431 is a high-redshift system at z = 3.35 consisting of two physically related galaxies, H6 and H7, separated by 30 kpc in projection [arXiv:1009.2495]. These galaxies are likely in an early merger stage, with H7 exhibiting dispersion-dominated gas and a velocity gradient indicative of rotation [arXiv:1009.2495].

sources.summary: The paper treats SMM J09431 as a two-galaxy SMG system, with CO data showing H7 rotation-like gradients, dispersion-dominated gas, and H6 emission possibly linked to an early merger.

quotes.quote: SMM J09431 (z = 3.35) -- a review of previous observations is given by , who themselves presented CO(4-3) data. The system consists of two galaxies, H6 and H7, separated by 30 kpc in projection, thus physically related and perhaps in an early stage of merging. Our new CO(6-5) data show a clear line spectrum for the south-west galaxy H7, with a global velocity gradient from -50 to +70 km s ^ 1 , probably indicative of rotation. find that the commonly adopted boundary between `dispersion-dominated' and `rotation-dominated' systems of v_ rot _0 (where v_ rot is the circular/orbital velocity and _0 is the intrinsic velocity dispersion) translates into _ int / v_ obs 1.25 (where v_ obs is the observed line-of-sight rotation and _ int is the integrated line FWHM divided by 2.355). H7 has _ int /v_ obs > 1.5 nearly everywhere, i.e. appears dispersion-dominated.

For the north-east galaxy H6, we detect a 4 line shifted about +210 km s ^ 1 relative to H7. Its width is 180 65 km s ^ 1 . There may also be broader and more redshifted emission extended over a larger region around H6 and towards H7, but at low significance.

gmw_00010117.name: NGC 579
gmw_00010117.overview: NGC 579 is a spiral host galaxy that contains a central nucleus [arXiv:1206.0748]. X-ray emission detected from its center is attributed to either diffuse galaxy activity or active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1206.0748].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 579 as the spiral host of SN 2007pk and attributes detected central and nearby X-ray emission to AGN or diffuse galaxy activity rather than the supernova.

quotes.quote: However, the SN is about 7'' from the center of the spiral host galaxy, NGC 579, and the centroids of the Swift XRT positions in individual exposures are clustered around the galaxy nucleus, rather than the SN position. We note that emission from the center of NGC 579 is clearly detected in the Chandra observation and that there is some emission in the source position. However, this emission may be due to diffuse emission from NGC 579. Therefore, without conclusive evidence that the emission is from the SN, here we assume that the observed flare as well as the quiescent X-ray emission from the position of the source is due to AGN activity in NGC 579. In Table tab:Samp , we adopted an upper limit on the X-ray luminosity of SN 2007pk, which is based on the average luminosity observed from the direction of the source, presumably due to AGN activity.

gmw_00010118.name: galaxy G
gmw_00010118.overview: Galaxy G is an early-type lens galaxy located at a redshift of 0.466 [arXiv:1608.06288]. It features an elliptical mass distribution and is accompanied by a nearby satellite galaxy, G1, which likely perturbs the lens potential [arXiv:1608.06288].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy G as the main early-type lens in HSC J1152+0047, measures its photometry and redshift, and models it as the elliptical mass component.

quotes.quote: HSC J1152+0047 consists of the main lens galaxy (G) with four lensed images (A, B, C and D) in a cross configuration (see fig:colimg ). A second galaxy (G1) located very close to image C, is probably a satellite of the main lens galaxy G and is likely to perturb the lens potential. We measured the relative positions and photometry of the lens galaxies and the lensed images from the HSC imaging using galfit Peng2002 .

The redshift of the lens galaxy is found to be z_ l =0.466 0.001 through the identification of CaII H and K absorption lines near the 4000 break as expected for a typical early-type galaxy. The lens galaxies are each modeled with an isothermal density profile. Galaxy G is allowed to have ellipticity whereas galaxy G1 is assumed to have spherically symmetric mass distribution since G1 is less massive and the contribution from the quadrupole moment of its mass distribution is not expected to be significant.

gmw_00010119.name: 4C12.03
gmw_00010119.overview: 4C12.03 is a low emission line radio galaxy associated with an elliptical host [arXiv:2105.11695]. It exhibits an X-shaped radio morphology with east-west wings and an inner double aligned with its main north-south axis [arXiv:2105.11695, arXiv:0811.1907].

sources.summary: The paper confirms 4C 12.03 as an X-shaped radio galaxy whose north-south active axis, steeper east-west wings, and aligned 80 kpc inner double suggest possible restarted activity.

quotes.quote: This X-shaped radio source is associated with an elliptical host galaxy Heckmanetal , classified as a low emission line radio galaxy located at redshift z = 0.156 1983MNRAS.204..151L . LalandRao2007 reported that 240 MHz and 610 MHz GMRT images show a symmetrical structure whose extent is 4^ along both axes, corresponding to 650 kpc. The overall X-shaped morphology has been explained by a million-year precession period 2011ApJ...734L..32G , or as a rapid realignment of a central supermassive black hole accretion disk system due to a relatively recent merger of a supermassive binary black hole LalandRao2007,MerrittEkers or as a result of disk instability Dennett2002 . Our new uGMRT and MeerKAT images, shown in Fig. sample-NVSS (bottom-left panel) and Fig. Meer-uGMRT (middle-left and middle-right panels respectively), confirm the X-shaped radio morphology. The northern and southern jets leading to the north and south hot spots respectively, form the active axis, whereas the east-west axis forms the low-surface brightness wings. The overall north-south morphology of the radio galaxy seems to follow an arc, and shows a clear asymmetry: the northern component culminates in a clear hot spot, as in FR IIs.

This radio galaxy also shows two inner brightness peaks, which form an inner double 80 kpc in size, aligned with the north-south axis of emission. Further investigation will be required to determine if this is the signature of restarted activity.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 4C12.03 as an X-shaped radio source without compact terminal hotspots, with very prominent wings and an embedded inner double aligned with the main lobes.

quotes.quote: 4C12.03: Although the radio source appears to have an edge-brightened morphology, the lobes do not possess compact hotspots at the ends lea91 . 4C12.03 has one of the most prominent set of wings among known X-shaped radio sources: the wings span an extent that is larger than that of the main radio axis. Once again, the radio source is of the double-double category and has an embedded inner double at the centre, with extent about 30 kpc, that is observed to be collinear with the main lobes.

gmw_00010120.name: B1531+104
gmw_00010120.overview: B1531+104 is a radio galaxy located within an Abell cluster [arXiv:0811.1907]. The source features asymmetric wings, lacks hotspots in its main lobes, and contains a bright inner double structure aligned with its main axis [arXiv:0811.1907].

sources.summary: The paper describes B1531+104 as a radio source in an Abell cluster, with asymmetric wings, no main-lobe hotspots, and a bright inner double aligned with the main axis.

quotes.quote: B1531+104: This source has been observed by owe97 and is a radio source in an Abell cluster. The source has wings that are asymmetric in extent: the eastern wing is less than half the extent of the western wing. There are no hotspots in both of the main pair of lobes. As in previous examples listed above, B1531+104 is observed to have an inner double structure that constitutes all of the bright structure in the source and is aligned with the main axis as defined by the main pair of lobes.

gmw_00010121.name: B1207+722
gmw_00010121.overview: B1207+722 is a radio galaxy located within an Abell cluster [arXiv:0811.1907]. It features a pair of wings centered on its core, and its two main lobes have their brightest regions recessed from their ends [arXiv:0811.1907].

sources.summary: The paper describes B1207+722 as an Abell-cluster radio source with wings centered on the core and main lobes whose brightest regions are recessed from their ends.

quotes.quote: B1207+722: This source is also a member of an Abell cluster and was imaged by owe97 . The observations clearly show a pair of wings centered on the core. The two main lobes have their brightest regions well recessed from the lobe ends; this attribute is more manifest for the northern lobe.

gmw_00010122.name: TXS 0730+504
gmw_00010122.overview: TXS 0730+504 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar associated with a Fermi LAT gamma-ray source [arXiv:2206.13665]. It features a bent radio jet, pronounced X-ray jet emission, and wing-like diverted emission resembling an X-shaped radio galaxy [arXiv:2206.13665].

sources.summary: The paper describes TXS 0730+504 as a flat-spectrum radio quasar with gamma-ray association, hotspot and bent jet structure, pronounced X-ray jet emission, and wing-like diverted emission.

quotes.quote: TXS 0730+504 TXS 0730+504 is a flat spectrum radio-quasar with a Fermi LAT ray association Abdollahi2020 . presented the VLA 1.4 GHz image of TXS 0730+504 which showed a hotspot towards the east and an FR I-like jet towards the west which then bends to the south abruptly. However, there seems to be a ``hotspot'' towards the southwest in the higher resolution image (see figure  fig:0730+504_vla ).

This is also the source with the most pronounced X-ray emission that covers the entire jet and also coincide with the location of the hotspot. The VLBA pc-scale jet is also aligned in the same direction as the X-ray jet Pushkarev2017 . The inner jet in TXS 0730+504 is pointing towards the hotspot and the rest of the emission appears diverted to the north, resembling wings in X-shaped radio galaxies Lal2019 .

gmw_00010123.name: PKS 1036+054
gmw_00010123.overview: PKS 1036+054 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of 0.473 [arXiv:2206.13665]. The galaxy lacks detected extended X-ray emission on kiloparsec scales and does not show stark differences in jet direction across different image resolutions [arXiv:2206.13665].

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1036+054 as a flat-spectrum radio quasar at redshift 0.473, lacking significant extended X-ray emission and strong resolution-dependent jet-direction changes.

quotes.quote: PKS 1036+054 PKS 1036+054 is classified as a flat-spectrum radio quasar at a redshift of 0.473 Healey2007 . We do not detect significant amounts of X-ray emission from the extended jet regions on kpc scales. Unlike PKS 0215+015 and TXS 0730+504, PKS 1036+054 does not show stark differences between jet directions in images with different resolutions.

gmw_00010124.name: COMBO-17 31611
aliases_external: COMBO-17 source #31611 / HUDF z=0.322 edge-on disk galaxy
gmw_00010124.overview: COMBO-17 31611 is a highly inclined disk galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.32 [arXiv:0807.0229, arXiv:astro-ph/0406207]. It features a bright disk with at best a small bulge [arXiv:0807.0229], surrounded by a very low surface brightness stellar halo [arXiv:astro-ph/0406207].

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes the HUDF galaxy's reported stellar halo, finding its inclined disk and minor-axis excess are strongly affected by PSF-scattered light, while possible B-band excess remains close to sky limits.

quotes.quote: ZibFer04 do however detect an extra component around a disc galaxy in the HUDF and report anomalous colours for this extended component. The HUDF galaxy studied by ZibFer04 is a highly inclined disc galaxy at a redshift of about 0.32 with at best a small bulge. ZibFer04 carefully removed the contribution from contaminating (mainly background) sources around the galaxy and extracted the luminosity profiles in wedges along the major and minor axes.

The extended minor-axis profile seen at heights greater than 1 arcsec from the midplane in the i and z bands can be fully explained by scattered light from the inner disc.
