On the southeast (negative z ) side there is a small excess in the V band that might be real, for instance due to small blue background galaxies seen here, but this component would have very remarkable colour properties (e.g., V - i < 0). It could also be due to the asymmetry in the main disc light distribution not modelled here or to some imperfections in the PSF models at that scale. The B band shows excess light beyond my model on both sides of the disc that could be a real halo detection, but these data points are very close to the sky background limit and would again result in some remarkable colours, this time mainly on the northwest side. Clearly, a large fraction of the light seen on the minor axis in excess of the distribution is due to scattered light from the extended PSF.

sources.summary: The paper identifies COMBO-17 31611 as a highly inclined UDF disk galaxy and reports a faint red stellar halo with low surface brightness, rounder isophotes, and colors distinct from the disk.

quotes.quote: We focus on a relatively large, well-formed disc galaxy located at RA =03^ h 32^ m 41 1 , Dec =-27 48 52 9 (J2000). This galaxy is clearly detected in all 4 bands and is identified with source 31611 at z=0.322 in the COMBO-17 photometric redshift survey of . The high inclination of this system makes it an ideal target for a study of faint extraplanar emission.

Our analysis of the UDF galaxy, COMBO-17 31611, has yielded the detection of a very low surface brightness structural component in addition to the bright disc. The main evidence for this component is: i) excess emission with respect to an exponential profile detected to 3 arcsec ( 14 kpc) from the disc plane at the level of _ V,i 29 mag arcsec ^ 2 ; ii) the isophotal shape is centrally-concentrated and becomes rounder with increasing radius, reaching b/a 0.6 at the faintest measured isophote; iii) colours far from the plane that are distinct from the disc, i-z 0.5 and V-i 0.7 which are 0.2-0.3 and 0.1 mag redder than the disc respectively. Based on these properties, we attribute this component to the stellar halo of the galaxy, making it the most distant detection of a stellar halo yet known.

gmw_00010125.overview: The galaxy 2335 is an early-type galaxy that acts as a strong gravitational lens [arXiv:2405.15427]. It is the brightest galaxy among its nearby neighbors, suggesting it may be a brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:2405.15427].

sources.summary: The paper finds 2335's predicted velocity dispersion much higher than observed and suggests its large Einstein radius may reflect a brightest cluster galaxy with cluster-halo contribution.

quotes.quote: For 2335, _ pred =426 15 km/s is much higher than the measured value _ obs =321 27 km/s . In fact, _ pred for 2335 is at the higher end of central velocity dispersions for ultra-massive quiescent galaxies e.g., Forrest:Wilson:2022 . The reason behind high _ pred for 2335 may be linked with its large Einstein radius R_ Eins =3 .59 , which is more typical of a galaxy group rather than an individual galaxy. In the DESI survey viewer, we find that the deflector galaxy in 2335 is the brightest among nearby galaxies at similar photometric redshift. Therefore, the deflector in 2335 may be a Brightest Cluster Galaxy (BCG), and the cluster halo may be contributing to its large Einstein radius.

gmw_00010126.overview: The galaxy 0142 is a strong gravitational lensing deflector with an Einstein radius of 2.33 arcseconds [arXiv:2405.15427]. It is classified as an early-type galaxy and may be a brightest cluster galaxy or brightest group galaxy [arXiv:2405.15427].

sources.summary: The paper says 0142's model-predicted dispersion is high but marginally consistent with observations, and suggests the deflector may be a brightest cluster or group galaxy.

quotes.quote: Similarly, 0142, which also has a high _ pred =385 29 km/s , may be a BCG or Brightest Group Galaxy (BGG). Athough, for 0142 _ pred is marginally consistent with _ obs =316 40 km/s at 1 uncertainty level and R_ Eins =2 .33 is typical of a galaxy-scale lens. We have encircled 2335 and 0142 deflectors in our - z diagrams discussed in the next section. The cluster halo can affect the main deflector galaxy's density profile, including the slope parameter Newman:Ellis:2015 . Thus, when measuring the evolution of the density profile, the brightest cluster/group galaxy lenses should be distinguished from the isolated galaxy lenses based on their host halo mass.

gmw_00010127.name: TV Ret
gmw_00010127.overview: TV Ret is a compact blue dwarf emission-line galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.0964 [arXiv:astro-ph/0611141]. It has a low metallicity of 0.12 solar, with emission line ratios that place it among H II galaxies near the Seyfert 2 boundary [arXiv:astro-ph/0611141].

sources.summary: TV Ret is identified as a compact blue dwarf emission-line galaxy at z=0.0964, with low metallicity and line ratios near the H II/Seyfert boundary.

quotes.quote: We find that the object is not a cataclysmic variable but an emission line galaxy with a redshift z=0.0964 . An R--image taken in very good seeing conditions shows that the object is extended. We show that TV Ret is a blue dwarf galaxy, probably compact, with an absolute magnitude of M_B = -17.5 , a metallicity of 0.12 solar, and an average temperature of 1.3 10^4 K. The line ratios place it among the H II galaxies, although close to the border of the Seyfert 2s.

gmw_00010128.name: PG 2304+042
gmw_00010128.overview: PG 2304+042 is a quasar featuring a compact, flat-spectrum radio core [arXiv:2308.16780, arXiv:2210.16764]. On kiloparsec scales, it is detected as a single compact component [arXiv:2308.16780, arXiv:2210.16764], while high-resolution imaging reveals weak, milliarcsecond-scale extensions [arXiv:2308.16780] and extreme radio variability at 8.6 GHz [arXiv:2308.16780, arXiv:2210.16764].

sources.summary: PG 2304+042 is detected as a compact VLBI core with high brightness temperature, changing spectral slope, weak extensions, and little C-band variability.

quotes.quote: On the kpc scale, PG 2304+042 was detected only a single compact component 1993MNRAS.263..425M . PG 2304+042 shows a compact core component in our VLBA images (Fig. fig:2304 ), which has a brightness temperature >3.2 10^ 7 K. The spectral index of the radio core is ^ 4.7 _ 1.6 = -0.26 0.03 consistent with a flat spectrum of ^ 4.8 _ 1.4 = -0.09 0.12 2022ApJ...936...73A but is steepened abruptly between 4.7 and 7.6 GHz with a spectral index of ^ 7.6 _ 4.7 =-1.36 0.40 (Fig. fig:2304 e ).

A similar spectrum shape can be seen in PG 1700+518 (Section sec:1700 ). There is a weak extension at 2.5 mas from the core to the northwest in the 4.7 and 7.6 GHz images, but the 1.6 GHz image shows some weak extension to the northeast 14 mas from the core. 2005ApJ...618..108B showed that PG 2304+042 has an extreme variability at 8.6 GHz. But the VLBI core shows no significant variability at C band between epochs 2015 and 2021.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 2304+042 as a compact, radio-variable quasar with VLA components, a 5-GHz VLBA core, and a reported flat-spectrum VLBI component.

quotes.quote: PG 2304+042 On kpc scales, PG 2304+042 was detected with a single compact component in the VLA D-array images and an unresolved component. This component coincides with a second elongated component in the VLA A-array images at 5 GHz 1994AJ....108.1163K . 2005ApJ...618..108B indicated extreme radio variability in PG 2304+042. PG 2304+042 shows a compact core component with a flux density of 0.54 mJy in our VLBA image at 5 GHz. also successfully detected a flat VLBI component in PG 2304+042 ( ^ 5GHz _ 1.4GHz =-0.09 ).

gmw_00010129.name: NGC 1134
gmw_00010129.overview: NGC 1134 is a peculiar, non-interacting starburst galaxy that features a complex inner structure with multiple dust filaments [arXiv:astro-ph/0409622, arXiv:2008.06039]. Its central region displays a three-arm spiral pattern and bar-like kinematics, while its radio continuum emission overlaps the optical disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0409622, arXiv:2008.06039].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes NGC 1134 as a peculiar, non-interacting galaxy with multi-arm structure, central bar-like kinematics, shock-front-like line profiles, and possible radial gas flows.

quotes.quote: Due to its complex inner structure (multiple dust filaments, etc.) the galaxy is included in Arp's Atlas of Peculiar Galaxies (1966), though it is not an interacting system. In the area <20 30 , HII regions trace a three-arm spiral (Fig. ngc1134 b), but in the outer part of the galaxy the spiral pattern is two-armed. The three-arm spiral structure in the center of NGC 1134 can also be seen when analyzing its direct images by constructing maps of a color Q-parameter free of dust influence (Bizyaev et al. 2001).

At small radii, <12 , the kinematic major axis (Fig. ngc1134 d) turns abruptly by more than 15 , so that the isovelocities (Fig. ngc1134 ) demonstrate an S-like shape typical for a bar potential. Above all, on both sides of the nucleus, at =4 7 there are regions where H emission-line profiles are two-component, with a separation between the components of 150-200 ( see Figs. ngc1134 g and ngc1134 h.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1134 as a starburst source whose radio continuum overlaps its optical disk and whose marginal polarization is weak and poorly organized.

quotes.quote: NGC 1134 NGC 1134 was classified as a starburst source based on their radio to FIR flux, the flux at 25 m to 60 m, and also due to the lack of any diffuse extended structures in radio images. Our image also shows radio continuum emission superposing the optical emission from the disk.

We detected marginal levels of polarization in NGC 1134, and UGC 903 which were undetected previously. The mean fractional polarization estimated from regions that showed polarization signal above three sigma threshold turned out to be in the range 1-4 for NGC 1134, NGC 253 and UGC 903.

gmw_00010130.name: UGC 903
gmw_00010130.overview: The radio emission of UGC 903 originates primarily from its galactic disk [arXiv:2008.06039]. This disk features faint, finger-like protrusions and exhibits marginal, weak levels of polarization [arXiv:2008.06039].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 903 as having radio emission mostly from its disk, faint disk protrusions, and only marginal, weak polarization in the authors’ RM-synthesis analysis.

quotes.quote: UGC 903 in their radio study of UGC 903 at 5 GHz find that most of the radio emission have their origin from the disk. They also find that faint finger-like structures that extend out of the disk.

We detected marginal levels of polarization in NGC 1134, and UGC 903 which were undetected previously. The mean fractional polarization estimated from regions that showed polarization signal above three sigma threshold turned out to be in the range 1-4 for NGC 1134, NGC 253 and UGC 903.

gmw_00010131.name: MARK 573
gmw_00010131.overview: MARK 573 is a Seyfert 2 galaxy with a nuclear spectrum that exhibits strong coronal emission [arXiv:1501.02022]. Its extended narrow-line region is over 8 kpc long and barrel-shaped, featuring a complex, knotty ionization cone and no signs of embedded H II regions [arXiv:1501.02022].

sources.summary: MARK 573 has a bright, extended barrel-shaped ENLR and knotty ionization cone, with strong nuclear coronal emission and no embedded H II regions.

quotes.quote: MARK 573: This Seyfert 2 galaxy ENLR has been imaged in [ O 3 ] by Schmitt03b , revealing a complex knotty ionisation cone on a 10 arcsec ( 2.2 kpc) scale. The WiFes data shows a very bright and much more extensive barrel-shaped ENLR which is over 8 kpc long. There is no sign of embedded \ regions. The nuclear spectrum shows strong coronal emission, see Figure fig:fig4 . Fischer10 using Wide Field Planetary Camera 2 and STIS observations argue that the ionising radiation field, confined to an ionisation cone from the central engine is inclined and intersects and ionises the inner spiral arms of the galaxy driving these into outflow.

gmw_00010132.name: NGC 5664
gmw_00010132.overview: NGC 5664 is a poorly studied Seyfert 2 galaxy [arXiv:1501.02022]. The galaxy is embedded in a star-forming ring and features a dynamically quiescent, polar bi-cone extended narrow-line region [arXiv:1501.02022].

sources.summary: NGC 5664 is a poorly studied Seyfert 2 embedded in a star-forming ring, with a large polar bicone ENLR that is dynamically quiescent.

quotes.quote: NGC 5664: This poorly-studied Seyfert 2 is embedded in a star-forming ring 13 arcsec (4.0kpc) in diameter. It displays a magnificent bi-cone ENLR orientated in the polar direction of this disk, with an opening angle of 90 . This is much fainter on the east side. The ENLR extends right across the WiFeS FOV, and is therefore larger than 25 arcsec (7.7kpc) across. The ENLR is co-rotating with the galaxy, and is dynamically quiescent.

gmw_00010133.name: ESO 138-G01
gmw_00010133.overview: ESO 138-G01 is a luminous Seyfert 2 galaxy that features a rich coronal-line spectrum [arXiv:1501.02022]. Its extended narrow-line region spans at least 35 arcseconds in diameter, showing split emission knots on both sides of the nucleus [arXiv:1501.02022].

sources.summary: ESO 138-G01 is a luminous Seyfert 2 with rich coronal lines, a broad-line hint, and ENLR emission traced across the field with split outer knots.

quotes.quote: ESO 138-G01: A luminous Seyfert 2 with a rich coronal-line spectrum. There is a suggestion of a broad-line component underlying H . The ENLR of this galaxy has been imaged by Schmitt05 , who traced the ENLR over about 16 arcsec, out to the inner jet region visible in Figure Images_2 . However, in the WiFeS data cube, we see the ENLR extending over the full field, out to at least 35 arcsec (6.4kpc) in diameter.
In particular, there are two knots of emission at 15 arcsec either side of the nucleus at PA = 105 . Both show pronounced line splitting with v 350 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00010134.name: FAIRALL 51
gmw_00010134.overview: FAIRALL 51 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy that exhibits a highly polarized continuum [arXiv:1501.02022]. Its nuclear spectrum displays broad helium line components and a strong, high-excitation coronal spectrum, while its extended narrow-line region spans up to several kiloparsecs [arXiv:1501.02022].

sources.summary: FAIRALL 51 is a Seyfert 1 with broad helium lines, strong intermediate-width coronal emission, high continuum polarization, and an ENLR spanning several kiloparsecs.

quotes.quote: FAIRALL 51: A Seyfert 1 with broad-line components visible in He 1 , He 2 , and down to H . The nuclear spectrum displays a strong high-excitation intermediate-width coronal spectrum with [ Fe 14 ] 5303 \ and [ Fe 10 ] 6374 \ lines prominent. Other forbidden lines are narrower. The continuum of this galaxy is highly polarised Smith04 . The ENLR can be traced over 10 7 arcsec, 2.8 1.9 kpc.

gmw_00010135.name: NGC 6812
gmw_00010135.overview: NGC 6812 is a classical LINER galaxy characterized by an E-type continuum and deep NaD absorption [arXiv:1501.02022]. It resides in a three-member group and may be interacting, featuring a polar-elongated emission-line region with velocity structures that suggest an outflow [arXiv:1501.02022].

sources.summary: NGC 6812 is a LINER in a small group, possibly interacting, with polar elongated emission-line gas whose velocity structure suggests outflow.

quotes.quote: NGC 6812: A classical LINER galaxy with E-type continuum and deep NaD absorption. This galaxy is in a group of 3, and may be interacting. The emission line region is elongated in the N-S direction (the polar direction in the galaxy), has dimensions 24 13 arcsec, 7.2 3.9 kpc. Its velocity structure suggests that it is probably an outflow.

gmw_00010136.name: A2261-BCG
gmw_00010136.overview: A2261-BCG is an early-type brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:2010.13980]. It is a candidate for hosting a recoiling supermassive black hole due to its exceptionally large, flat stellar core [arXiv:2010.13980].

sources.summary: The paper treats A2261-BCG as a recoiling black-hole candidate with an unusually large flat core, but finds no central X-ray point source above cluster gas.

quotes.quote: We use Chandra X-ray observations to look for evidence of a recoiling black hole from the brightest cluster galaxy in Abell 2261 (A2261-BCG). A2261-BCG is a strong candidate for a recoiling black hole because of its large, flat stellar core, revealed by Hubble Space Telescope imaging observations. We took 100-ksec observations with Chandra and combined it with 35 ksec of archival observations to look for low-level accretion onto a black hole of expected mass M 10^ 10 \ that could possibly be located in one of four off-center stellar knots near the galaxy's center or else in the optical center of the galaxy or in the location of radio emission. We found no X-ray emission arising from a point source in excess of the cluster gas and can place limits on the accretion of any black hole in the central region to a 2--7 keV flux below 4.3 10^ 16 \ erg \ s^ 1 \ cm^ 2 , corresponding to a bolometric Eddington fraction of about 10^ 6 . Thus there is either no 10^ 10 \ black hole in the core of A2261-BCG, or it is accreting at a low level.

A2261-BCG has a number of properties that set it apart from most other galaxies. First, it has one of the largest cores in its stellar surface brightness profile. Early-type galaxies can be separated into two categories based on their surface brightness profiles: core and cusp 1995AJ....110.2622L .

gmw_00010137.name: KISSR 618
gmw_00010137.overview: KISSR 618 is a Seyfert galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2106.09304]. Very Long Baseline Array observations at 1.5 GHz reveal a 30-parsec-long radio jet within the galaxy [arXiv:2106.09304].

sources.summary: The paper reports VLBA detection of a 30 pc radio jet in KISSR 618 and finds its emission lines can be fit by AGN photoionization plus shock-precursor models.

quotes.quote: Clear jet-like features are detected in KISSR 618, KISSR 872, and a possible jet component (J1) is detected at the 5 level in KISSR 1154 about 50 parsec from the radio core. The double optical nuclei galaxy, KISSR 872, shows the longest parsec-scale radio jet ( 200 parsec), which is detected at both 1.5 and 4.9 GHz (the inner 10 parsec). It is unclear if the VLBA image of KISSR 967 shows the beginning of a curved jet in Figure fig2 . The VLBA observations fail to detect any emission at 4.9 GHz in KISSR 618, KISSR 967 and KISSR 1154. This could imply that the core-jet emission has a steep radio spectrum.

The dusty AGN photoionization model as well as a shock+precursor model with 300 500 km s ^ 1 shock velocities fits the line data well in the Seyfert galaxy, KISSR 618. The gas density is 150 cm ^ 3 and the temperature is 10 ^4 K. The 1.5 GHz VLBA image reveals a 30 parsec long radio jet.

gmw_00010138.name: KISSR 967
gmw_00010138.overview: KISSR 967 is an active galactic nucleus that exhibits compact radio emission [arXiv:2106.09304]. Very Long Baseline Array observations of the source reveal an unresolved core with an estimated brightness temperature of 6 million Kelvin [arXiv:2106.09304].

sources.summary: The paper detects compact VLBA emission in KISSR 967, but treats any curved jet identification as uncertain and notes its unresolved-core brightness temperature.

quotes.quote: It is unclear if the VLBA image of KISSR 967 shows the beginning of a curved jet in Figure fig2 . The VLBA observations fail to detect any emission at 4.9 GHz in KISSR 618, KISSR 967 and KISSR 1154. This could imply that the core-jet emission has a steep radio spectrum.
We estimate the brightness temperature of the unresolved cores in KISSR 967 and KISSR 1154 to be 6 10^6 K and 7 10^6 K, respectively, using the relation by Ulvestad05 .
As seen in Figure fig12 , meaningful conclusions cannot be drawn about the best-fit model in KISSR 967, because the errors on the line (e.g., [ O iii ] ) flux measurements are too high.

gmw_00010139.name: KISSR 1154
gmw_00010139.overview: KISSR 1154 is a Seyfert galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2106.09304]. Very Long Baseline Array observations of the galaxy reveal a compact radio core and a possible jet component located approximately 50 parsecs away [arXiv:2106.09304].

sources.summary: The paper finds a possible 50 pc jet component and compact core in KISSR 1154, with shock-only and AGN-photoionization models fitting its emission-line properties.

quotes.quote: Clear jet-like features are detected in KISSR 618, KISSR 872, and a possible jet component (J1) is detected at the 5 level in KISSR 1154 about 50 parsec from the radio core. The double optical nuclei galaxy, KISSR 872, shows the longest parsec-scale radio jet ( 200 parsec), which is detected at both 1.5 and 4.9 GHz (the inner 10 parsec). It is unclear if the VLBA image of KISSR 967 shows the beginning of a curved jet in Figure fig2 . The VLBA observations fail to detect any emission at 4.9 GHz in KISSR 618, KISSR 967 and KISSR 1154. This could imply that the core-jet emission has a steep radio spectrum.

Shock-only Model A dust-free AGN photoionization and shock-only model with velocities between 100 300 km s ^ 1 fit the observed emission-line properties of the Seyfert galaxy KISSR 1154. The gas density estimated from the Sulphur lines is 400 cm ^ 3 and the temperature is 10 ^4 K using the PyNeb software; Luridiana15 . The shock+precursor model requires a higher value of magnetic fields in the medium, making it unrealistic. Its VLBA image shows a ``core'' and a possible jet knot 50 parsec away.

gmw_00010140.name: KISSR 1321
gmw_00010140.overview: KISSR 1321 is a Seyfert galaxy that represents the faintest FIRST radio source in its sample [arXiv:2106.09304]. The galaxy is not detected with the VLBA, and its line ratios are consistent with either a shock-only model or dusty active galactic nucleus photoionization [arXiv:2106.09304].

sources.summary: The paper reports that KISSR 1321, the faintest FIRST source in the sample, is not VLBA-detected, while its line ratios fit dusty AGN photoionization or shock-only models.

quotes.quote: Only the Seyfert galaxy with the faintest radio emission in the VLA FIRST image, viz., KISSR 1321 (S ^ total _ FIRST =1.6 mJy), is not detected with the VLBA at either radio frequency. The detection rate of parsec-scale emission for KISSR sources with VLA FIRST core flux density 2.4 mJy is 100 .

Similarly, for KISSR 1321, a dusty AGN photoionization model as well as the shock-only model work. The estimated gas density using the [ S II ] line is 110 cm ^ 3 . It is interesting though that this source is not detected with the VLBA.

gmw_00010141.name: KISSR 434
gmw_00010141.overview: KISSR 434 is a Seyfert galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2106.09304]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations of the galaxy reveal a curved radio jet spanning 150 parsecs in length [arXiv:2106.09304].

sources.summary: The paper notes that KISSR 434 has a curved 150 pc VLBI jet, but its emission-line data favor dusty AGN photoionization rather than shock models.

quotes.quote: As presented in Kharb19 , the dusty AGN photoionization model fits the data well for the Seyfert galaxy, KISSR 434, while the shock+precursor model or the shock-only model is not a good fit. This was surprising because KISSR 434 exhibits a 150 parsec long curved jet in its 1.5 GHz VLBA image. A possibly precessing jet could be stirring up NLR clouds, causing the emission line peak splits; it is however not shock-ionising the NLR gas.
The only exception is KISSR 434 where a curved 150 parsec long radio jet is observed with VLBI but the shock+precursor model does not fit the emission line data.

gmw_00010142.name: FIRST J160613.1+312623
gmw_00010142.overview: FIRST J160613.1+312623 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=4.56 [arXiv:2109.10599]. It is classified as a GHz Peaked Spectrum source, featuring a young, developing radio structure with two-sided jets and terminal hotspots [arXiv:2109.10599].

sources.summary: The paper studies FIRST J160613.1+312623 as a z=4.56 radio galaxy, identifying compact two-sided jets, GPS behavior, young age, high jet power, and a dusty gas-rich host.

quotes.quote: The source FIRST J160613.1+312623 (in short, ) is a radio galaxy at z=4.56 2018ApJS..239...20M . It is very faint in the optical band 1993ApJS...88....1S but bright in the infrared named WISEA J160608.53+312446.5, 2013wise.rept....1C and radio bands e.g., 1977AJ.....82..776O . \ has been detected in several radio surveys, including the Green Bank 91 m telescope survey at 4.85 GHz 1991ApJS...75.1011G,1991ApJS...75....1B , the Faint Images of the Radio Sky at Twenty Centimeters FIRST, 1995ApJ...450..559B of the Very Large Array (VLA) and NRAO VLA Sky Survey at 1.4 GHz NVSS, 1998AJ....115.1693C , the VLBA Calibrator Survey VCS, 2002ApJS..141...13B and the Combined Radio All-Sky Targeted Eight GHz Survey of the VLA at 8.4 GHz CRATES, 2007ApJS..171...61H .

Very long baseline interferometry (VLBI) images reveal a two-sided jet structure with edge-brightened terminal hotspots separated by about 68 parsecs. No evidence for the presence of extended emission (relic of past activity) is found, suggesting that it might be a nascent radio source. We study the radio properties of including radio spectrum, variability, core brightness temperature, jet proper motion. All observations consistently indicate that it is a GHz Peaked Spectrum (GPS) source, making it the highest redshift GPS galaxy known to date. The expansion velocity of the hotspots and the turnover in the radio spectrum suggest that \ is a young (kinematic age of 3000 years) and developing radio source.

gmw_00010143.name: PGC007748
gmw_00010143.overview: PGC007748 is a massive early-type galaxy located at a distance of approximately 151 to 183 Mpc [arXiv:2103.07478]. It has an estimated accreted mass fraction of 86 percent, suggesting that the majority of its mass was acquired through major mergers [arXiv:2103.07478].

sources.summary: The paper maps PGC007748 with deep VEGAS imaging and MUSE data, finding an 86 percent accreted fraction, transition radii within the MUSE field, and a flat color segment.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present the new deep images from the VEGAS survey of three massive ( M_ * 10^ 12 M _ ) galaxies from the MUSE Most Massive Galaxies (M3G) project, with distances in the range 151 D 183 Mpc: PGC007748, PGC015524 and PGC049940. The long integration time and the wide field of view of OmegaCam@VST allowed us to map the light and color distributions down to _g 30 mag/arcsec ^2 and out to 2R_e .

The higher accreted mass fraction estimated for PGC007748 and PGC015524 (86 and 89 , respectively), combined with the flat _R profiles suggest that a great majority of the mass has been acquired through major mergers, which have also shaped the shallower metallicity profiles observed at larger radii.

For PGC007748 and PGC015524 instead, both the first ( R_ tr1 ) and the second ( R_ tr2 ) transition radii are within 60 arcsec. The azimuthally averaged (g - ) color profiles show a similar behaviour up to the first transition radius ( R_ tr2 ) for all the three galaxies (bottom panels of Fig. PGC7748 , PGC015524 and PGC049940 ). They all have redder colors in the central regions. A gradient towards bluer colors is observed from the galaxies' centers out to the first transition radius ( R_ tr1 ). For R_ tr1 R R_ tr2 the color profile of PGC007748 remains almost flat, while for PGC015524 it is slightly decreasing.

gmw_00010144.name: SDSS J1052+1036
gmw_00010144.overview: SDSS J1052+1036 is a low-redshift Type-1.9 active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of approximately 0.088 [arXiv:2401.17683]. The galaxy exhibits a highly blue-shifted broad H-alpha emission line, which is best explained by emission from an active galactic nucleus disk [arXiv:2401.17683].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J1052+1036 as a low-redshift Type-1.9 AGN whose blue-shifted broad H is best explained by AGN disk emission rather than BBH or rSMBH scenarios.

quotes.quote: In this manuscript, very blue-shifted broad H with shifted velocity 2200km/s is reported in the low redshift Type-1.9 AGN SDSS J1052+1036. Blue-shifted broad emission lines may arise due to the presence of a rotating gas disk around central black hole (BH), but may also be a signature of rare phenomena such as gravitational wave recoil of a supermassive BH (rSMBH) or the presence of a binary BH (BBH) system. Here, due to larger shifted velocity of stronger and wider blue-shifted broad H , the BBH system is disfavoured. Meanwhile, if this object contained a rSMBH, intrinsic obscuration with E(B-V) 0.6 should lead to a detectable broad H , indicating the rSMBH scenario not preferred. We find that the blue-shifted broad H can be well explained by emission from an AGN disk, indicating that SDSS J1052+1036 is likely a disk-emitting AGN.
Here, a candidate at redshift 0.088 is reported with blue-shifted velocity 2200km/s in broad H in a Type-1.9 AGN SDSS J105232.97+103620.08 (= ), with different scenarios discussed.

gmw_00010145.name: Horologium II
gmw_00010145.overview: Horologium II is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy candidate and a satellite of the Milky Way [arXiv:1505.04948]. It features an old, metal-poor stellar population and is located at a heliocentric distance of approximately 78 kpc [arXiv:1505.04948].

sources.summary: The paper reports Horologium II as a newly discovered ultra-faint Milky Way satellite candidate, measuring its size, luminosity, old metal-poor population, distance, alignment, and possible common origin.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Properties of Horologium II Parameter & Value & Unit _ J2000 & 3 16 32.1 5.0 & h m s _ J2000 & 50 01 05 5 & ^

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a new ultra-faint Milky Way satellite candidate, Horologium II, detected in the Dark Energy Survey Y1A1 public data. Horologium II features a half light radius of r_ h =47 10 pc and a total luminosity of M_ V =-2.6^ +0.2 _ 0.3 that place it in the realm of ultra-faint dwarf galaxies on the size-luminosity plane. The stellar population of the new satellite is consistent with an old ( 13.5 Gyr) and metal-poor ( [ Fe/H ] 2.1 ) isochrone at a distance modulus of (m-M)=19.46 0.20 , or a heliocentric distance of 78 8 kpc, in the color-magnitude diagram. Horologium II has a distance similar to the Sculptor dwarf spheroidal galaxy ( 82 kpc) and the recently reported ultra-faint satellites Eridanus III ( 87 8 kpc) and Horologium I ( 79 8 kpc). All four satellites are well aligned on the sky, which suggests a possible common origin.

This new object, Horologium II, was initially detected at the 7-8 significance levels in two separate, but overlapping DECam images. To fill the CCD chip gaps, we combined the photometric catalogs of the two frames and removed duplicates with 1 tolerance. Hor II was then recovered with a significance of 10 .

gmw_00010146.name: J031552-190644
gmw_00010146.overview: J031552-190644 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy hosted by a disc-dominated spiral galaxy [arXiv:1701.07036]. Located near the center of the galaxy cluster A428, it exhibits strong, narrow neutral hydrogen absorption against its compact radio core [arXiv:1701.07036].

sources.summary: J031552-190644 is a well-studied FR-I radio source in a spiral host near A428, with strong narrow H i absorption against its compact core and similar column-density results in this survey.

quotes.quote: J031552-190644 (z = 0.0671) A previously known strong H i absorption feature Ledlow2001 is detected against the compact flat spectrum core of this well-studied FR-I radio source. The AGN is hosted by a disc-dominated spiral galaxy Ledlow1998 . The host galaxy is located close to the centre of the galaxy cluster A428. found that the very narrow peak absorption in the host galaxy gave a large apparent optical depth of 0.98 0.06 that is only seen against the radio core and not against either of the two observed radio jets, hence ruling out a large, diffuse H i halo.

They measure a column density of N _ HI 1.82 10 ^ 18 T_ S f _ FWHM cm ^ 2 , with a FWHM velocity width of _ FWHM = 34 km s ^ 1 giving a value of N _ HI 6.19 10 ^ 19 T_ S f cm ^ 2 . suggests that, given this high optical depth, the absorbing gas is either of a high density, or the sightline goes through the disc in such a way to maximise the amount of absorbing gas (e.g. through a spiral arm or very dense cloud, possibly originating in the ISM of the host galaxy due to the narrow linewidth they observe). Our measurement of the peak optical depth is similar to that of ; we find the H i column density to be N _ HI = 5.62 ( 0.27) 10 ^ 19 T_ S f cm ^ 2 (Table 4). The absorption line feature in our spectrum is best fitted by two narrow Gaussians, both located around the spectroscopic optical redshift, and matches the narrow, deep feature that observed.

gmw_00010147.name: J060555-392905
gmw_00010147.overview: J060555-392905 is a flat-spectrum radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.0452 [arXiv:1701.07036]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus with a low accretion rate, and its mid-infrared colors suggest a star-forming host galaxy with a gas-rich disk [arXiv:1701.07036].

sources.summary: J060555-392905 is a flat-spectrum AGN radio source with a new narrow H i detection; the absorption may trace cold gas near the AGN or a gas-rich galactic disk.

quotes.quote: J060555-392905 (z = 0.0452) This is a flat-spectrum radio source with a new H i detection. It was spectroscopically classified as an AGN source with only absorption features (Aa) within its optical spectrum by . The presence of a foreground star affects both the spectrum and the visual classification of the morphology of the host galaxy (Fig. 4).

A one-component, narrow Gaussian fit slightly blueshifted ( 68 4 km s ^ 1 ) from the spectroscopic optical redshift was the most statistically likely in modelling (Table 3). Given the narrow absorption line and its slightly blueshifted location, the cold H i gas we observe could be located close to the host galaxy's AGN, and perhaps be an outflow of a clump of gas. However, the galaxy's mid-infrared colours suggest at a star-forming host galaxy with a low AGN accretion rate (Section 5.1.1), and hence the absorption could be due to a gas-rich galactic disc.

gmw_00010148.name: J084452-100059
gmw_00010148.overview: J084452-100059 is a Fanaroff-Riley type I radio galaxy with a steep-spectrum radio profile [arXiv:1701.07036]. Its optical image and infrared colors suggest an elliptical morphology, and it exhibits a tentative, weak broad H I absorption line [arXiv:1701.07036].

sources.summary: J084452-100059 is a steep-spectrum FR-I galaxy with a tentative weak broad H i absorption line, elliptical-like colors and image, and low-significance but statistically favored line model.

quotes.quote: J084452-100059 (z = 0.0423) We find a tentative new detection of a weak broad line against this steep-spectrum source (spectral index of = -0.7 ). As with J060555-392905, it was spectroscopically identified as an Aa galaxy in . It is classified as a FR-I type galaxy due to its extended low-frequency emission in NVSS. Beyond that there is a lack of information regarding the morphology of the galaxy within the literature. Its infrared colours are similar to those of elliptical galaxies, and its optical image suggests this morphology as well (Fig. 2).

The feature is best modelled by a wide, weak absorption line centred slightly blueward of the source galaxy's optical redshift (shifted by 180 km s ^ 1 ). This detection has low significance compared to the other detections (Table 3). However, the logarithmic odds ratio of R = 13.9 0.3 means the spectral line model is favoured over the continuum fit (where there is a higher probability for a model to be true relative to another if it has a (log) Bayes odds ratio R > 1).

gmw_00010149.name: Little Cub
gmw_00010149.overview: Little Cub is an extremely metal-poor, gas-rich blue compact dwarf galaxy with a stellar mass of approximately 10^5 solar masses [arXiv:1708.01260]. It is interacting with the nearby spiral galaxy NGC 3359, which appears to be stripping its neutral gas [arXiv:1708.01260].

sources.summary: The paper reports the Little Cub as an extremely metal-poor nearby BCD, measuring low oxygen abundance, small stellar mass, high gas richness, and evidence for stripping during interaction with NGC 3359.

quotes.quote: In this Letter, we report the discovery of one of the lowest-metallicity BCDs currently known, J1044+6306, found in the constellation Ursa Major (a.k.a. the Great Bear), which we nickname the Little Cub. In Section ODR , we present the results of our spectroscopic observations of the Little Cub made using the Kast spectrometer on the Shane 3 m telescope at Lick Observatory and the Low Resolution Imaging Spectrometer (LRIS) at Keck Observatory. We analyze the spectra and derive chemical abundances in Section AD . In Section Disc , we discuss the physical properties of the Little Cub and the environment in which it resides, including its potential interaction with the nearby spiral galaxy NGC 3359.

Our analysis of these spectra show that the Little Cub is one of the lowest-metallicity star-forming galaxies known in the nearby universe, with a direct gas-phase oxygen abundance of only12 + log(O/H) = 7.13 0.08. We estimate that the Little Cub contains roughly 10 ^ 5 M_ of stars and is gas-rich, with a neutral gas to stellar mass ratio of 96. We report that the Little Cub exhibits a velocity offset of 53 km s ^ 1 from a nearby grand design spiral galaxy (NGC 3359), at a projected distance of just 69-90 kpc. The Little Cub also shows evidence of neutral gas being stripped, further supporting the idea that these two systems are interacting.

gmw_00010150.name: SDSS J2248 0036
gmw_00010150.overview: SDSS J2248 0036 is a candidate BL Lacertae object [arXiv:astro-ph/0703289]. It is classified as a low-polarization active galactic nucleus, and correcting for host galaxy starlight does not elevate its polarization above the high-polarization threshold [arXiv:astro-ph/0703289].

sources.summary: The paper discusses SDSS J2248 0036 as a candidate BL Lac sightline with possible interstellar polarization, but aperture and host-galaxy corrections still do not make it highly polarized.

quotes.quote: only those in the fields of SDSS J083943.36+354001.5 (SDSS J0839+3540; hereafter the IAU designation for objects is shortened to SDSS J hhmm ddmm ) and SDSS J2248 0036 show evidence for P_ ISP approaching 2 . This high level of ISP is dwarfed by the measured polarization of SDSS J0839+3540. In contrast, inclusion of the ISP along the sight line to SDSS J2248 0036 does not increase its polarization over the P > 3 threshold.

The effect is most pronounced for SDSS J2248 0036, however the nearly factor of 2 increase in P between a 6 and 2 aperture is not enough for this candidate to meet the high-polarization criterion. Likewise, correcting for host galaxy starlight does not elevate the high-polarization resolved BL Lac objects into the range of P > 10 that is populated exclusively by objects with unknown redshifts. Note that Galactic ISP can be ruled out as the sole polarizing mechanism for low-polarization objects like SDSS J2248 0036 because P would be constant with aperture.

gmw_00010151.name: SDSS J0201+0025
gmw_00010151.overview: SDSS J0201+0025 is an active galactic nucleus with an unknown redshift [arXiv:astro-ph/0703289]. It is considered a candidate BL Lacertae object, though its true classification remains questionable due to a low polarization upper limit [arXiv:astro-ph/0703289].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J0201+0025 as an unknown-redshift candidate with only a polarization upper limit; further polarimetry is needed, and its true BL Lac classification remains questionable.

quotes.quote: In addition, only upper limits could be placed on P for two objects with unknown redshifts included in this group: SDSS J0201+0025 and SDSS J1048+6203. The 1-sigma upper limit on P for SDSS J0201+0025 is 5 , so further polarimetry is required to definitively determine if it is highly polarized.

One curve represents the entire polarization survey sample of 42 objects, while the other omits the z > 1 objects as well as SDSS J0201+0025 and SDSS J1048+6203 since their classification as true BL Lac objects is questionable as discussed in 4.1.

gmw_00010152.name: PKS 0144+209
gmw_00010152.overview: PKS 0144+209 is an optically empty radio source with no detected optical counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations reveal an elongated structure that may represent either a core-jet system or a compact symmetric object [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068].

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0144+209 as an optically empty GPS radio source with elongated VLBI structure that could be core-jet or a CSO depending on core identification.

quotes.quote: This radio source does not have an optical counterpart and is reported as an empty field in Table 1. Snellen et al. (2002) estimated its APM (the Automated Plate Measurement Facility at Cambridge) red magnitude is > 20, and the APM blue magnitude is > 22 based on the digitized Palomar Observatory Sky Survey (POSS) plates. In the Jodrell Bank--VLA Astrometric Survey (JVAS), the total flux density at 8.4 GHz for the source is 337.2 mJy and the source is classified as suitable (i.e. pointlike at subarcsecond resolution) as a MERLIN phase calibrator (Wilkinson et al. 1998).

The VLBI images exhibit an elongated structure with a position angle of 40^ . At 2.3 GHz (Fig. 0144S ), the total source size detected is more extended to the North-West than at 8.4 GHz (Fig. 0144X ). The source is a core-jet source if its southernend is the core of the source. However, if the strongest central component is the core, the source could be a CSO instead.

gmw_00010153.overview: 0159+839 is a possible quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068] that is associated with a ROSAT X-ray source [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068]. Radio observations reveal a compact VLBI morphology with an inverted spectral index and a core-jet structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068].

sources.summary: The paper treats 0159+839 as a possible quasar-like GPS source with ROSAT emission, compact VLBI morphology, an inverted spectrum, and a likely core-jet interpretation.

quotes.quote: 0159+839 ( JVAS J0207+8411 , NVSS J020713+84111 ) Spoelstra et al. (1985) classified this object as a 17th magnitude 'stellar' object, de Vries et al. (2000) marked the object as a 'quasar?'. This is a ROSAT source, 1RXS J020716.5+841126 . In the radio, the JVAS flux density at 8.4 GHz is 77.9 mJy and the source is a suitable MERLIN phase calibrator (Wilkinson et al. 1998).

Our VLBI images show a weak, slightly resolved source at 2.3 GHz (Fig. 0159S ), and a point-like object at 8.4 GHz (Fig. 0159X , where about a half of the JVAS flux density is accounted for). The source is resolved into core-jet like (components A,B), and a hint of a third component C in the direction of the jet, while it is unresolved at 8.4 GHz. Further, the VLBI structure as a whole has an inverted spectral index ( = -0.38 ).

gmw_00010154.overview: 0602+780 is a very faint galaxy that functions as a suitable phase calibrator [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068]. Radio observations show a point-like source with an inverted spectrum and possible jet emission that is resolved out [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 0602+780 as a very faint galaxy with point-like VLBI images, an inverted spectrum, and possible resolved-out jet emission.

quotes.quote: 0602+780 ( JVAS J0610+7801 , NVSS J061024+78013 ) This object is identified as a very faint galaxy by Stanghellini et al. (1993). In the radio, the JVAS flux density at 8.4 GHz is 124.1 mJy and the source is a suitable MERLIN phase calibrator (Wilkinson et al. 1998). In the VCS it is found to be point source. Our images at both frequencies show a point-like source (Fig. 0602S and Fig. 0602X ), with a rather inverted spectrum ( = -0.57 ). About 15 of the JVAS flux density is not accounted for in the VLBI image of Fig. 0602X , possibly related to a jet component completely resolved out by the present observations at both frequencies.

gmw_00010155.name: PKS 0802+212
gmw_00010155.overview: PKS 0802+212 is a faint radio galaxy with an apparent magnitude of 22.5 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations of the source reveal a compact core-jet morphology and indicate flux variability [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 0802+212 as a faint galaxy whose VLBI images show a compact core-jet morphology and flux variability signatures.

quotes.quote: PKS 0802+212 ( JVAS J0805+2106 , NVSS J080538+210651 ) The source is identified as a galaxy with m_ R =22.5 (de Vries et al. 2000). In the radio, the JVAS flux density at 8.4 GHz is 348.8 mJy and the source is a suitable MERLIN phase calibrator (Wilkinson et al. 1998). The source was detected with a total flux density of 0.22 Jy at 22 GHz by Ter asranta et al. (2001). Images at 2.3 GHz and at 8.4 GHz are available from the VCS. Our VLBI image at 2.3 GHz (Fig. 0802S ) shows a marginally resolved object, whose structure is well highlighted at 8.4 GHz (Fig. 0802X ) where a compact core-jet morphology is visible.

Both our images are consistent with the VCS results. Indeed the total flux density accounted for in the 8.4 GHz image exceeds the VLA measurement of the JVAS by about 17 . All these pieces of information suggest a core-jet classification for this source.

gmw_00010156.name: PKS 0914+114
gmw_00010156.overview: PKS 0914+114 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.178 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068]. It is classified as a gigahertz-peaked spectrum source and a compact symmetric object [arXiv:1005.3360], featuring a core, a one-sided jet, and two lobes [arXiv:1005.3360].

sources.summary: The paper presents multifrequency VLBI imaging of PKS 0914+114, describing its GPS spectrum, core, one-sided jet, lobes, component spectra, and classification as a compact symmetric object.

quotes.quote: PKS 0914+114 The source has a GPS spectrum (Stanghellini et al. 1998), recently it has been optically identified as an empty field (Labiano et al. 2007). In the 1.6 GHz VLBI image, it shows a core component A, one-side jet B and two lobes C, E (Liu et al. 2007). Considering the source structure at 1.6 GHz, we have re-processed the data at 2.3, 5, 8.4 GHz with careful calibration and `clean' in AIPS, the component E has been restored at 2.3, 5 GHz, and component C is restored at 8.4 GHz, as shown in Fig. fig1 , Fig. fig2 , Fig. fig3 .

The component spectra (Fig. fig4 ) from the VLBI images at 1.6, 2.3, 5, and 8.4 GHz, show that the core A has a flatter spectrum than other components. The data are listed in Table tb2 . Based on its structure and spectra, we classify this GPS source as a CSO.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 0914+114 as a z=0.178 galaxy whose steep-spectrum, multi-component VLBI structure makes it a CSO candidate.

quotes.quote: PKS 0914+114 ( NVSS J091716+111336 ) This source is associated with a galaxy, m_ =20.0 (Stanghellini et al. 1993), z=0.178 (de Vries et al. 1998). Our VLBI image at 2.3 GHz (Fig. 0914S ), accounting for the whole source flux density at this frequency, shows four well separated components, with no obvious interpretation of the structure. Only one of them, likely component C, is detected at 8.4 GHz (Fig. 0914X ). About 50 of the total flux density at this frequency (cf. Stanghellini et al. 1998) is missing in the image. If it is the component C, its spectral index would be = 1.73 , slightly steeper than the optically thin spectral index ( = 1.6 ) derived from VLA data.
Given this steep spectral index and the source structure seen in our data, we can consider this object a CSO candidate.

gmw_00010157.name: B3 1133+432
gmw_00010157.overview: B3 1133+432 is an active galactic nucleus that is classified as an empty field in the optical spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068]. It is considered a good compact symmetric object candidate, exhibiting a very compact, lobe-dominated double morphology in radio observations [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068].

sources.summary: The paper classifies B3 1133+432 as a good CSO source, interpreting its double VLBI morphology as a compact lobe-dominated structure despite no detected core.

quotes.quote: B3 1133+432 ( NVSS J113555+425844 ) This is an empty field in the optical (Stickel et al. 1994). Our VLBI images at 2.3 GHz and at 8.4 GHz (Fig. 1133S and Fig. 1133X ) show a double source, with both components resolved by the present observations. This source has been studied in detail by Dallacasa et al. (2002) at 1.7 GHz and by Orienti et al. (2004) at 5.0 GHz and at 8.4 GHz with the VLBA, as part of a study on the Compact Steep Spectrum sources from the B3-VLA sample selected by Fanti et al. (2001).

Our results are consistent with the aforementioned papers, although our 8.4 GHz image could not account for about 35 of the total flux density. The spectral indices we derived for our components between 2.3 and 8.4 GHz ( = 1.23 and = 1.65 ) are steeper than those derived by Orienti et al. (2004) between 5.0 and 8.4 GHz due to the smaller fraction of flux density accounted for in our 8.4 GHz image. Despite the lack of a core detection, which would be one requirement for a proper CSO classification, we consider this object as a good CSO source, since we interpret the overall morphology as a very compact, lobe dominated double; to detect the core we would need a much higher dynamic range and possibly a better UV-coverage at 8.4 GHz.

gmw_00010158.overview: The radio source 1824+271 is associated with a faint optical counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations reveal a double structure aligned roughly east-west, which is consistent with a compact symmetric object [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068].

sources.summary: The paper presents 1824+271 as a faint GPS-associated object with double steep-spectrum VLBI components consistent with a low-dynamic-range CSO classification.

quotes.quote: 1824+271 ( J1826+2707 ) It is associated with a faint object with m_ R =22.9 (O'Dea et al. 1990). Given such weak magnitude its optical counterpart does not have a morphological classification (G/Q) although galaxies are more frequently found than quasars associated with GPS sources. In the radio, our 2.3 and 8.4 GHz VLBI images (Fig. 1824S and Fig. 1824X ) show a double structure roughly aligned in the East-West direction; both components have steep spectra ( = 1.37 for the brighter A component, = 1.63 for the weaker B component) and are consistent with the overall steep radio spectrum ( = 1.4 ) derived from the literature data.

These flux densities indicate that while the whole source flux density is accounted for in our 2.3 GHz VLBI image, some fraction of the flux density may be missing in our 8.4 GHz VLBI image. The source structure is consistent with the expectation of a CSO seen in low dynamic range images, and therefore we suggest this classification.

gmw_00010159.name: PKS 2121 014
gmw_00010159.overview: PKS 2121 014 is a galaxy-hosted gigahertz-peaked spectrum source located at a redshift of 1.158 [arXiv:1005.3360]. It is classified as a compact symmetric object [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068, arXiv:1005.3360] and exhibits a double structure that suggests a close interaction [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 2121 014 as an interacting z=1.158 galaxy with resolved double VLBI structure and classifies it as a CSO.

quotes.quote: PKS 2121 014 ( NVSS J212339 011234 ) This is a galaxy with m_ R =23.3, which shows double structure presumably indicating a case of close interaction (de Vries et al. 2000). The redshift for this system is 1.158 (Snellen et al. 1996). Our VLBI images exhibit a double structure with both components well resolved. At 2.3 GHz (Fig. 2121S ) a second component may be added to the main one (component A), possibly representing a sort of tail/lobe emission.

However, the component A is quite weak at 8.4 GHz (Fig. 2121X ) with a spectral index of 1.43, indicating it is not the core of the source. Surprisingly, component C has a mid-steep spectrum ( =0.65 ), despite its considerably larger size at 2.3 GHz. As in the case of 1824+271, we consider this source as a CSO.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2121 014 as a galaxy-hosted GPS source at redshift 1.158, with steep-spectrum lobes, an inverted undetected component, and a CSO classification.

quotes.quote: PKS 2121 014 This GPS source is hosted by a galaxy at a redshift of 1.158. Using the VLBI results at 1.6, 2.3, 5, and 8.4 GHz we obtained the spectra for each component (Fig. fig5 ), the two lobes A, C show steep spectra, in which we have reprocessed the 8.4 GHz data as shown in Fig. fig6 with careful calibration and `clean' in AIPS. As discussed in Xiang et al. (2006), the two components A, C in the 8.4 GHz image (Xiang et al. 2005) might be swapped due to worse phase calibration, so the new image Fig. fig6 is a correction. The component B as seen at 2.3, 5 GHz (Xiang et al. 2005, 2006) was not detected in this 1.6 GHz observation, assuming an upper limit of 10 mJy set by 3 in the image, it has an inverted spectrum which is a sign of a jet or probably an absorbed core. Based on its double structure and steep spectra of lobes, we classify this GPS source as a CSO.

gmw_00010160.name: PKS 2322 040
gmw_00010160.overview: PKS 2322 040 is an elliptical galaxy that is possibly a member of a small galaxy group [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068]. It is classified as a compact symmetric object candidate and exhibits a double radio structure at 2.3 GHz [arXiv:astro-ph/0501068].

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2322 040 as an elliptical galaxy in a possible group, with double 2.3 GHz structure and a CSO-candidate classification.

quotes.quote: PKS 2322 040 ( NVSS J232510 034446 ) This is a galaxy with m_ R =23.5, which appears to be a regular elliptical and possibly a member of a small group of galaxies (de Vries et al. 2000). Our VLBI images show a double structure at 2.3 GHz (Fig. 2322S ), while only a single component is detected at 8.4 GHz. This component is slightly resolved (Fig. 2322X ). The whole flux density is accounted for in the 2.3 GHz image, while at 8.4 GHz we can account for 166 mJy only, while a flux density of 240 mJy is reported at this frequency in the NED database.

The missing flux density may be in the secondary component 'B' which is lost in our 8.4 GHz image due to poor UV coverage in the North-South direction (there is a negative beam sidelobe at the position of component 'B' in Fig.22 preventing us from collecting enough flux density in that area). The spectral index for the component A is 1.1 (consistent with the spectral index of the whole source =1.16 , as measured from the NED values), indicating it is more likely a lobe rather than the core of the source. The source 2322 040 can be considered a CSO candidate.

gmw_00010161.name: LIRG 1
gmw_00010161.overview: LIRG 1 is a disk galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.73 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It features a well-defined spiral structure, a red core that dominates the K-band light, and several compact optical knots [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 1 is a z=0.73 disk with spiral structure, a red K-band-dominant core, compact optical knots, and little K-band substructure.

quotes.quote: LIRG 1 is a z=0.73 disk galaxy. The \ images show well defined spiral structure. A red core, not seen in the B band image, is prominent at longer wavelengths and dominates the light in the K band. The B image shows several compact knots that also appear in the V,i, and z band images. Aside from the prominent core, the K band image appears to be devoid of substructure.

gmw_00010162.name: LIRG 2
gmw_00010162.overview: LIRG 2 is a luminous, gas-rich major merger at a redshift of approximately 0.6 [arXiv:astro-ph/0504195, arXiv:0801.1135]. The system features a dusty starburst with multiple tight knots [arXiv:astro-ph/0504195], a concentrated central galaxy, and a star-forming linear structure [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: The paper describes XID-56 as a luminous, X-ray-bright gas-rich merger with multiple tight knots, a dusty starburst, and recent or ongoing star formation in blue structures.

quotes.quote: Two are Deep Field South sources, XID-56 and XID-536, with complex morphologies suggestive of recent merger activity. Substructures seen in the NIR AO image (FWHM 0.1 ), including multiple tight knots in XID-56 and a double nucleus in XID-536, are confirmed in the optical \ images. These structures are unresolved in the best seeing-limited ( 0.5 FWHM) NIR images. Stellar population synthesis models of the substructures indicate that XID-56 is a gas rich merger with a recent burst of star formation and significant amounts of dust.

The photometry shows that both galaxies are luminous ( L ^ ) with XID-56 0.2 magnitudes ( B-V ) bluer than XID-536. XID-56 is a 40x stronger x-ray source than its redder counterpart and is classified as a Seyfert 2 galaxy based on its optical spectrum and x-ray emission (Szokoly et al. 2004). Both sources have morphologies suggestive of recent merger activity. Figure fig:MK shows the B,V,i,z ( ) and K' (Keck AO) images for galaxy XID-56.

sources.summary: LIRG 2 is presented as a z=0.6 major merger with a concentrated central galaxy, aligned secondary core, possible AGN, and star-forming linear feature.

quotes.quote: LIRG 2, first studied in Melbourne et al. (2005), is a z=0.6 major merger. The morphology includes a concentrated central galaxy, as well as a prominent linear feature extending to the NE. This linear feature is aligned with a secondary core to the SW of the main galaxy. The secondary core is clearly seen across all wavebands, which may indicate the presence of an Active Galactic Nucleus. The colors of the linear feature are consistent with star formation.

sources.summary: BLAST 830 has two radio counterparts; the chosen identification is an interacting z=0.605 galaxy, while blended emission may also include nearby sources.

quotes.quote: BLAST 830: z_ spec = 0.605 (or z_ spec = 0.735 ) This source does not have a counterpart at 350 m or 500 m , but the 250 m flux density is too faint for this to offer a useful redshift constraint. There are two alternative, statistically-significant radio counterparts, and we select VLA 239 in favour of VLA 1179. The chosen identification appears to be an interacting galaxy with spectroscopic redshift z = 0.605 . The edge-on disc galaxy a few arcsec NE lies at z = 0.735 and so is not physically associated with the adopted identification. However, the 250 m flux density may receive contributions from both these objects, and from VLA 1179.

gmw_00010163.name: LIRG 3
gmw_00010163.overview: LIRG 3 is a luminous infrared galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.02 [arXiv:0801.1135]. Although it appears as a collection of blue star-forming knots, it possesses an underlying stellar disk and a red central core that may contain a small horizontal bar [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 3 appears clumpy in blue light but has an underlying stellar disk, a red K-band core, and possibly a small central bar.

quotes.quote: At first glance, LIRG 3 ( z=1.02 ) appears to be a collection of star forming knots, with little underlying structure. However, the z band image reveals an underlying stellar disk, while the K band, is dominated by a red central core that might contain a small (2.5 kpc diameter) horizontal bar. The blue knots do not appear in the K band image.

gmw_00010164.name: LIRG 4
gmw_00010164.overview: LIRG 4 is a barred spiral luminous infrared galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.10 [arXiv:0801.1135]. The galaxy features a luminous core that may indicate dusty star formation, with spiral arms emerging from a bar that is visible in the redder wavebands [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 4 is a z=1.10 barred spiral LIRG with red-band spiral arms and a luminous core that may indicate dusty star formation.

quotes.quote: This z=1.10 LIRG shows tenuous spiral arms in the V , i , and z band images. This spiral structure does not appear in the lower SNR B and K band images. The spiral arms emerge from a bar that is prominent in the redder wavebands. The core is luminous in both the red and blue bands, possibly an indication of dusty star formation.

gmw_00010165.name: LIRG 5
gmw_00010165.overview: LIRG 5 is a compact luminous infrared galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.08 [arXiv:0801.1135]. The galaxy is elongated in the northeast-southwest direction and features a complex nuclear structure with a central nucleus, several blue knots, and southern diffuse emission [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 5 is a compact z=1.08 source with a complex knotty nucleus, southern diffuse emission, K-band nuclear dominance, and NE-SW elongation.

quotes.quote: LIRG 5, z=1.08 , is a compact source with a complicated nuclear structure composed of a central nucleus and 2-3 blue knots. Diffuse emission appears to the south of the main galaxy, especially in the V band image. The K band image is dominated by the nucleus. The galaxy is elongated in the NE-SW direction in all bands.

gmw_00010166.name: LIRG 6
gmw_00010166.overview: LIRG 6 is a disk galaxy at a redshift of z=0.78 that features strong blue spiral arms [arXiv:0801.1135]. Its core appears underluminous in the B-band, suggesting the presence of dust, while K-band imaging reveals a bar-like linear structure [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 6 is a z=0.78 disk galaxy with blue spiral arms, a K-band bar-like feature, and a dust-suggestive underluminous B-band core.

quotes.quote: LIRG 6 is a z=0.78 disk galaxy with strong blue spiral arms. The K band image shows a bar-like linear feature in the N-S direction. The core appears under-luminous in the B band image suggesting dust.

gmw_00010167.name: LIRG 7
gmw_00010167.overview: LIRG 7 is a spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.01 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It features a nucleus that is highly bright across all wavebands, along with optical knots situated within its spiral arms [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 7 is a z=1.01 spiral galaxy with a bright nucleus, optical knots in its spiral arms, and smoother low-SNR K-band morphology.

quotes.quote: LIRG 7 is a z=1.01 spiral galaxy. The nucleus is very bright across all wavebands. Several knots appear in the optical bands within the spiral arms. The lower SNR K band image does not show knots or spiral arms.

gmw_00010168.name: LIRG 8
gmw_00010168.overview: LIRG 8 is a spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.2 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It features a bright nucleus across all wavebands and several blue knots in its disk, though these knots are not visible in its smooth K-band image [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 8 is a z=1.2 spiral with a bright multi-band nucleus, blue disk knots, and a smooth K-band image lacking those knots.

quotes.quote: LIRG 8 is similar to LIRG 7. It is a 1.2 spiral galaxy with a bright nucleus across all wavebands. It contains several blue knots in the disk. The K band image is smooth and shows no sign of the blue knots.

gmw_00010169.name: LIRG 9
gmw_00010169.overview: LIRG 9 is a luminous infrared galaxy at a redshift of 0.37 [arXiv:0801.1135]. The galaxy is dominated by a luminous core that is a strong X-ray source, suggesting the presence of an active galactic nucleus surrounded by a blue ring [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 9 is dominated by a luminous likely AGN core, has strong X-ray emission, lacks blue star-forming knots, and shows a blue ring.

quotes.quote: LIRG 9, at z=0.37 is dominated by a luminous, possibly unresolved core, indicative of an AGN. This galaxy is a strong x-ray source suggesting that the core is dominated by AGN light. The lack of significant blue star forming knots also suggests that the AGN may be the primary source of the IR luminosity in this system. The core is surrounded by a blue ring. The K band image appears relatively free of sub-structure aside from the core.

gmw_00010170.name: LIRG 10
gmw_00010170.overview: LIRG 10 is a compact spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.002 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It features a red core, a blue southeastern arc that appears to be merging with the system, and potential signatures of a minor merger [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 10 is a compact z=1.002 spiral with a red core, possible minor merger signatures, a blue southeastern arc, and smooth K-band morphology.

quotes.quote: Another compact spiral galaxy with a red core, LIRG 10 has a redshift of z=1.002 . According to the photometric redshifts from COMBO-17, the irregular galaxy to the NE has a redshift of z=0.91 . Therefore it may or may not be associated with the LIRG. Despite this the LIRG may still be undergoing a minor merger.

There is a blue arc to the SE that does not seem associated with the rest of the spiral structures but does look like it is merging with the system. Other than the luminous core, the K band image does not show signs of substructure.

gmw_00010171.name: LIRG 11
gmw_00010171.overview: LIRG 11 is an edge-on spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.95 [arXiv:0801.1135]. The galaxy is nearly invisible in the B-band, but it features a dust lane across its core that is visible in V, i, and z-band imaging [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 11 is an edge-on z=0.95 spiral with a dust lane across the core, K-band core visibility, and very faint B-band emission.

quotes.quote: LIRG 11 is an edge on spiral galaxy at z=0.95 . The V , i , and z band images show a dust lane across the core. The K band image of the core does not appear to be affected by the dust lane. The galaxy is almost invisible in the B band image.

gmw_00010172.name: LIRG 12
gmw_00010172.overview: LIRG 12 is a compact, centrally concentrated galaxy at a redshift of z=0.55 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It features an extended low-surface-brightness disk, possible K-band substructure, and an active galactic nucleus that may drive its infrared luminosity [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 12 is compact and centrally concentrated but may have an extended low-surface-brightness disk, K-band substructure, X-ray emission, and central AGN-dominated IR luminosity.

quotes.quote: At first glance this galaxy ( z=0.55 ) looks like a very compact, centrally concentrated galaxy across all five bands. However, at fainter flux levels there appears to be a very extended low surface brightness disk component in the N-S direction. This is the only LIRG in our sample that might contain K band substructure not prominent in the other bands. A small red knot appears just above the central core in the K band image.
This galaxy is also an x-ray source. The central AGN may be the main source of IR luminosity in this system as it also appears to be free of prominent blue star forming regions.

gmw_00010173.name: LIRG 13
gmw_00010173.overview: LIRG 13 is a disk galaxy at a redshift of z=0.89 that features a red core surrounded by bright blue knots [arXiv:0801.1135]. The galaxy may be undergoing a minor merger, as evidenced by a warped linear K-band feature and a blue companion to the northwest [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 13 is a z=0.89 disk galaxy with a red core, bright blue knots, warped K-band linear feature, and possible minor merger companion.

quotes.quote: This z=0.89 , disk galaxy has a very red core surrounded by a series of bright blue knots. The K band image of shows a warped linear feature running from the SE to the NW, along the semi major axis of the galaxy. This galaxy may be experiencing a minor merger. There is a very blue companion to the NW of the galaxy.

gmw_00010174.name: LIRG 14
gmw_00010174.overview: LIRG 14 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=0.84 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It appears as an unresolved point source in five imaging bands, with no detected evidence of an underlying host galaxy [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 14 is identified as a z=0.84 QSO, appearing unresolved in all five bands with no imaged underlying galaxy evidence.

quotes.quote: LIRG 14 is a z=0.84 QSO and therefore is different from the other galaxies in the sample. It appears as an unresolved point source in all 5 bands. There is no evidence in these images for an underlying galaxy.

gmw_00010175.name: LIRG 15
gmw_00010175.overview: LIRG 15 is a luminous infrared galaxy at a redshift of z=0.65 [arXiv:0801.1135]. Its morphology is dominated by a ring of luminous blue knots, an off-center red core, and a southern blue tail that indicates recent merger activity [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: LIRG 15 is a z=0.65 galaxy dominated by a ring of blue knots, an off-center red core, and a blue tail suggesting recent merger activity.

quotes.quote: The morphology of LIRG 15 ( z=0.65 ) is dominated by a ring of luminous blue knots. The primary core is red but is off-center from the rest of the galaxy. To the NW of the red core is a bluer knot. Finally a blue tail trails off in the southward direction at the SW corner of the galaxy indicating recent merger activity.

gmw_00010176.name: COMPGAL 1
gmw_00010176.overview: COMPGAL 1 is an inclined disk galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.42 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It features a very blue western arc with a luminous blue knot at its southern tip, a red southeastern knot, and a very red core that is the sole structure visible in K-band imaging [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 1 is an inclined z=0.42 disk with a blue western arc, luminous blue knot, red southeastern knot, and red K-band core.

quotes.quote: COMPGAL 1 is an inclined disk galaxy at z=0.42 . It contains a very blue arc to the W of the nucleus. At the southern tip of the blue arc is a very blue, luminous knot. A red knot of similar size is located to the SE of the core. The core of the galaxy is very red, and is the only structure seen in the K band image.

gmw_00010177.name: COMPGAL 2
gmw_00010177.overview: COMPGAL 2 is a compact disk galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.67 [arXiv:0801.1135]. The galaxy features blue northern and southern arcs with associated blue knots, as well as a redder core that lacks a significant bulge population [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 2 is a compact z=0.67 disk galaxy with blue arcs and knots, plus a redder core but weak K-band nucleation.

quotes.quote: At z=0.67 , COMPGAL 2 is a compact disk galaxy with blue southern and northern arcs. It also contains two blue knots, one associated with each arc. The core of the galaxy is redder than the arcs, but the K band image is not as nucleated as the other bands, implying the lack of a significant bulge population.

gmw_00010178.name: COMPGAL 3
gmw_00010178.overview: COMPGAL 3 is an irregular galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.44 [arXiv:0801.1135]. Its central region is dominated by a linear feature running northeast to southwest, and a set of bright blue knots is visible to the northeast of its core [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 3 is a z=0.44 irregular with a NE-SW central linear feature and bright blue knots northeast of its core.

quotes.quote: COMPGAL 3 is a z=0.44 irregular galaxy. Its central region is dominated by a linear feature that runs NE to SW. This feature is visible in all bands although is very faint in the K band image. A set of bright blue knots is seen to the NE of the core.

gmw_00010179.name: COMPGAL 4
gmw_00010179.overview: COMPGAL 4 is an edge-on disk galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.50 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It features a red bulge that is visible in the K-band encircled by blue arcs, and its disk may have a warp pointing toward a faint northern neighbor [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 4 is an edge-on z=0.50 disk with a red K-band bulge, blue arcs, a possible warped disk, and uncertain northern neighbor.

quotes.quote: COMPGAL 4 ( z=0.50 ) is an edge on disk galaxy with a well defined red bulge that is prominent in the K band image. The bulge is encircled by blue arcs in the other bands. The disk may have a warp at large radii to the NE. The upturn in the disk is pointed towards a faint blue galaxy to the N. It is not clear if this neighboring galaxy is a companion or just a superposition.

gmw_00010180.name: COMPGAL 5
gmw_00010180.overview: COMPGAL 5 is a compact disk galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.73 [arXiv:0801.1135]. The galaxy contains a red bulge and features a blue arc located to the northwest of its core [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 5 is a compact z=0.73 disk galaxy with a prominent red bulge and a blue arc northwest of its core.

quotes.quote: COMPGAL 5 is a compact disk galaxy at z=0.73 . It contains a prominent red bulge. A blue arc is located to the NW of the galaxy core.

gmw_00010181.name: COMPGAL 6
gmw_00010181.overview: COMPGAL 6 is an inclined disk or irregular galaxy at a redshift of z=0.42 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It features a diffuse, red core surrounded by blue knots, as well as a peculiar blue feature in its southeastern corner [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 6 is a z=0.42 inclined disk or irregular galaxy with a red diffuse K-band core, surrounding blue knots, and a peculiar southeastern blue feature.

quotes.quote: COMPGAL 6 ( z=0.42 ) appears to be either an inclined disk galaxy, or possibly an irregular galaxy. It contains a fairly red core, which is diffuse in the K band. The core is surrounded by a series of blue knots. The south east corner of this galaxy shows a peculiar blue feature.

gmw_00010182.name: COMPGAL 7
gmw_00010182.overview: COMPGAL 7 is a galaxy at a redshift of z=0.77 that appears to be a small, edge-on spiral with a red core [arXiv:0801.1135]. Because it is the reddest galaxy in its sample, it may alternatively be a dwarf elliptical with a faint blue companion to the northwest [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 7 is a z=0.77 small edge-on spiral candidate with a prominent red core, possible dwarf-elliptical nature, and potential blue companion.

quotes.quote: At first glance COMPGAL 7 ( z=0.77 ) appears to be another small edge on spiral with a prominent red core. However, it is the reddest galaxy in our sample, so it might be a dwarf elliptical. A faint blue galaxy lies to the NW and may be a companion.

gmw_00010183.name: COMPGAL 8
gmw_00010183.overview: COMPGAL 8 is a disk galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.77 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It features two spiral arms to the north, a blue knot on its western arm, a southern linear blue feature, and a very blue core that is faint in the K band [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 8 is a z=0.77 disk with northern spiral arms, a southern blue feature, a very blue faint K-band core, and a western-arm knot.

quotes.quote: COMPGAL 8 is a disk galaxy at z=0.77 . It has two prominent spiral arms to the N and another linear blue feature to the S. The core of this galaxy is very blue. The core is the only feature that appears in the K band image, but even the core is faint in K . There is a blue knot on the western spiral arm.

gmw_00010184.name: COMPGAL 9
gmw_00010184.overview: COMPGAL 9 is a highly luminous, very blue irregular galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.70 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It features a red core offset to the southeast, along with several blue knots and wisps situated to the north [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 9 is a luminous blue z=0.70 irregular galaxy with an offset red core and northern blue knots and wisps.

quotes.quote: COMPGAL 9 is an irregular galaxy at z=0.70 . It has a red core that is offset to the SE from much of the rest of the galaxy. The galaxy also contains several blue knots and blue wisps that lie to the N of the galaxy core. This galaxy is very luminous M_B=-20.88 , but unlike the LIRGs, it is a very blue system B-V=0.235 .

gmw_00010185.name: COMPGAL 10
gmw_00010185.overview: COMPGAL 10 is a galaxy at a redshift of z=0.46 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It exhibits a blue irregular morphology in bluer bands, but appears as a red dwarf spiral or elliptical galaxy with a red central bulge in the z and K bands, which may indicate a recent minor merger [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 10 is a z=0.46 object whose blue irregular and red dwarf-spiral/elliptical morphologies may signal a recent minor merger.

quotes.quote: When viewed in the bluer bands, COMPGAL 10 ( z=0.46 ) looks like another irregular system. In the z and K band images it loots like a dwarf spiral or elliptical with a red central bulge. The differences between the blue and red morphologies may be indicating a recent minor merger between a gas rich cloud and a dwarf elliptical or spiral galaxy.

gmw_00010186.name: COMPGAL 11
gmw_00010186.overview: COMPGAL 11 is a galaxy at a redshift of z=0.61 [arXiv:0801.1135]. It exhibits an irregular morphology in blue bands, a ring-like structure in its bluest band, and a disk-like appearance with a red core in redder bands [arXiv:0801.1135].

sources.summary: COMPGAL 11 is a z=0.61 galaxy appearing irregular in blue bands and disk-like in redder bands, with a red core, blue arc, and ring-like blue morphology.

quotes.quote: COMPGAL 11 ( z=0.61 ) looks like a irregular galaxy in the blue bands and like a small disk galaxy in the redder bands. It has a red core. A blue arc runs through the core from the S to N, and may be a spiral structure. In the bluest band, the galaxy looks like a ring.

gmw_00010187.name: UGC5692
gmw_00010187.overview: UGC5692 is a dwarf galaxy that has undergone a recent increase in its star formation rate [arXiv:1203.4826]. The galaxy's brightest star cluster is young, with an estimated age of less than 10 million years [arXiv:1203.4826].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC5692 as a high outlier in cluster-formation relations, partly explained by recent increased star formation and its youngest bright cluster.

quotes.quote: There is also one high outlier galaxy (UGC5692) in the < 100 Myr age range that lies significantly above the trend line. This galaxy is also a high outlier in the _ SFR relationship. However, showed that outliers above the trend were in fact not outliers after using the youngest ( t_ age 10 Myr) clusters to show that the brightest cluster is more likely to be young and therefore more accurately reflects the recent SFR. This is true for UGC5692, whose brightest cluster has an age less than 10 Myr. UGC5692 has undergone a recent increase in SFR; using the < 10 Myr data point (log SFR (M _ yr ^ 1 ) 3 and the same magnitude) brings this galaxy within the scatter of the relationship.

gmw_00010188.name: 3C356
gmw_00010188.overview: 3C356 is a radio galaxy at a redshift of z=1.079 [arXiv:1010.1385, arXiv:1605.03196] that is associated with a poor cluster of galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0203446]. The system features two main bright elliptical galaxies [arXiv:1605.03196] that both appear to harbor active galactic nuclei [arXiv:astro-ph/0203446] and show disturbed morphologies indicative of a collision [arXiv:astro-ph/0203446].

sources.summary: The paper models 3C356 as a radio galaxy whose SED differs from quasars and is fit by pure AGN models, with a modest combined-model starburst component.
